nodes.title: What Is Evolution? Course Intro
nodes.subtitle: Why evolution unifies biology + practical applications (Lec 1 slides 1-9)

popup.heading: Lecture 1 — What Is Evolution?

sections.label: Slide 1: Evolution
sections.body:
Evolution

BIOL 4230/8236
Dr. Travis Robbins

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture12.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.bio.georgiasouthern.edu/bio-home/harvey/lect/images/domain1.gif)

sections.label: Slide 2: Course objectives
sections.body:
Course objectives
1. To demonstrate the importance and relevance of evolutionary theory to all disciplines in the biological sciences.

2. To understand evolutionary biology as a rigorous scientific field and to describe what evolutionary theory does and does not tell us about the diversity of life on Earth.

3. To bring together the prevailing ideas on evolutionary theory to help you create an informed framework for thought about evolutionary issues in all fields of biology.

SPEAKER NOTES: Up to now, much of what you have learned are facts (with some exceptions)

sections.label: Slide 3: What is evolution?
sections.body:
What is evolution?
What isn’t evolution?
Why is it important?
Biological Evolution:
Any change in the inherited traits of a population that occurs from one generation to the next
Occurs across generations NOT within the lifetime of an individual
Evolution provides an explanation for the diversity of life, but does NOT address the origin of life

sections.label: Slide 4: Evolution is one of the most successful scientific theories
sections.body:
Evolution is one of the most successful scientific theories
Scientific theory:
Set of principles that explain a major aspect of the natural world
Supported by independent lines of evidence
Well-confirmed explanation of nature
Most reliable, rigorous, and comprehensive form of scientific knowledge
NOT just a hunch
Other scientific theories?
 Gravitation – attraction of objects proportional to their mass
 Plate tectonics – motions of Earth’s lithosphere
 Aerodynamics – flow of air around objects

SPEAKER NOTES: Theories are used to make testable predictions, and they are formulated from hypotheses that have been corroborated through the scientific method.

Gravitation – phenomenon that objects attract each other with a force that is proportional to their mass
Plate tectonics – describes large-scale motions of Earth’s lithosphere (Earth’s crust)
By understanding theoretical principles of how air flows around objects and its consequences, we can make predictions.

Every time we fly in an airplane, we see are testing aerodynamic principles.  We can make predictions about lift, and we see the results.  In evolution, we can make predictions, collected data, and see the results

sections.label: Slide 5: Evolution Unifies all of Biology
sections.body:
Evolution Unifies all of Biology
The theory of evolution is the conceptual foundation that underlies all areas of modern biology

“Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution”
Dozhansky, T., 1973. Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution. American Biology Teacher, 35, pp.125-129.
Theodosius Dobzhansky

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture10.jpg" (originally from URL: http://whyevolutionistrue.files.wordpress.com/2011/01/dobzhansky.jpeg)

sections.label: Slide 6: “Seen in the light of evolution, biology is, perhaps, intellectually the most satisfying and inspirin
sections.body:
“Seen in the light of evolution, biology is, perhaps, intellectually the most satisfying and inspiring science. Without that light it truly becomes a pile of sundry facts – some of them interesting or curious but making no meaningful picture as a whole”
- Theodosius Dobzhansky 1973
Evolutionary biology integrates many fields

sections.label: Slide 8: Evolution helps answer big questions
sections.body:
Evolution helps answer big questions
How did the remarkable diversity of life come to be?

How might changes to the environment affect species?

How did we get here? …”light will be thrown on the origin of man and his history”
					-Darwin 1859
Need to understand evolution to understand why the world is the way that it is
			- provides a natural explanation

SPEAKER NOTES: Take a couple minutes to talk about the perceived controversy or debate between evolution and religion.  There is a common perception that to accept evolution you don’t believe in god, or if you believe in god you reject evolution.  I want to make it clear that these two things are not mutially exclusive and comparing the two is like comparing apples to oranges. Evolutionary biology cannot inform one’s religious beliefs, and religion has nothing to say about evolutionary biology.  Science and religion take two fundamentally different approaches to understanding the world we live in.

There is no doubt that religious beliefs are important and provide meaning to individuals, and it isn’t my intention to take this away by teaching evolution. Disproving the existence of god is not a goal of evolutionary biology. Evolutionary biology seeks to provide natural explanations for the diversity of life on Earth.

Science is based on natural explanation – things we can physically measure and analyze.
Religious explanations are based on faith – you don’t need physical evidence to have faith. Supernatural explanations cannot be tested by science

In this class, we are going to treat evolution as the science that it is, and we will rarely be talking about the conflicts between evolution and religious beliefs that often get stirred up.  That’s for another class.

I will note, however, that evolution does conflict with the specific teachings of creationism.

sections.label: Slide 9: Practical applications
sections.body:
Practical applications

- Human epidemics
Black Death (1300’s) – killed 30-50% of the population (~25 million)

New World smallpox (1500’s) – Decimated Native American populations

Influenza epidemic (1918) – killed 50 million people

Ebola (2013 to ??) – over 8000 have died

COVID-19 (2020 to 2024) – over 7 million people
A single vaccine might last a lifetime for some viruses
			…but not for the flu virus

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture7.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-5.dropbox.com/t/0/AAAP3R2pm2yy1APssa6-HdL6WzKpK38L16yfokPu2f7PLA/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1357153200/0/2/01.15_fmt1.jpeg/l4dLK_FPfju4nGqVmLQGY8BCyvtONZQ8zjNf8qXW-44?size=1280x960)

examples: Dobzhansky: "Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution"
Practical applications include antibiotic resistance, vaccine design, crop improvement, conservation.
popup.mnemonic: CHANGE: Change in allele frequencies over generations — the minimal definition of evolution.

flashcard.front: Why is evolution considered the unifying theory of biology?
flashcard.back: Every subfield of biology — from cell biology to ecology — rests on the insight that organisms share common ancestry and have been modified over time by natural selection, drift, mutation, and gene flow. Without evolution, biological diversity and shared traits across species have no causal explanation (Dobzhansky 1973).

quiz.question: Which of the following is the most precise scientific definition of evolution?
quiz.correct: A change in the frequency of alleles in a population across generations
distractors: Organisms becoming more complex over time
The appearance of new species through sudden mutation
Improvement of individuals during their lifetime

visual.description: Evolution as the unifying hub of biology

regions.label: Core claim
items: Change in allele frequencies over generations

visual.mnemonic: Allele frequency change = evolution. Everything else is a consequence.
visual.trap: Common misconception: evolution means "progress" or "improvement." Evolution is directional only in the sense of local fitness — there is no global ladder.

v2.definition: Evolution is descent with modification, the heritable change in populations across generations producing biological diversity from common ancestors.

mnemonic.explanation: Inherit then change, two ingredients build all biodiversity from one ancestor over time.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Individuals evolve.' Only POPULATIONS evolve. A single organism cannot change its allele frequencies during its lifetime.

actions.quizPrompt: Why do biologists say populations, not individuals, evolve?
actions.flashcardBack: Descent with modification, heritable change in populations across generations.

nodes.title: Flu Virus: Evolution in Action
nodes.subtitle: H1N1 rapid evolution — a real-time case study (Lec 1 slides 10-21)

popup.heading: Lecture 1 — Influenza as Evolution Case Study

sections.label: Slide 10: Microscopic shell made of protein, that encases 13 genes on 8 RNA segments
sections.body:
Microscopic shell made of protein, that encases 13 genes on 8 RNA segments
Influenza virus

Travels into our respiratory tract and makes us sick

Hemagglutinin – a protein that binds the virus to the host cell that it is infecting

Neuraminidase – proteins that enable virus to detach from host cell

SPEAKER NOTES: Flu Virus – microscopic shell made of protein, that encases 13 genes on 8 RNA segments

Travel into our respiratory tract and make us sick

Hemagglutinin is a protein that binds the virus to the host cell that it is infecting.

Neuraminidase – proteins that enable virus to detach from host cell

sections.label: Slide 11: Influenza virus entering host cell
sections.body:
Influenza virus entering host cell

Travels into our respiratory tract and makes us sick

Hemagglutinin – a protein that binds the virus to the host cell that it is infecting

Neuraminidase – proteins that enable virus to detach from host cell

SPEAKER NOTES: Virus genes enter nucleus of cell. The cell makes new copies of the flu genes, and the proteins in which they are encased. Virus is assembled at the cell membrane, and then bud off to infect other cells

Each infected cell can make thousands of copies of viral genomes

sections.body:
Mistakes happen

Mutations help create a diversity of new strains

Rapid spread via natural selection

SPEAKER NOTES: Some mutations and neutral, others might make the virus unable to invade host cell, others may make this process more efficient or enable virus to replicate faster

Mutations that affect Hemagglutinin proteins are important because vaccines are made up of this protein. Vaccines will trigger the body to make antibodies that attack these proteins.

sections.label: Slide 17: Viral evolution by natural selection
sections.body:
Viral evolution by natural selection
Hemagglutinin inhibition (HAI) – tests how well the virus escapes antibodies used in the vaccine

Higher titer = more antibodies attach to virus
Effective vaccine against
non-mutated virus
Mutations in viral proteins reduced the effectiveness of the vaccine, favored by natural selection and increased

sections.label: Slide 18: All strains of flu virus descend from strains that affect other animals (zoonotic)
sections.body:
All strains of flu virus descend from strains that affect other animals (zoonotic)

Reassortment – Genetic material from different strains gets mixed into new combinations
Bird flu occasionally becomes adapted to replicating in humans
Reassorted strains can be devastating

SPEAKER NOTES: Aided by the ability of viruses to swap genes

Two virsus infect one cell at the same time, genes can get shuffled around, and then get packaged into a new virus. Can give rise to proteins that can invade human cells. Can spread easily from person to person.  Immune system is not adapted to these new strains

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-1.dropbox.com/t/0/AAAaLcL6tVp1f2QbBXuHF_ce056XB1hEgZW0EJ_6ZneqTw/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1357243200/0/2/01.21_fmt.jpeg/2hS67V5QrV9Osk_xwwOrtAet7k3t4xg0cPxAijztHXg?size=1280x960)

sections.label: Slide 19: H1N1 flu strain in 2009
sections.body:
H1N1 flu strain in 2009
Evolutionary biologists became involved – international collaboration
Uploaded genetic sequences of the new strain from patients all over the world
Reconstructed evolutionary relationships of the viruses (phylogeny)

sections.label: Slide 20: Compared samples collected from around the world
sections.body:
Compared samples collected from around the world
Focused on similarities of genes to determine relatedness
Estimated when the common ancestor lived
H1N1 probably spread around the world before it was discovered

SPEAKER NOTES: Used a molecular clock to give a clue of when different strains diverged from a common ancestor.

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture5.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-4.dropbox.com/t/0/AAAB_i5vVjWSVIHDcKhyYR0hfW7HLO9iEmp9D_Wex8ObmQ/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1357153200/0/2/01.22_fmt.jpeg/IGOOtwpZ8G7unhfF1SzsWzG1I0NtRD8lSAQtekeRHC8?size=1280x960)
Image 2: File "Picture5.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-4.dropbox.com/t/0/AAAB_i5vVjWSVIHDcKhyYR0hfW7HLO9iEmp9D_Wex8ObmQ/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1357153200/0/2/01.22_fmt.jpeg/IGOOtwpZ8G7unhfF1SzsWzG1I0NtRD8lSAQtekeRHC8?size=1280x960)

sections.label: Slide 21: A deeper look at the evolutionary history of H1N1
sections.body:
A deeper look at the evolutionary history of H1N1
H1N1 is a mix of genes from viruses that originally infected pigs and birds
Triple reassortment of bird, pig and human flu
H1N1 was not that bad
Killed 280,000 people
Killed 16,000 Americans

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture6.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-2.dropbox.com/t/0/AADwuScPIfKpoFn6jE_nbXdkd1iplvKR4skkYswDYoLKxg/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1357153200/0/2/01.23_fmt.jpeg/wr7JhESe_KHrVCXIfwE6h-LKvs9_e0VuczuSzVCdUqc?size=1280x960)

examples: H1N1 2009 pandemic: traced back to swine-origin reassortment; tracked in real time via global genomic sampling.
Hemagglutinin and neuraminidase surface proteins accumulate amino acid substitutions — driving antigenic drift.
warnings: Not all mutations are selected for — most are neutral or deleterious. Only rare beneficial substitutions are fixed.
popup.mnemonic: HA-NA: HemAgglutinin and NeurAminidase — the flu's two most-evolving proteins, and the targets of vaccines.

flashcard.front: How does influenza virus illustrate rapid evolution by natural selection?
flashcard.back: Influenza has an 8-segment RNA genome and a high error-rate RNA polymerase (no proofreading). Errors create variants constantly. Host immunity selects for variants whose surface proteins (HA, NA) evade recognition, so amino acid substitutions accumulate on the surface. Comparing samples worldwide showed that the 2009 H1N1 pandemic strain descended from reassortment between human, avian, and swine flu lineages — tracked in real time by phylogenetic analysis.

quiz.question: Which feature of influenza most directly explains why new vaccine formulations are needed each year?
quiz.correct: High mutation rate in surface proteins HA and NA drives antigenic drift
distractors: The virus physically grows larger each year
Humans gradually lose immunity to all pathogens over time
New animal hosts invent new flu types annually

visual.description: Flu evolution loop: error-prone replication → surface mutation → selection by host immunity
visual.mnemonic: Error → Drift → Escape → Spread.
visual.trap: Antigenic DRIFT (small mutations, gradual) ≠ antigenic SHIFT (reassortment of whole RNA segments, sudden pandemic strains).

v2.definition: Influenza H1N1 evolves rapidly through mutation, antigenic drift, and antigenic shift, requiring annual vaccine updates worldwide.

mnemonic.explanation: Drift accumulates point mutations. Shift recombines whole RNA segments between strains creating pandemic risk.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Drift and shift mean the same thing.' Drift is small mutations, shift is whole-segment reassortment causing pandemics.

actions.quizPrompt: Distinguish antigenic drift from antigenic shift in influenza.
actions.flashcardFront: Why do we need a flu shot every year?
actions.flashcardBack: Antigenic drift continually mutates surface proteins, evading prior immunity.

sections.label: Slide 23: Take-home points of this lecture
sections.body:
Take-home points of this lecture
Evolution is a scientific theory with a huge body of evidence to support it

Evolution is a predictive science and is accepted as fact

Understanding evolutionary principles is important in all disciplines of biology

And important to human health

popup.mnemonic: Read → Attend → Apply: passive reading isn't enough for BIOL 4230.

flashcard.front: What are the three main reasons the instructor gives for why understanding evolutionary principles is important?
flashcard.back: (1) It explains patterns we observe in all living organisms; (2) It has direct practical applications (medicine, agriculture, conservation); (3) It provides the only coherent framework for asking "why" questions in biology.

quiz.question: According to Lecture 1, evolutionary biology is best described as:
quiz.correct: A predictive, testable framework that unifies all subfields of biology
distractors: A set of historical narratives about extinct species
A philosophy restricted to explaining past events only
A belief system with no empirical tests

visual.mnemonic: MEA: Mechanisms → Evidence → Applications.

v2.definition: Evolution unifies all biology and provides practical tools for medicine, agriculture, and conservation through testable hypotheses.

mnemonic.explanation: Like a computer operating system, evolution is the framework every other biological process runs on.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Evolution is just a theory.' In science, theory means well tested explanation supported by overwhelming evidence.

actions.quizPrompt: Name three real-world applications where evolutionary theory is essential.
actions.flashcardBack: It unifies biology and powers medicine, agriculture, and conservation.

popup.heading: Lecture 2 — Evolutionary Thinking Before Darwin

sections.label: Slide 1: Evolutionary thinking before and after Darwin
sections.body:
Evolutionary thinking before and after Darwin

Evolution
BIOL 4230
University of Nebraska Omaha
Dr. Travis Robbins

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (alt text: "Charles Darwin - Theory, Book & Quotes")

sections.label: Slide 2: Evolutionary thinking before and after Darwin
sections.body:
Evolutionary thinking before and after Darwin
Lecture goals

To appreciate the history of evolutionary thinking and the major players around Darwin’s time

To understand the observations that lead to the concept of natural selection

To understand the logic of and evidence for natural selection and its role in evolutionary change

sections.label: Slide 3: Early naturalists focused on naming and classifying organisms
sections.body:
Early naturalists focused on naming and classifying organisms
Ancient Greek philosophers (400 BC) studied patterns of organismal diversity
The Great Chain of Being
Lower to higher forms
Primary school of thought up to mid 1800’s – species were created simultaneously and were fixed and immutable
Leading naturalists believed that Earth was only thousands of years old
Evolution – referred to developmental change from embryo to free-living adult form (i.e. Pokemon)

sections.label: Slide 4: Special Creation
sections.body:
Special Creation
Species 1
Species 2
Species 3
All species created separately and simultaneously by God

Each species was a discrete entity with no geographic variation

Once created it didn’t go extinct!

Creation event was recent: Archbishop James Usher (1664) – creation was on October 23rd, 4004 BC

Time
at 9am!

Species form

SPEAKER NOTES: Species did not change or give rise to another
All species were created recently

Usher looked at old testament geneologies

Published as a marginal note in an English bible, and this was accepted for many centuries after

sections.label: Slide 5: Naturalists were aware that different species shared characteristics
sections.body:
Naturalists were aware that different species shared characteristics
Carl Linnaeus (Carolus Linnaeus) – father of taxonomy

1707-1778
Believed
this taxonomy reflected the “Divine Plan” or “divine blueprints”
species later changed through interbreeding, and producing a hybrid species

SPEAKER NOTES: Believed species later changed through interbreeding, and producing a hybrid species

sections.body:
The idea of change over time was debated

 Organic particles transformed a seed or egg into adult form

 Organic particles changed as the species moved to a new habitat
Georges Buffon (1707-1788)
Director of the King’s Garden, Paris

 Earth changes little by little over time
Rain erodes mountains
Molten rock pushes up new mountains

 Given evidence for vast changes, the Earth must be very old
James Hutton (1726-1797)
Scottish chemist, amateur geologist

SPEAKER NOTES: Buffon – French Nobleman
Influenced by Physics and chemistry – small particles made up the world (atoms & molecules)

Hutton – Scottish farmer
Rocks change: rain erodes mountains, molten rock pushes up to make new ones

The idea of change over time was debated

 Discovered fossils of species that no longer exist

 Documented extinction in many species

French paleontologist

 Noticed changes in fossil species composition with age of rocks

 Organized geological history from oldest to youngest
William Smith (1769-1839)

sections.label: Slide 9: Change was evident, but by what mechanism?
sections.body:
Change was evident, but by what mechanism?
Life moved from simplicity to complexity (ladder of progress, and no extinction)

Primitive life (e.g., microbes) is spontaneously generated all the time

Argued that plants and animals can adapt to their environment
e.g., an organ can change through use and be passed on to decendents

Jean-Baptiste Lamark (1744-1829)
Naturalist, Expert on plants and invertebrates

SPEAKER NOTES: Did not accept extinction,
Fossil species evolved into living forms

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture8.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-1.dropbox.com/t/0/AADnzig7m7RFz21eeWmlxHGudtWNO1x3_nnzhrhGjSoYPA/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1357160400/0/2/02.10_fmt.jpeg/xNqLJNHykdZp0NIK4To2lAKi7N-RmzumoVxgi_D1PTM?size=1280x960)

quotes: "Special creation" = the pre-Darwinian view that each species was divinely created in its present form.
examples: Linnaeus (1707-1778): classified organisms into hierarchical groups — kingdom, class, order, genus, species.
Cuvier: documented extinction using fossils, proposed catastrophism.
Lamarck: first formal theory of evolution — inheritance of acquired characteristics (incorrect mechanism, but correctly recognized change).
Lyell: uniformitarianism — geological processes are gradual and ongoing (influenced Darwin).
popup.mnemonic: LCLLL: Linnaeus (classification) → Cuvier (extinction) → Lamarck (first mechanism) → Lyell (deep time) → Darwin.

flashcard.front: What was Lamarck's theory of evolution and why was its mechanism ultimately rejected?
flashcard.back: Lamarck proposed that organisms change during their lifetime in response to use/disuse, and that these acquired traits are passed to offspring (e.g., giraffe necks stretching). Modern genetics shows that changes to somatic tissues are not inherited — only changes to germ line DNA pass to offspring. Lamarck was correct that evolution happens and that it is an ongoing process, but the mechanism (inheritance of acquired characteristics) is false for most traits. Lamarck deserves credit for being the first to propose a mechanistic theory.

quiz.question: Which of these pre-Darwin thinkers FIRST articulated a formal mechanism for evolutionary change, even though that mechanism was wrong?
quiz.correct: Jean-Baptiste Lamarck
distractors: Carl Linnaeus
Georges Cuvier
Charles Lyell

visual.mnemonic: Linnaeus named, Cuvier buried, Lamarck mechanized, Lyell stretched time.
visual.trap: Do NOT confuse Lamarck's (wrong) inheritance of acquired characteristics with modern epigenetic inheritance — the latter is real but rare and does not explain most adaptation.

v2.definition: Pre-Darwinian thinkers Linnaeus, Cuvier, and Lamarck laid groundwork by classifying species and proposing change without selection.

mnemonic.explanation: Linnaeus named life, Cuvier proved extinction, Lamarck guessed how, then Darwin synthesized them all.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Lamarck believed in natural selection.' Lamarck believed in inheritance of acquired characteristics, an incorrect mechanism.

actions.quizPrompt: What did Linnaeus, Cuvier, and Lamarck each contribute to evolutionary thought?
actions.flashcardBack: He believed traits acquired during life were inherited (wrong mechanism).

nodes.title: Darwin's Voyage of the Beagle
nodes.subtitle: The observational foundation of evolutionary theory (Lec 2 slides 10-12)

popup.heading: Lecture 2 — Darwin's Voyage

sections.label: Slide 10: Charles Darwin
sections.body:
Charles Darwin

 Grew up in a wealthy family

 Went to study Medicine at Edinburgh

 Studied theology at Cambridge

 Joined Captian Fitzroy as a naturalist on the H.M.S. Beagle (1831)
Charles Darwin (1809-1882)

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture6.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-6.dropbox.com/t/0/AAAikuhjAoGyrD5NPCIMunhGvExtpz2H--wFoPIEJFP5yg/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1357160400/0/2/02.01_fmt1.jpeg/goAfx5kX5UjKUGWaaonDXXYIoQPqcOiHdvsM_GUbNKc?size=1280x960)

sections.label: Slide 11: Voyage of the Beagle from 1831 To 1836
sections.body:
Voyage of the Beagle from 1831 To 1836

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture11.jpg"
Image 2: File "Picture10.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 12: During the voyage
sections.body:
During the voyage
Collected fossils, plants, trapped animals

Studied the geology of South America

Charles Lyell – The Principles of Geology (3 vol, 1830-1833)
Uniformitarianism: Natural laws that we observe around us now are also responsible for events in the past
The Earth was shaped by the cumulative action of gradual processes (sediment deposition & erosion)

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture12.jpg"
Image 2: File "Picture13.jpg"

examples: 1831-1836: HMS Beagle circumnavigation. Darwin is 22 at departure.
Galápagos finches: slight morphological differences between islands — a key observation that later fueled natural selection theory.
Darwin waited over 20 years before publishing On the Origin of Species (1859), partly due to fear of controversy.
popup.mnemonic: Beagle→Book: 5 years voyage (1831-36) + 23 years thinking = Origin (1859).

flashcard.front: Why did Darwin wait so long (more than 20 years) to publish his theory of evolution by natural selection?
flashcard.back: Darwin was a meticulous scientist who wanted his argument to be airtight. He spent the decades after the Beagle voyage accumulating evidence from barnacles, pigeon breeding, plant hybridization, biogeography, and correspondence with other naturalists. He also knew the theory would be religiously and socially controversial. He was finally prompted to publish in 1858 when Alfred Russel Wallace independently arrived at the same theory and sent Darwin a manuscript — both read joint papers to the Linnean Society in 1858, and Origin followed in 1859.

quiz.question: Darwin's Voyage of the Beagle (1831-1836) contributed most directly to his theory because:
quiz.correct: He observed variation between isolated populations that no single region alone would have revealed
distractors: He isolated DNA from island species
He performed laboratory breeding experiments on finches
He measured gene frequencies across populations

visual.mnemonic: FIVA: Finches, Island biogeography, Volcanoes, Andes.
visual.trap: Darwin did not "see evolution in action" on the Beagle — he built the theory later from the totality of his observations.

v2.definition: Darwin's five-year Beagle voyage gathered fossil and biogeographic evidence that inspired his natural selection theory.

mnemonic.explanation: Darwin spent half a decade observing patterns then twenty more years building the explanation.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Darwin published immediately after the voyage.' He waited 23 years until Wallace's letter forced publication in 1859.

actions.quizPrompt: What evidence from the Beagle voyage shaped Darwin's thinking?
actions.flashcardFront: When did Origin of Species publish?
actions.flashcardBack: 1859, twenty-three years after Darwin returned from the Beagle voyage.

nodes.title: Natural Selection: The Four Ingredients
nodes.subtitle: Variation, heritability, differential reproduction → evolution (Lec 2 slides 13-23)

popup.heading: Lecture 2 — The Logic of Natural Selection

sections.label: Slide 13: Darwin’s Theory of Evolution by Natural Selection
sections.body:
Darwin’s Theory of Evolution by Natural Selection

Influenced largely by Lyell’s Uniformitarianism, and observations from his voyage
Darwin thought he collected very different species
Blackbirds, wrens, finches
John Gould, a London ornithologist, found they were all finches

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture8.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-5.dropbox.com/t/0/AADkB65LKKtWTq-048BKdm4ag0mNSdUIHESKrSE1tyKZvw/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1357160400/0/2/02.13_fmt.jpeg/KqKhM4ioJxEmhfjdaV41CZ8cj0iFv-lvyGs8iNC5qkM?size=1280x960)

sections.label: Slide 14: Major ingredients of Natural Selection
sections.body:
Major ingredients of Natural Selection
All species have great potential fertility
But most die before they can reproduce

Thomas Malthus
1766-1834
Malthus showed that populations increase exponentially, but limiting factors should cause population growth to level off.  Populations grow faster than the resources needed to sustain them

SPEAKER NOTES: Malthus showed that populations increase exponentially, but limiting factors should cause population growth to level off.  Populations grow faster than the resources needed to sustain them

sections.body:
Major ingredients of Natural Selection

2) Populations normally display stability in size
3) Population growth levels off in most species due to limiting factors
What are some limiting factors?

sections.body:
Inference from ingredients
1) Organisms have a high reproductive potential
2) Populations remain stable (i.e., don’t increase forever)
3) Resources are limited

Many individuals must die, and there is a struggle to survive

SPEAKER NOTES: Competition for resources. Only some survive

sections.label: Slide 18: Major ingredients of Natural Selection
sections.body:
Major ingredients of Natural Selection
4) Populations must display phenotypic variation
        - No two individuals are exactly the same

sections.label: Slide 19: Natural selection favors phenotypes that are best suited for the environment
sections.body:
Natural selection favors phenotypes that are best suited for the environment
Individuals with poorly-suited phenotypes will have low fitness
Fitness: The ability of an individual to survive and reproduce relative to the ability of other individuals of the same population.

An Adaptation: A trait or characteristic of organisms that increases their fitness relative to individuals without the trait. Produced by natural selection

sections.label: Slide 20: What else is needed for evolution by natural selection?
sections.body:
What else is needed for evolution by natural selection?
5) Heritability – the degree to which a trait is inherited
Mechanisms of inheritance were not understood in Darwin’s time

Mom

Dad
Gregor Mendel

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture7.jpg" (originally from URL: https://encrypted-tbn3.google.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcQHtb1l_WytTjqXF2OpFk4W2Ih8USlAxGsVFslfY9hoDchbzzKB2g)
Image 2: File "Picture9.jpg" (originally from URL: http://2.bp.blogspot.com/_BwlbMaa50jo/S9nhogkYe0I/AAAAAAAAGTM/PJPvt3rVVuc/s1600/punnett+square+green+pea.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 21: Inference from ingredients
sections.body:
Inference from ingredients
1) Organisms have a high reproductive potential
2) Populations remain stable (i.e., don’t increase forever)
3) Resources are limited
4) Populations display phenotypic variation
5) Some traits are heritable, but the degree to which a trait is inherited varies

SPEAKER NOTES: Competition for resources. Only some survive

sections.label: Slide 22: Darwin puzzled over natural selection for 20 years before publishing
sections.body:
Darwin puzzled over natural selection for 20 years before publishing

Alfred Russel Wallace was coming to the same conclusions in Southeast Asia
Pushed Darwin to publish (1858)
Linnean Society of London (1858) read Darwin & Wallace’s work, but it didn’t make an impression

On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection (1859)
Became a best seller and shocked many readers
His work established Evolution as a scientific field of study

SPEAKER NOTES: Linnean Society of London (1858) read Darwin & Wallace’s work, but it didn’t make an impression

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture9.jpg" (originally from URL: http://wallacefund.info/sites/wallacefund.info/files/u2/ARW%20in%201869.Small_.jpg)
Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (alt text: "Biography of Charles Darwin, 19th Century Naturalist")

sections.label: Slide 23: The logic of natural selection
sections.body:
The logic of natural selection
Individuals within populations are variable.

Some of these variations are heritable and are passed on to offspring.

In every generation, some offspring are more successful at surviving and reproducing than others.

The survival and reproduction of individuals are not random. Those with traits better suited for survival and reproduction are naturally selected.

examples: Variation: individuals in a population differ in traits (beak size, coat color, etc.)
Heritability: offspring resemble parents more than unrelated individuals.
Differential reproductive success: some variants leave more descendants.
Inference: the successful variants' traits become more common each generation.
warnings: If ANY ingredient is missing, natural selection cannot occur. A trait must be heritable — non-heritable differences (e.g., sunburn) do not evolve.
Natural selection acts on phenotypes, but evolution is measured as allele frequency change at the genotype level.
popup.mnemonic: VHI-DRS: Variation + Heritability + Inference-from-Differential-Reproductive-Success = Evolution.

flashcard.front: What are the four necessary ingredients for evolution by natural selection, and what is the logical inference that follows from combining them?
flashcard.back: (1) Phenotypic variation among individuals; (2) Heritability of that variation (offspring resemble parents); (3) Differential survival and reproduction based on phenotype (i.e., fitness differences); (4) More offspring produced than can survive (Malthusian overproduction). INFERENCE: The phenotypes linked to higher fitness will become more common each generation — the population evolves. If ANY ingredient is missing, no evolution by natural selection occurs.

quiz.question: A population of plants shows variation in leaf shape, but leaf shape is entirely determined by soil chemistry and not passed from parent to offspring. Can natural selection act on this variation?
quiz.correct: No — without heritability, differential reproduction will not change allele frequencies
distractors: Yes — any variation will lead to evolution
Yes — but only if plants have sexual reproduction
No — natural selection cannot act on plants at all

visual.mnemonic: VHOD→N: Variation, Heritability, Overproduction, Differential fitness → Next generation changed.
visual.trap: Natural selection does NOT produce traits — it only sorts among existing variants. The source of variation is mutation + recombination.

v2.definition: Natural selection requires variation, heritability, and differential reproductive success, the three ingredients that produce evolution by selection.

mnemonic.explanation: Latin for 'life', the four ingredients that produce evolutionary life from random differences.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Selection requires intent.' Natural selection has no goal. Organisms with higher fitness simply leave more descendants.

actions.quizPrompt: List the four ingredients required for natural selection.
actions.flashcardBack: Variation, Inheritance, Differential reproduction, Adaptation.

nodes.title: Galápagos Finches: Selection Case Study

The Evolution of Beak Shape in Galápagos Finches

ground finch
(Geospiza fortis)
Research by Drs. Peter & Rosemary Grant (Princeton University)

The Daphne Major population

are nonrandom concerning beak depth.

sections.label: Slide 29: Why did selection favor larger beaks?
sections.body:
Why did selection favor larger beaks?
Proximate mechanism: a change in abundance and characteristics of available seed

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture7.jpg" (alt text: "FG03_08b")
Image 2: File "Picture6.jpg" (alt text: "FG03_08c")

sections.label: Slide 30: Positive selection on beak size
sections.body:
Positive selection on beak size

…Lead to a shift in beak size in the next generation

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (alt text: "Beak depth hatchlings before & after drought")
Image 2: File "Picture4.jpg" (alt text: "Beak depth hatchlings before & after drought")

sections.label: Slide 31: It’s not all about survival
sections.body:
It’s not all about survival

Sexual selection – a form of natural selection involving differential reproductive success resulting from competition for fertilization

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture12.jpg" (alt text: "indian peafowl, blue peafowl, bird, peacock, tail")
Image 2: File "Picture18.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.robertweselmann.com/images/elk_massive_antlers.jpg)
Image 3: File "Picture16.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.nhm.ac.uk/resources-rx/images/1049/dynastes-neptunus-06_82507_1.jpg)

examples: Peter and Rosemary Grant measured beak depth in Geospiza fortis on Daphne Major across decades.
1977 drought: small soft seeds disappeared; only large hard seeds remained. Birds with larger, deeper beaks survived at higher rates.
Next generation had significantly larger average beak size — directly measured evolution by natural selection.
popup.mnemonic: 4 conditions confirmed in finches: Variable beaks, Heritable beaks, Survival differences, Beak size mattered → Positive selection for larger beaks.

flashcard.front: How did the Grants' Galápagos finch study provide direct empirical evidence for each ingredient of natural selection?
flashcard.back: (1) VARIATION: Beak depth varied among individual Geospiza fortis. (2) HERITABILITY: Parent-offspring regression showed beak depth is heritable (h² ≈ 0.65-0.8). (3) DIFFERENTIAL SURVIVAL: During the 1977 drought, birds with deeper beaks ate hard Tribulus seeds and survived while smaller-beaked birds died. (4) INFERENCE CONFIRMED: The next generation had significantly larger average beak depth — selection was measured, not inferred.

quiz.question: In the Grant finch study, what was the selective pressure that drove evolution of larger beaks in 1977?
quiz.correct: A drought eliminated small, soft seeds, leaving mostly large, hard seeds that only birds with deep beaks could crack
distractors: A new predator preferred small-beaked birds
Birds intentionally grew larger beaks during the drought
Climate cooling caused beaks to grow larger

visual.mnemonic: Drought → Hard seeds → Deep beaks win.
visual.trap: After the drought ended, selection sometimes reversed direction (wet years favored smaller beaks). Selection is not unidirectional — it tracks environment.

v2.definition: Grant and Grant tracked Galapagos finches across droughts, showing beak size evolved measurably within just a few generations.

mnemonic.explanation: Hard seeds during drought favor strong beaks. Wet years reverse the trend within months.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'The finches evolved during the drought.' The POPULATION evolved as birds with shallow beaks died.

actions.quizPrompt: How did the 1977 drought drive beak-size evolution in Geospiza fortis?
actions.flashcardFront: Who studied Galapagos finch evolution in real time?
actions.flashcardBack: Peter and Rosemary Grant on Daphne Major (40 plus years).

nodes.title: Descent with Modification & Common Ancestry
nodes.subtitle: Artificial selection, homology, tree-thinking (Lec 2 slides 32-41)

popup.heading: Lecture 2 — Descent with Modification

sections.label: Slide 32: Artificial selection
sections.body:
Artificial selection
Breeders non-randomly choose individuals with favorable traits (often economic reasons) to use as breeding stock

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.google.com/url?source=imglanding&ct=img&q=http://biology.nicerweb.com/med/Artificial_selection_pigeons.jpg&sa=X&ei=VbT0UJCqO4Xq9ATTtIFI&ved=0CAsQ8wc&usg=AFQjCNF0OYGuuG60yiRtn3n-zsOqyCgm_Q)
Image 2: File "Picture10.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.google.com/url?source=imglanding&ct=img&q=http://www.csus.edu/indiv/l/loom/wk%2015/dogs.jpg&sa=X&ei=eLX0UITJLI2i8ATgkoHoAQ&ved=0CAwQ8wc&usg=AFQjCNE4ZJWQo_gMr0W7O8v6PRovI8A6Zg)
Image 3: File "Picture8.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.google.com/url?source=imglanding&ct=img&q=http://www.doctortee.com/dsu/tiftickjian/cse-img/biology/evolution/mustard-selection.jpg&sa=X&ei=QLX0UNLeEYrO9QTz3oG4Bw&ved=0CAwQ8wc&usg=AFQjCNEuZODcMZxUuTLymO80gfusRJzA2w)

sections.label: Slide 33: Important points for understanding natural selection
sections.body:
Important points for understanding natural selection
1) Natural selection acts on individuals, but its consequences are seen in populations
2) Natural selection acts DIRECTLY on  phenotypes, but evolution is changes in allele frequencies
3) Natural selection is NOT forward looking – selection does not anticipate future environmental changes (always a generation behind an environmental change)
4) Evolution by natural selection results in “decent with modification”

SPEAKER NOTES: Each generation is descended from survivors of selection from the previous generation.

Offspring are better adapted to environments than their parent’s generation was
Selection does not anticipate environmental changes for future generations

sections.label: Slide 34: Descent with modification
sections.body:
Descent with modification

Homology: A similarity in a character or the characteristics of two species due to inheritance from a common ancestor.
What would be an example of an analogous structure?

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture8.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 35: Homologies in vertebrate development
sections.body:
Homologies in vertebrate development

Branching blood vessels for absorbing oxygen in gills change to allow absorption of oxygen by lungs

sections.label: Slide 36: Important points for understanding natural selection
sections.body:
Important points for understanding natural selection
1) Natural selection acts on individuals, but its consequences are seen in populations
2) Natural selection acts DIRECTLY on  phenotypes, but evolution is changes in allele frequencies
3) Natural selection is NOT forward looking – selection does not anticipate future environmental changes (always a generation behind an environmental change)
4) Evolution by natural selection results in “decent with modification”
5) Natural selection is NOT a progressive linear march (there are no ‘higher’ or ‘lower’ plants or animals)

sections.label: Slide 37: These images Imply a march of progress, and misrepresent evolution
sections.body:
These images Imply a march of progress, and misrepresent evolution

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-1.dropbox.com/t/0/AACu4jHQ6JJXc_yWZOfUTIlUZCvA-gr8A1EfztkX6biRtg/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1357254000/0/2/01.26_fmt.jpeg/xc64fAjtiH77OHG9h7XbZMUeeBVdmZ9BlqgxKkmOvqI?size=1280x960)

sections.label: Slide 38: Evolution is a tree-like process of diversification
sections.body:
Evolution is a tree-like process of diversification

Phylogenetic trees are a visual representation of decent with modification

sections.label: Slide 39: Summary of points for understanding natural selection
sections.body:
Summary of points for understanding natural selection
1) Natural selection acts on individuals, but its consequences are seen in populations
2) Natural selection acts on phenotypes, but evolution is changes in gene frequencies
3) Natural selection is NOT forward looking
4) Evolution by natural selection results in “decent with modification”
5) Natural selection is NOT a progressive linear march
6) Natural selection does NOT lead to perfection

sections.label: Slide 40: Vestigial organs
sections.body:
Vestigial organs
Functionless or rudimentary version of a body part that has an important function in other, closely related, species

Erectile hair

SPEAKER NOTES: Muscles attach to our hair follicles – makes hair stand up when cold or when frightened

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture14.jpg" (alt text: "Brown Kiwi 1.jpg")
Image 2: File "Picture7.jpg" (alt text: "Gorilla")
Image 3: File "Picture6.jpg" (alt text: "Hair")
Image 4: File "Picture3.jpg"
Image 5: File "Picture4.jpg" (alt text: "Kiwi wing")
Image 6: File "Picture6.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-2.dropbox.com/t/0/AADL3aVNJJpjMIy2MFmlCacJzF8-uMCeGIznpSRln42h_g/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1357254000/0/2/04.27_fmt.jpeg/GcGM_noMIf6e3EDGtMxgorhNwprGzlZCtDifgQS9hOY?size=1280x960)
Image 7: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: http://cbcreatures.webs.com/spurs1.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 41: Lecture take home points
sections.body:
Lecture take home points
The idea of evolutionary change was suggested by many before Darwin, but Darwin’s theory of evolution by natural selection provided a mechanism by which evolution works

There are five main ingredients that led to Darwin’s conclusions about natural selection

Homologies provide evidence for decent with modification

Natural selection acts on individual phenotypes, but can change gene frequencies in populations

Evolution is a response to natural selection

VISUALS: None.


###############################################

examples: Artificial selection on dogs, cabbage (all Brassica oleracea cultivars), corn — proves phenotypes can be dramatically reshaped by selection in few generations.
Vertebrate embryonic homologies: pharyngeal arches, notochord — shared developmental stages reveal common ancestry.
Vestigial organs: whale pelvis, human appendix, cave-fish eyes — traces of ancestral use.
warnings: The "march of progress" image (ape → human) is misleading — evolution is branching, not linear. Humans did not evolve FROM modern chimpanzees; we share a common ancestor.
popup.mnemonic: DHA-V: Descent + Homology + Artificial selection + Vestigial organs = evidence for common ancestry.

flashcard.front: How do vestigial organs provide evidence for descent with modification?
flashcard.back: Vestigial organs (e.g., whale hip bones, human appendix, flightless bird wings, cave fish eye sockets) are structures that have lost their original function but persist as reduced, functionless remnants. Their existence is only explicable under descent with modification: an ancestor used the structure, selection pressure for that function was relaxed, and the structure degraded over generations but was not fully eliminated. Under special creation, these "useless" structures would require ad-hoc explanations.

quiz.question: Which evidence most directly refutes the "march of progress" interpretation of evolution?
quiz.correct: Phylogenies show evolution is a branching tree, with modern species as tips — not a linear sequence
distractors: Mutations never happen in modern populations
All modern species are equally ancient
Fossils are rare and poorly preserved

visual.description: Branching vs linear models of evolution
visual.mnemonic: Tree not ladder.
visual.trap: Humans are not descended from chimps — humans and chimps share a common ancestor ~7 million years ago.

v2.definition: Common descent links all species through a tree of life evidenced by homology, artificial selection, and shared development.

mnemonic.explanation: Bat wing, whale flipper, and human arm share bone structure inherited from one ancestor.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Bird wings and bat wings are homologous as wings.' Wings are analogous, the bones underneath are homologous.

actions.quizPrompt: How does artificial selection support Darwin's natural selection argument?
actions.flashcardBack: Homology equals shared ancestor; analogy equals similar function only.

nodes.title: Genes, Proteins & Gene Expression
nodes.subtitle: DNA-RNA-protein and regulatory networks (Lec 3 slides 1-9)

popup.heading: Lecture 3 — Genes and Heritable Variation

sections.label: Slide 1: Genes and heritable variation
sections.body:
Genes and heritable variation

A brief review of molecular genetics

Evolution
BIOL 4340
University of Nebraska Omaha
Dr. Travis Robbins

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 2: Genetic variation
sections.body:
Genetic variation
A brief review of molecular genetics
Lecture goals
1) To understand the role of mutations in evolution
2) To understand how sexual reproduction creates new genetic variation
3) To understand the link between genotype and phenotype (or lack thereof)
4) To distinguish between a genetic polymorphism and a polygenic trait

sections.label: Slide 3: Proteins are workhorses of the cell
sections.body:
Proteins are workhorses of the cell
Amino acids – What all living things use to form proteins
Amino acid sequences determine the protein’s function
Shape and charge

SPEAKER NOTES: One of the most important classes of molecules are proteins

Amino acids bind together to form proteins, which serve many different kinds of functions

-enzymes, storage of molecules, create cellular structure

20 different amino acids, and the sequence of amino acids in a protein determines the proteins function.

Charges in amino acids attract or repel other amino acids. This causes them to fold into a 3-dimensional shape, which allows it to carry out its function

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-5.dropbox.com/t/0/AAC94t8XJ_2V7Ps4_0f6VGrjH72ih_uMn5gThdJGIeo7Bg/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1358985600/0/2/05.02_fmt.jpeg/e9vimS0GVB03B0sLpb1uJz-skDU5b4CtRpD1oKAGNO8?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

sections.label: Slide 4: Proteins are workhorses of the cell
sections.body:
Proteins are workhorses of the cell
Amino acids – What all living things use to form proteins
RNA U =  DNA T

Amino acid sequences determine the protein’s function
Shape and charge

DNA stores the information for assembling that sequence as nucleotides
purines (A or G) and pyrimidines (C or T)

SPEAKER NOTES: One of the most important classes of molecules are proteins

Amino acids bind together to form proteins, which serve many different kinds of functions

-enzymes, storage of molecules, create cellular structure

20 different amino acids, and the sequence of amino acids in a protein determines the proteins function.

Charges in amino acids attract or repel other amino acids. This causes them to fold into a 3-dimensional shape, which allows it to carry out its function

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 5: DNA – a homology found across all organisms
sections.body:
DNA – a homology found across all organisms
Nucleotides link together to form the backbone of DNA
New copies of DNA are made when cells divide
Mistakes occur – mutations: A change to the genomic sequence of an organism

SPEAKER NOTES: Compelling evidence that we all shared a common ancestry

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-3.dropbox.com/t/0/AACFZ491mdyeaA_myX1jNToQ763KAlwRjivtB1i3WTANtA/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1358985600/0/2/05.03_fmt.jpeg/NIFG_OdYghFcLOLWyW8zXLQQbYQVP8psKN59mPz_EFI?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

sections.label: Slide 6: Gene expression
sections.body:
Gene expression
Genes – segments of DNA with nucleotide sequences that code for proteins, RNA, or regulate expression of other genes
Cells use information from genes to produce proteins
this information is transformed into a product (function)
called gene expression

Not all genes are expressed simultaneously
Different cells express different genes and at different times

SPEAKER NOTES: Information tranformed into a product via process of Transcription and translation

Transcription - RNA polymerase reads DNA sequence and produces mRNA

Translation – mRNA decoded by a ribosome to produce strand of amino acids

Hair cells express different proteins than do our blood cells

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-2.dropbox.com/t/0/AABwHSD0gkH8_SQfN0RomFvaebGz0PBA9q6Rc2M1n_GoaQ/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1358985600/0/2/05.04_fmt.jpeg/jQ_py2ksfNQ3rlgqDemT74GynwSBLJNXrnPXDyDWtZ4?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

sections.label: Slide 7: Hindlimbs in dolphins
sections.body:
Hindlimbs in dolphins

Subtle changes in gene expression can give rise to new phenotypes

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 8: Gene expression is regulated by many factors
sections.body:
Gene expression is regulated by many factors

DNA is tightly bundled around histones and into chromosomes

By unwinding or winding of DNA around histones, cells can expose or hide genes
Transcription factors – Proteins that bind to specific DNA sequences and switch on or off sequences
Hormones – Molecular signals that flow throughout the body and bind to cells and alter gene expression

SPEAKER NOTES: Can determine when and where genes are expressed

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (alt text: "05.09.png")

sections.label: Slide 9: Gene expression is often influenced by a complex network of interactions
sections.body:
Gene expression is often influenced by a complex network of interactions

Gene Expression and Regulation | Amoeba Sisters (9:54)

SPEAKER NOTES: Can determine when and where genes are expressed

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture5.jpg"

examples: DNA is a universal homology across all life — the code itself is evidence of common ancestry.
Dolphin hindlimb genes are still present but transcriptionally silenced — regulation, not gene loss, explains limb absence.
Gene expression is regulated by complex networks: enhancers, transcription factors, miRNA, chromatin state.
popup.mnemonic: DNA → RNA → Protein (Central dogma), but REGULATION of this flow is what determines phenotype.

flashcard.front: Why are dolphin hindlimb genes a surprising illustration of gene expression regulation?
flashcard.back: Dolphins are limbless in hindquarters, but genomic sequencing reveals the genes for building hindlimbs are still present in their DNA. Their hindlimbs do not develop because the regulatory pathway controlling limb outgrowth is suppressed during development — not because the genes were deleted. This shows that evolution can "lose" traits not by removing genes but by changing expression patterns. Occasionally, a dolphin is born with vestigial hindlimbs, revealing the dormant genetic capacity.

quiz.question: The observation that DNA is used as the hereditary molecule in all domains of life (bacteria, archaea, eukaryotes) is evidence for:
quiz.correct: Common ancestry of all extant life
distractors: Independent origins of each domain
Convergent molecular evolution
Horizontal gene transfer eliminating other molecules

visual.mnemonic: Genes code, regulation decides.
visual.trap: Having a gene does NOT mean the trait is expressed. Regulation is just as important as sequence.

v2.definition: Genes encode proteins through DNA to RNA to protein, with regulatory networks controlling when and where genes activate.

mnemonic.hook: DNA to RNA to Protein to Trait
mnemonic.explanation: The central dogma flows one way, plus regulation determines when each gene speaks aloud.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'One gene equals one trait.' Most traits involve regulatory networks plus environment, polygenic not single gene.

actions.quizPrompt: Trace the central dogma from DNA to phenotype.
actions.flashcardBack: Transcription (DNA to mRNA) then translation (mRNA to protein).

nodes.title: Mutations: Raw Material of Evolution
nodes.subtitle: Types, rates, and effects (Lec 3 slides 10-17)

popup.heading: Lecture 3 — Mutations

sections.label: Slide 10: Mutations are the raw material for evolution
sections.body:
Mutations are the raw material for evolution

23 pairs of chromosomes in humans  & Neanderthals
24 pairs in other apes
Result of chromosome fusion of chromosome #2.

Chimps have nearly identical DNA sequences to chromosome #2, but in chimps those sequences are on 2 separate chromosomes.

Also, human chromosome 2 has 2 centromeres (normally they have 1), one is vestigial.

SPEAKER NOTES: 23 pairs of chromomsomes in humans  & Neanderthals
24 pairs in other apes

Result of chromosome fusion of chromosome #2.  Chimps have nearly identical DNA sequences to chromosome #2, but in chimps those sequences are on 2 separate chromosomes. Also human chromosome 2 has 2 centromeres (normally they have 1), one is vestigial.

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (alt text: "05.13.png")

sections.label: Slide 11: Effects of mutations
sections.body:
Effects of mutations
Gene product is not always altered due to mutation (code redundancy)
Can change how much protein is made, or the timing or location of its production
Can prevent transcription factors from binding, thereby inhibiting gene expression
Mutations have evolutionary consequences because they add heritable genetic variation

SPEAKER NOTES: If mutations alter the protein coding region of a gene with a different sequence of amino acids, the protien can fold into a new shape.  May change function or change the rate of the activity.

Can also make the transcription factor bind more strongly

sections.label: Slide 12: Effects of point mutations
sections.body:
Effects of point mutations

Albinism: one amino acid change in the FGFR3 gene

Thumb polydactyly: Single C to T transition in the LMBR1 gene

Hutchinson-Gilford progeria syndrome: point mutation in the Lamin A gene (1 in 8 million)

SPEAKER NOTES: Symptoms of aging appear at a young age (1 in 8 million). People with this live to mid teens or early twenties

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-4.dropbox.com/t/0/AABoZOFGGFZ9uF6IeyTk9lssHYbWIufkYTwmiYKSYnAPGg/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1359313200/0/2/05.14_fmt.jpeg/d6hg1nBU4O1fhkJeYV-A6eRNpwP8WxngiVsn1zAO0Ak?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)
Image 2: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-4.dropbox.com/t/0/AABoZOFGGFZ9uF6IeyTk9lssHYbWIufkYTwmiYKSYnAPGg/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1359313200/0/2/05.14_fmt.jpeg/d6hg1nBU4O1fhkJeYV-A6eRNpwP8WxngiVsn1zAO0Ak?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)
Image 3: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-4.dropbox.com/t/0/AABoZOFGGFZ9uF6IeyTk9lssHYbWIufkYTwmiYKSYnAPGg/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1359313200/0/2/05.14_fmt.jpeg/d6hg1nBU4O1fhkJeYV-A6eRNpwP8WxngiVsn1zAO0Ak?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

sections.label: Slide 13: How common are mutations?
sections.body:
How common are mutations?
Experimentally address this with yeast (unicellular fungi)

Reared colonies from a single ancestor for 4800 generations
Sequenced 12 million base pairs from some descendants
0.00000003% chance of undergoing a point mutation – very rare
Populations are made up of millions of cells
Each site (i.e. base pair, nucleotide) in its DNA has a 0.0000003% chance of undergoing a point mutation.  3 out of 100 million.
Thousands of mutations will arise in each generation

SPEAKER NOTES: Each site in its DNA has a 0.0000003% change of undergoing a point mutation.  3 out of 100 million

Thousands of mutations will arise in each generation

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://scienceforkids.kidipede.com/biology/cells/pictures/yeastbuds.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 14: Mutation rate in Arabidopsis thaliana
sections.body:
Mutation rate in Arabidopsis thaliana

Spontaneous mutations in the nuclear genomes

Five lines that had been maintained by single-seed descent for 30 generations.
Results
Found 99 base substitutions and 17 insertions and deletions
A spontaneous mutation rate of 7 × 10-9 base substitutions per site per generation.
0.000000007% chance or 7 out of 1 billion.

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg"
Image 2: File "Picture3.jpg" (alt text: "Arabidopsis_thaliana_plant.jpg")

sections.label: Slide 15: How common are mutations per generation?
sections.body:
How common are mutations per generation?

SPEAKER NOTES: Each site in its DNA has a 0.0000003% change of undergoing a point mutation.  3 out of 100 million

Thousands of mutations will arise in each generation

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg"
Image 2: File "Picture8.jpg"
Image 3: File "Picture6.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 16: Mutation rates
sections.body:
Mutation rates
| Organism | Mutation | Rate | Units |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Bacteriophage T2 | r  r+ | 1 × 10-8 | Per replication |
| E. coli | his+  his– | 2 × 10-6 | Per cell division |
|  | his–  his+ | 4 × 10-8 | Per cell division |
| Drosophila | W  w | 4 × 10-5 | Per gamete |
| Corn | R  r | 4.9 × 10-4 | " |
|  | Pr  pr | 1.1 × 10-5 | " |
|  | Sh  sh | 1.2 × 10-6 | " |
| Mouse | D  d | 3 × 10-5 | " |
| Human | Huntington's | 1 × 10-6 | " |
|  | Hemophilia A | 2-4 × 10-5 | " |
| C. elegans (nematode) | Point, insertion, deletion | 1.6 x 10-7 | Per base pair per generation |
| A. thaliana (plant) | Point, insertion, deletion | 7 x 10-9 | Per base pair per generation |

sections.label: Slide 17: Mutations in multicellular organisms
sections.body:
Mutations in multicellular organisms
Not all mutations are expressed or transmitted to the next generation
Genes are in duplicate (diploidy)  – some mutations are hidden by functional copies of the gene
Somatic mutations: mutations that affect cells of the body
They affect all daughter cells produced
They affect the phenotype/fitness of an individual
But not passed to offspring
Germ-line mutations: mutations in gametes (sperm or egg)
Passed on across generations
Create heritable genetic variation important for evolution

SPEAKER NOTES: Somatic mutations can be passed in plants via vegetative reproduction

examples: Point mutations: substitution (silent / missense / nonsense), insertion, deletion.
Arabidopsis thaliana mutation rate: ~7 × 10⁻⁹ per site per generation.
Human germ-line mutation rate: ~70 new mutations per generation (of ~3 billion bp).
Mutations in multicellular organisms: only germline mutations are heritable; somatic mutations affect only the individual.
warnings: Most mutations are neutral or slightly deleterious. Strongly beneficial mutations are rare.
Mutation rate is NOT determined by need — it is a stochastic process.
popup.mnemonic: MRS-BE: Mutations are Random, Stochastic, Beneficial-rarely, Essential (no evolution without them).

flashcard.front: Compare the evolutionary consequences of somatic versus germline mutations.
flashcard.back: SOMATIC mutations occur in non-reproductive cells (skin, liver, etc.). They affect only the individual and die with that individual — they cannot be inherited, so they do not contribute to population-level evolution. GERMLINE mutations occur in cells that give rise to gametes (eggs and sperm). These can be passed to offspring and become the raw material of evolution. Cancer is typically caused by accumulated somatic mutations; evolution is driven by germline mutations.

quiz.question: A woman develops a BRCA1 mutation in breast tissue during adulthood. This mutation is:
quiz.correct: Somatic — it will not be inherited by her children
distractors: Germline — her children will inherit it with 50% probability
Both somatic and germline — all cell mutations are heritable
Not a true mutation because it occurred after birth

visual.mnemonic: SMNF: Silent, Missense, Nonsense, Frameshift — ordered by typical severity.
visual.trap: A "silent" mutation is not always silent — some affect mRNA stability, splicing, or codon usage and can have phenotypic effects.

v2.definition: Mutations are random heritable changes in DNA, the ultimate source of all genetic variation that fuels evolution.

mnemonic.explanation: Mutations arise blindly. Selection then sorts beneficial from harmful: random input, non random output.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Mutations happen to help organisms adapt.' Mutations are random with respect to need, selection filters them.

actions.quizPrompt: Why are mutations called 'random with respect to fitness'?
actions.flashcardBack: Mutation, random heritable changes in DNA sequence.

nodes.title: Sex Cells & Meiotic Variation
nodes.subtitle: How meiosis generates diversity (Lec 3 slides 18-19)

popup.heading: Lecture 3 — Meiotic Sources of Variation

sections.label: Slide 18: Sex cells introduce new genetic variation
sections.body:
Sex cells introduce new genetic variation
Sexually-reproducing organisms do not produce genetic clones because parental chromosomal complements are not genetically identical

Allele: One of several alternative forms of the DNA sequence of the same locus
Allele differences between chromosome pairs in each generation create variation among the gamete cells produced

SPEAKER NOTES: Different alleles inherited from parents

sections.label: Slide 19: Meiosis generates variation
sections.body:
Meiosis generates variation
Genetic recombination: exchange of genetic material between paired chromosomes during meiosis
i.e. Crossing over
Independent assortment
Sexual reproduction then shuffles up combinations of alleles even more
Millions of allele combinations

Meiosis - Plants and Animals (6:45)

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-6.dropbox.com/t/0/AAAmOvVEIokjGtMJOz4fRe7Kbs7nQPQ6CmoG72TCW1rrUg/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1359313200/0/2/05.15_fmt%282%29.jpeg/BAZRghv0iveWPr_h123VOMSk4HtB0754p3CSGsKOLvo?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

examples: Meiotic crossing over (recombination) during prophase I — shuffles alleles between homologs.
Independent assortment of homologs during metaphase I — 2^n possible combinations for n chromosome pairs (2^23 ≈ 8 million in humans).
Random fertilization: any sperm can meet any egg, multiplying variation further.
popup.mnemonic: CIR: Crossing over + Independent assortment + Random fertilization = meiotic variation engine.

flashcard.front: How many genetically distinct gametes can a single human potentially produce, ignoring new mutations?
flashcard.back: Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes. Through independent assortment alone, 2²³ ≈ 8.4 million different combinations of homologs are possible. Adding crossing over (recombination) between homologs dramatically increases this number to effectively infinite (~10^1000+ depending on crossover positions). Random fertilization then multiplies this by the same factor in the partner — so each human offspring is effectively genetically unique (identical twins excepted).

quiz.question: Which meiotic process contributes to genetic variation by shuffling alleles between homologous chromosomes?
quiz.correct: Crossing over (recombination) during prophase I
distractors: Independent assortment during metaphase I
Mitotic division of germ cells
Sister chromatid separation during anaphase II

visual.mnemonic: Three levels of mixing in sex.
visual.trap: Mitosis does not generate variation — only meiosis + fertilization do. This is why asexual lineages accumulate mutations linearly while sexual lineages can recombine them.

v2.definition: Meiosis generates genetic diversity through independent assortment and recombination, shuffling parental alleles into novel offspring combinations.

mnemonic.explanation: Meiosis shuffles chromosomes and trades parts before dealing each gamete a new genetic hand.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Meiosis creates new mutations.' It SHUFFLES existing variation; mutations come from DNA replication errors only.

actions.quizPrompt: How does meiosis generate genetic diversity without new mutations?
actions.flashcardBack: Independent assortment of chromosomes and recombination via crossing over.

Rarely is it a one-to-one linkage

sections.label: Slide 29: Lecture take home points
sections.body:
Lecture take home points
Mutations occur during DNA replication, and are a major source of heritable genetic variation (raw material for evolution)

Gene expression can be regulated by many factors (e.g., transcription factors, hormones, environment)

Mutation rates are low, but when considering population sizes mutations are common

Sexual reproduction also creates new genetic variation

Phenotype is the expression of the genotype

VISUALS: None.


###############################################

examples: Genetic polymorphism: simultaneous existence of two or more discrete forms in a population (e.g., blood types, color morphs).
Polygenic traits: phenotype determined by many genes (e.g., height, skin color, disease risk) — produces continuous variation.
Environmental effects: same genotype can produce different phenotypes (reaction norms — covered more in Lec 5-6).
popup.mnemonic: PPE: Polymorphism (discrete) vs Polygenic (continuous) vs Environmental (plasticity).

flashcard.front: What is the difference between a genetic polymorphism and a polygenic trait? Give an example of each.
flashcard.back: A POLYMORPHISM is the presence of two or more DISCRETE phenotypic variants in a population maintained by genetic variation at one or a few loci — e.g., human ABO blood types (3 alleles → 4 phenotypes), Mendelian pea flower color. A POLYGENIC trait is continuous variation controlled by MANY genes each contributing a small amount, usually producing a bell-curve distribution — e.g., human height, skin color, IQ, disease risk (BMI, hypertension). Polygenic traits can also be influenced by environment, producing gene × environment effects.

quiz.question: Human height is considered a polygenic trait because:
quiz.correct: It is influenced by many genes each contributing a small amount, producing continuous variation
distractors: It is controlled by a single dominant/recessive gene pair
It is determined entirely by environment and nutrition
It does not vary within populations

visual.mnemonic: Discrete vs Continuous vs Plastic.
visual.trap: Continuous variation does NOT mean purely environmental — many continuous traits are highly heritable.

v2.definition: Phenotype emerges from genotype interacting with environment, often through polygenic traits showing continuous quantitative variation.

mnemonic.explanation: DNA sets the possibilities. Environment determines which possibilities become real, neither alone is destiny.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Identical twins have identical phenotypes.' Same genotype but different environments produce different heights and weights.

actions.quizPrompt: Why don't identical twins have identical phenotypes?
actions.flashcardBack: Genotype times Environment (gene expression depends on context).

nodes.title: Sources of Evolutionary Change
nodes.subtitle: The 5 mechanisms that change allele frequencies (Lec 4 slides 1-3)

popup.heading: Lecture 4 — Sources of Evolutionary Change

sections.label: Slide 1: Genetic Evolution in Populations
sections.body:
Genetic Evolution in Populations

Evolution
BIOL 4340
University of Nebraska Omaha
Dr. Travis Robbins

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture3.jpg"
Image 2: File "Picture10.jpg"
Image 3: File "Picture7.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 2: Mendelian genetics in populations
sections.body:
Mendelian genetics in populations
Population: A group of inter-breeding individuals that exist together in space and time (share a distinct gene pool)

Population genetics: Study of the distribution of alleles within populations and the mechanisms that cause allele frequencies to change over time
Track inherited genes over generations by following allele and genotype frequencies
From population genetic perspective – Evolution is the change in allele frequency across generations

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture7.jpg"
Image 2: File "Picture8.jpg" (alt text: "HistoricalRange.jpg")

sections.label: Slide 3: Sources of evolutionary change
sections.body:
Sources of evolutionary change
Four forces that bring change in allele or genotype frequencies in populations

Genetic drift
Natural selection
Migration
Mutation

A lack of the force can be a null hypothesis and useful to understand how genotype or allele frequencies change within populations

SPEAKER NOTES: In order to understand how allele frequencies change in response so some outside force, it is important to establish a null hypothesis.

In statistics, a null hypothesis is a general statement that there is no relationship between two variables.  Compare 2 groups

In this case, we compare allele frequencies between what is expected versus what is observed.

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture5.jpg" (alt text: "A close up of a light saber Description automatically generated")

examples: Five evolutionary forces: mutation, genetic drift, gene flow, natural selection, non-random mating.
In a Hardy-Weinberg "null" population, all five forces are absent and allele frequencies do not change.
popup.mnemonic: MUD-GAS: Mutation, Unequal (drift), Drift, Gene flow, Assortative mating, Selection.

flashcard.front: Name the five evolutionary forces that can change allele frequencies in a population, and briefly describe each.
flashcard.back: (1) MUTATION — new alleles appear via DNA replication errors. (2) GENETIC DRIFT — random sampling causes allele frequencies to fluctuate between generations (stronger in small populations). (3) GENE FLOW (migration) — alleles move between populations when individuals immigrate/emigrate. (4) NATURAL SELECTION — differential reproductive success based on heritable traits. (5) NON-RANDOM MATING (assortative or inbreeding) — affects genotype frequencies (but technically not allele frequencies). All five disrupt Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium.

quiz.question: A population that satisfies Hardy-Weinberg assumptions will:
quiz.correct: Have allele frequencies that do not change from generation to generation
distractors: Evolve rapidly due to random mutation
Lose all genetic variation within a few generations
Exhibit directional natural selection

visual.mnemonic: Five forces disrupt H-W equilibrium.
visual.trap: Only mutation creates NEW variation. The others sort and redistribute existing variation.

v2.definition: Five forces change allele frequencies: mutation, gene flow, genetic drift, non random mating, and natural selection.

mnemonic.hook: MMDNS: Mutation, Migration, Drift, Nonrandom, Selection
mnemonic.explanation: Five forces push allele frequencies. Remove all five and Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium holds perfectly.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Natural selection is the only force of evolution.' Drift, gene flow, and mutation also change allele frequencies.

actions.quizPrompt: Name the five evolutionary forces and their effects.
actions.flashcardBack: Mutation, gene flow, drift, non-random mating, selection.

nodes.title: Hardy-Weinberg Theorem
nodes.subtitle: The null model of population genetics (Lec 4 slides 4-12)

popup.heading: Lecture 4 — Hardy-Weinberg Principle

sections.label: Slide 4: Hardy-Weinberg Principle
sections.body:
Hardy-Weinberg Principle

Specifies a null hypothesis as what genotype frequencies should be in the absence of outside forces
If we know the gene or genotype frequencies in one generation….
…Then we can predict what they will be in the next generation

SPEAKER NOTES: In science, Null models are statements that give us a basis for comparison. Physics: Newton’s first law – object in motion  stays in motion or an object at rest stays at rest when no force is acting.  By understanding this, we see that objects speed up or slow down when forces are acting on them.

In Biology we need the same kinds of null models for a basis of comparison. HW model tells us what happens to genotype frequencies when outside forces are absent.  So when we make observations that don’t fit HW predictions, then we can make inferences about what evolutionary processes are working.

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://home.cogeco.ca/%7Eparksidescienceq/SBI%204U/Hardy%20Weinberg%20photo.jpg)
Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://home.cogeco.ca/%7Eparksidescienceq/SBI%204U/Hardy%20Weinberg%20photo.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 5: Assumptions of the Hardy-Weinberg model
sections.body:
Assumptions of the Hardy-Weinberg model
No outside forces

No genetic drift
No natural selection
No migration (no gene flow)
No mutation
An additional assumption
Random mating (with respect to alleles at their genetic locus)
In the absence of evolution, allele and genotype frequencies do not change

Conclusions:
