Deficiency in heterozygotes (Aa)

What does this mean?

examples: Hardy-Weinberg equations: p + q = 1, p² + 2pq + q² = 1.
Five assumptions: (1) no mutation, (2) no selection, (3) no migration, (4) random mating, (5) infinite population (no drift).
Example: ADH locus in fruit flies — allele frequencies calculated from electrophoresis phenotypes.
If observed genotype frequencies match p² + 2pq + q² predictions, the population is in HWE for that locus.
warnings: HWE is a NULL hypothesis — we test whether a population is in equilibrium to detect evolutionary forces.
A population can be in HWE for one locus and not others.
popup.mnemonic: p² + 2pq + q² = 1. If observed ≠ expected → something is causing evolution.

flashcard.front: State the five assumptions of the Hardy-Weinberg principle and explain what happens if each one is violated.
flashcard.back: (1) NO MUTATION — if violated, new alleles enter the population and allele frequencies drift. (2) NO NATURAL SELECTION — if violated, fitness differences skew genotype frequencies. (3) NO MIGRATION (GENE FLOW) — if violated, alleles move in/out and frequencies change. (4) RANDOM MATING — if violated (e.g., assortative mating or inbreeding), genotype frequencies deviate from p² + 2pq + q² even without allele frequency change. (5) INFINITE POPULATION SIZE — if violated, genetic drift randomly changes allele frequencies (stronger in smaller populations). HWE gives us a NULL model: if observed genotype frequencies deviate from the predictions, one of these five assumptions is being violated.

quiz.question: In a population with alleles A and a at frequencies p = 0.6 and q = 0.4, what is the expected frequency of heterozygotes under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium?
distractors: 0.50 (always one-half)

visual.mnemonic: p² + 2pq + q² = 1 always works when population is at equilibrium.
visual.trap: Rare dominant alleles hide rarer recessive alleles. Always calculate q from q² (homozygous recessives), not from a count of dominant phenotypes.

v2.definition: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium predicts allele frequencies stay constant when no evolutionary forces act, the null model of population genetics.

keyTerms.term: p squared plus 2pq plus q squared

keyTerms.def: no mutation, drift, migration, mating, selection

mnemonic.hook: p squared plus 2pq plus q squared
mnemonic.explanation: The genotype equation works ONLY when all five evolutionary forces are absent, a hypothetical baseline.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'HWE describes real populations.' HWE is a NULL model. Deviation from HWE is how we DETECT evolution.

actions.quizPrompt: What are the five assumptions of Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium?
actions.flashcardBack: p squared plus 2pq plus q squared equals 1 (null model).

nodes.title: Genetic Drift, Bottlenecks & Founder Effects
nodes.subtitle: Random allele frequency change in finite populations (Lec 4 slides 13-22)

popup.heading: Lecture 4 — Genetic Drift

sections.label: Slide 13: Out of Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium
sections.body:
Out of Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium
Evolution is happening by one or more outside forces

Genetic drift
Natural selection
Migration (gene flow)
Mutation
Non-random mating
Next step is to test which assumption was violated

In other words, which mechanisms of evolution are at work?
Two mechanisms we will explore in more detail
1) Genetic drift
2) Natural selection

sections.label: Slide 14: 1) Genetic drift as a mechanism
sections.body:
1) Genetic drift as a mechanism
Genetic drift:
Evolution arising from random changes in the genetic composition of a population from one generation to the next

Example

Experimental evolution of Drosophila flies

Buri, P., 1956. Gene frequency in small populations of mutant Drosophila. Evolution, pp.367-402.

SPEAKER NOTES: Work by Peter Buri at Iowa State University, in the 1950’s

Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://biology.unm.edu/ccouncil/Biology_203/Images/PopGen/bottleneck.gif)

sections.label: Slide 21: Genetic bottleneck: Dramatic reduction in population size, having long-lasting effect on genetic var
sections.body:
Genetic bottleneck: Dramatic reduction in population size, having long-lasting effect on genetic variation

Frequency
Low genetic diversity even after populations have rebounded (~150 years after population recovery)
Loss of heterozygosity: i.e., certain alleles went to near or complete fixation

SPEAKER NOTES: More recent work has looked at genetic variation from museum specimens before the population went through a bottleneck. Showed that there was indeed much more heterozygosity, providing more evidence that these patterns were due to a drastic reduction in population size resulting in a bottleneck

Heterozygosity – measure of different alleles at one or more loci

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture6.jpg" (alt text: "img010.jpg")

sections.label: Slide 22: Founder effects
sections.body:
Founder effects
The loss of allelic variation associated with founding a new population from a small number of individuals

New population can differ considerably from the source population

Small number of founders results in genetic bottleneck
End lecture here

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://pdsblogs.org/chargerapes513/files/2012/10/foundere-1sj3vh8.jpg)

examples: Drift in Drosophila (Buri 1956): small populations fixed or lost bw75 allele randomly within ~19 generations.
Genetic bottleneck: dramatic population reduction causes major allele frequency changes that persist long after recovery (e.g., cheetahs, northern elephant seals).
Founder effect: a few individuals colonize a new area and carry a non-random sample of the source allele pool (e.g., Amish populations, Polynesian colonization).
warnings: Drift is NOT directional — it is random. But its effects are STRONGEST in small populations.
Bottleneck effects persist for many generations even after population recovers, because lost alleles are gone permanently.
popup.mnemonic: BFS: Bottleneck, Founder effect, Small population = strong drift.

flashcard.front: Explain how the founder effect differs from a genetic bottleneck, and give an example of each.
flashcard.back: A GENETIC BOTTLENECK occurs when an existing population is dramatically reduced in size (disease, disaster, overhunting), eliminating many alleles by chance. The survivors rebuild the population from a non-representative sample — example: cheetahs (~10,000 years ago bottleneck reduced genetic diversity to near-clonal levels; northern elephant seals reduced to ~20 individuals in the 1890s). A FOUNDER EFFECT occurs when a small number of individuals colonize a new habitat, bringing only a subset of the source population's alleles. The new population is genetically distinct from the source not by selection but by sampling chance — example: Amish populations in Pennsylvania show elevated frequencies of recessive disorders (e.g., polydactyly) because founders happened to carry those alleles. Both reduce genetic diversity through drift.

quiz.question: Genetic drift has the strongest effect on allele frequency change in which type of population?
quiz.correct: Small populations
distractors: Large populations with equal sex ratios
Populations at Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium
Populations with high gene flow

visual.mnemonic: Crash vs Colonize.
visual.trap: Drift can fix DELETERIOUS alleles if the population is small enough — selection is too weak to remove them.

v2.definition: Genetic drift is random change in allele frequencies due to finite sampling, stronger in small populations than large ones.

mnemonic.explanation: Drift's strength scales inversely with population size. Fewer individuals means bigger random swings.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Drift only matters in tiny populations.' Drift acts in all finite populations, selection just dominates in large ones.

actions.quizPrompt: Distinguish bottleneck from founder effect.
actions.flashcardBack: Random allele frequency change from finite-sample chance effects.

sections.label: Slide 28: Not all phenotypes are determined by two alleles at one locus
sections.body:
Not all phenotypes are determined by two alleles at one locus
Dominance effects:  A dominant allele will mask phenotypic expression of a recessive allele at a given locus
Other allelic interactions complicate patterns of selection
Pleiotropic effects: A single gene affects many different phenotypes

Ester1 allele in mosquitos provide insecticide resistance

…but makes individuals more prone to predators
Epistatic effects: Effects of an allele at one locus are modified by alleles at other loci
Additive effects: An allele that yields twice the phenotypic effect when two copies are present at a locus, rather than one (i.e., there is no dominance)

Why is this?

sections.label: Slide 30: Lecture take home points
sections.body:
Lecture take home points
Natural selection, genetic drift, mutation, and migration cause changes in allele frequencies in populations (these are mechanisms of evolution).

Hardy-Weinberg theorem predicts allele frequencies in the absence of evolutionary forces.

Genetic drift causes evolutionary change via random changes in allele frequencies.

Natural selection can cause evolutionary change via non-random processes.

Many phenotypes are determined by alleles at multiple loci.

sections.label: Slide 31: Practice
sections.body:
Practice
End of Chapter Questions for Practice (p. 199)

Multiple Choice
Questions 8 and 10

Short Answer
Questions 1 and 4

VISUALS: None.


###############################################

examples: Selection coefficient (s): the fitness reduction of a genotype relative to the best. Relative fitness w = 1-s.
Dominant, recessive, and additive allele interactions produce different rates of allele frequency change under selection.
Most complex traits are influenced by many loci — selection on one trait changes allele frequencies at many loci simultaneously.
popup.mnemonic: SCAR: Selection Coefficient = 1 - (fitness / best fitness). Relative fitness drives allele frequency change.

flashcard.front: How do the rates of allele frequency change differ when selection acts on a dominant versus a recessive allele?
flashcard.back: When selection FAVORS a DOMINANT allele, it rises in frequency rapidly at first because even heterozygotes express the trait — but fixing the last copies of the recessive allele is slow because rare recessives hide in heterozygotes (2pq is hard to eliminate). When selection FAVORS a RECESSIVE allele, it rises SLOWLY at first because only q² homozygotes are exposed to selection — but once it becomes common, it increases rapidly. SELECTING AGAINST a recessive deleterious allele is also inefficient: as q decreases, more and more of the remaining allele hides in heterozygotes, making it nearly impossible to fully eliminate.

quiz.question: A recessive lethal allele is under strong negative selection. Why does it persist at low frequencies rather than being eliminated entirely?
quiz.correct: Heterozygotes are not exposed to selection, so rare alleles hide in 2pq
distractors: The allele mutates back every generation
Recessive alleles are never subject to selection
Selection cannot eliminate deleterious alleles under any conditions

visual.mnemonic: Dominance hides rare alleles from selection.
visual.trap: You cannot eliminate a deleterious recessive allele completely by selection alone — it always hides in heterozygotes at low frequencies.

v2.definition: Selection coefficients quantify fitness differences between genotypes, predicting how allele frequencies change each generation.

mnemonic.explanation: Relative fitness equals one minus selection coefficient, a clean numerical way to compare genotypes.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Fitness equals strength or survival.' Fitness equals NUMBER of offspring left in next generation, reproduction is the metric.

actions.quizPrompt: How do selection coefficient and relative fitness relate?
actions.flashcardBack: Number of viable offspring left in next generation (relative).

sections.label: Slide 3: Quantitative Genetics, Selection, and Phenotypic Plasticity
sections.body:
Quantitative Genetics, Selection, and Phenotypic Plasticity
Lecture goals
To explain how continuous variation in phenotypes arises

To understand the different components of phenotypic variation

To explain how selection and heritability affect the speed of evolution

To understand how phenotypic plasticity can evolve

To contrast plastic and evolutionary changes within a phenotype

sections.label: Slide 4: Qualitative versus Quantitative
sections.body:
Qualitative versus Quantitative
Qualitative traits: Phenotypes in discrete categories
Influence of two alleles at one locus

Quantitative traits: Distribution of phenotypes is continuous

Influence of combined alleles at many loci

Polygenic traits: phenotypic variation
is attributed to many genes

SPEAKER NOTES: Mendel was quite lucky that he happened to choose a trait that was determined by two alleles at a single locus. Much easier to understand the complexities of quantitative traits when first have a basic understanding of the genetics of qualitative traits.

sections.body:
Phenotypic variation is not entirely due to genetics
Environment also produces phenotypic variation via
phenotypic plasticity

Ʃ(x – x)2
VP =
N

sections.label: Slide 13: Phenotypic variation
sections.body:
Phenotypic variation
Partition phenotypic variance into different components
VPhenotypic = VGenetic + VEnvironment
Variance due to environmental differences
Total phenotypic variance in population
Variance due to genetic differences
Degree of heritability will affect the potential to evolve
If VG = 0, then VP = VE
No evolution
If VG < VE
then evolutionary change can be slow

If VG > VE
then evolutionary change can be rapid

examples: Qualitative: discrete categories (flower color, presence/absence) — often Mendelian, 1 or 2 loci.
Quantitative: continuous variation (height, weight, yield) — many loci each contributing a small effect.
Phenotypic variance: V_P = V_G + V_E + V_GxE (+ covariance terms). Genetic variance (V_G) includes additive, dominance, and epistatic components.
popup.mnemonic: VP = VG + VE: Phenotypic variance = Genetic + Environmental. Add interaction term V_GxE if relevant.

flashcard.front: Why do quantitative (polygenic) traits show a continuous bell-curve distribution?
flashcard.back: Quantitative traits are controlled by MANY genes, each with a small additive effect. When many independent small contributions are added (multi-locus segregation), the central limit theorem produces a continuous, approximately normal distribution. Environmental variation adds further to the continuous spread. This contrasts with Mendelian traits controlled by 1-2 loci, which produce discrete categories.

quiz.question: The equation V_P = V_G + V_E describes:
quiz.correct: Decomposition of phenotypic variance into genetic and environmental components
distractors: Total mutation rate in a population
The fitness of each genotype
The rate of genetic drift in small populations

visual.mnemonic: Separate genes, environment, and their interaction.
visual.trap: VA (additive genetic variance) is the component that RESPONDS to selection. Dominance and epistatic variance do not transmit as predictably across generations.

v2.definition: Quantitative traits show continuous variation produced by many genes plus environment, unlike Mendelian discrete categorical traits.

mnemonic.explanation: Adding many small additive effects produces normal distributions, central limit theorem applied to genetics.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Continuous traits aren't genetic.' Continuous variation comes from MANY genes acting together plus environment, still heritable.

actions.quizPrompt: Why do polygenic traits produce bell-curve distributions?
actions.flashcardBack: Quantitative equals many genes continuous; Mendelian equals single gene discrete.

sections.label: Slide 30: Response to selection (R)
sections.body:
Response to selection (R)

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-6.dropbox.com/t/0/AAANHe0RJiYKbeBAnEcIEl3AzvrdJnUWpc9OL50LM0R9gw/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1360515600/0/2/08.04_fmt.jpeg/KyV9T66XjFQIQbqku9cU9t5MH3TRjGQw6jmnF_kxEkU?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

examples: Broad-sense heritability (H²) = VG / VP.
Narrow-sense heritability (h²) = VA / VP — the proportion of phenotypic variance due to ADDITIVE genetic effects.
Breeders equation: R = h² × S, where R = response to selection, S = selection differential.
Parent-offspring regression: slope estimates h² directly.
Stabilizing selection (human birth weight), disruptive selection (oil content of corn), directional selection (beak size).
warnings: High h² does NOT mean the trait is "genetic" in common speech — it means phenotypic variance IN THAT POPULATION IN THAT ENVIRONMENT is mostly due to additive genetic variance.
Heritability is population- and environment-specific.
popup.mnemonic: R = h² × S. Big h² + big S → big response. Any zero → no response.

flashcard.front: State the breeders equation, define each term, and explain what determines the response to selection.
flashcard.back: BREEDERS EQUATION: R = h² × S. R = RESPONSE to selection (change in mean phenotype from parent generation to offspring generation). h² = NARROW-SENSE HERITABILITY = V_A / V_P (proportion of phenotypic variance due to additive genetic variance). S = SELECTION DIFFERENTIAL = mean phenotype of breeding individuals minus mean of whole population. The response depends on BOTH h² (how much heritable variation there is) AND S (how strong selection is). If h² is zero, NO response even with strong selection. If S is zero (no selection), NO response even with high h².

quiz.question: In a population with h² = 0.5 for body size, a selection differential of S = 10 g is imposed. What is the predicted response R in offspring?
distractors: 10 g (no heritability correction)
0.5 g (only additive variance matters)

visual.mnemonic: R = h² × S. Multiplicative — any zero = no response.
visual.trap: h² is specific to a population and environment. Changing the environment changes V_E and therefore h².

v2.definition: The breeder's equation R equals h squared times S predicts evolutionary response from heritability and the selection differential.

mnemonic.explanation: Response to selection equals heritability times selection differential, the breeder's recipe for predictable change.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'High heritability means genes determine the trait.' Heritability measures variation explained genetically WITHIN one specific population.

actions.quizPrompt: Apply R equals h squared S to predict beak depth response under drought.
actions.flashcardBack: R equals h squared times S (response equals heritability times selection differential).

The most iconic response to Chaoborus is the development of defensive structures on the exoskeleton.
Neck Teeth: In species like Daphnia pulex, juveniles develop small, sharp protrusions on the back of the head (the "neck").
The Function: Chaoborus larvae are "gape-limited" ambush predators that catch prey using specialized antennae and then swallow them. Neck teeth make the Daphnia harder to grasp and significantly increase the chance that the larva will fumble and release the prey.

Interpretation of results

sections.label: Slide 45: Take home points
sections.body:
Take home points
Quantitative traits are normally distributed, and are influenced by alleles at multiple loci

Phenotypic variation is due to genes and the environment

Additive genetic variation is heritable

Directional, stabilizing and disruptive selection have different effects on the distribution of phenotypes

The response to selection can be predicted using heritability estimates and selection differentials

Phenotypic plasticity itself has a heritable genetic basis

VISUALS: None.


###############################################

examples: Reaction norm: a plot showing the phenotype of one genotype across an environmental gradient.
Plasticity types: continuous (reaction norm) vs discrete (polyphenism — e.g., seasonal coat color, caste in social insects).
G × E interaction: different genotypes have different reaction-norm shapes. Detect by common-garden experiments.
Common garden: grow multiple genotypes in the same environment to separate genetic from environmental effects.
popup.mnemonic: Plastic vs Genetic: Common garden test answers "is the difference genetic or environmental?"

flashcard.front: What is a reaction norm, and how does a common garden experiment distinguish genetic from environmental contributions to phenotype?
flashcard.back: A REACTION NORM is the relationship between a genotype's phenotype and an environmental variable — plotted as a line showing how the same genotype expresses different phenotypes in different environments. A COMMON GARDEN experiment grows multiple genotypes (populations, sibships, clones) in a SHARED environment, removing environmental differences as an explanation. Any remaining phenotypic differences must be GENETIC. A reciprocal transplant (growing each genotype in multiple environments) can reveal G × E interactions — genotypes may differ in HOW they respond to environment, not just their mean.

quiz.question: Two plant populations from high and low elevations are grown together in a single common garden at sea level. The populations still differ in growth rate. What does this show?
quiz.correct: The difference is at least partly genetic — environment alone cannot explain it
distractors: The difference must be entirely environmental
The two populations are the same species
Plasticity is absent in these populations

visual.mnemonic: Crossing lines = G × E.
visual.trap: Plasticity itself is a heritable trait — selection can shape reaction norms. But plasticity ≠ adaptation.

v2.definition: Reaction norms map how a single genotype produces different phenotypes across environments, phenotypic plasticity made visible.

mnemonic.explanation: One genotype performs different phenotypes depending on the environmental stage it lives on.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Plasticity is not adaptive.' Plasticity itself can BE the adaptation when environments fluctuate predictably between states.

actions.quizPrompt: How can phenotypic plasticity itself be adaptive?
actions.flashcardBack: Genotype's phenotype profile across environments (plasticity graph).

sections.body:
Response to selection (R)
Is beak size the direct target of selection?
Observational vs Experimental studies

Observational approach: draw inferences about cause and effect using correlations

Experimental approach: Provide direct insight about cause and effect when independent variable is manipulated

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-6.dropbox.com/t/0/AAANHe0RJiYKbeBAnEcIEl3AzvrdJnUWpc9OL50LM0R9gw/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1360515600/0/2/08.04_fmt.jpeg/KyV9T66XjFQIQbqku9cU9t5MH3TRjGQw6jmnF_kxEkU?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

popup.mnemonic: MOD: Measure → Observe differential success → Document inheritance.

flashcard.front: Why are empirical studies of natural selection in wild populations so difficult, and what three kinds of evidence must be gathered to prove selection is occurring?
flashcard.back: Field selection studies are difficult because: (1) populations are large and individuals must be marked and tracked; (2) the environmental variable causing selection must be identified and often experimentally manipulated; (3) heritability of the trait must be shown separately. To prove selection, you must show: (1) phenotypic VARIATION in the trait, (2) differential FITNESS (survival or reproduction) correlated with the trait, (3) HERITABILITY of the trait. Only then can you predict an evolutionary response (R = h² × S).

quiz.question: Which of these alone is NOT sufficient evidence that natural selection is occurring on a trait?
quiz.correct: Observing phenotypic variation in the population
distractors: Heritable differences in fitness correlated with the trait
Measured change in mean phenotype across generations matching breeders equation
Experimental manipulation of the trait causing altered fitness

visual.description: Proving natural selection in the field
visual.mnemonic: VFHP: Variation, Fitness, Heritability, Prediction.
visual.trap: Showing a phenotype-fitness correlation alone is NOT proof of selection — the correlation could be due to confounded environmental effects.

v2.definition: Empirical selection studies measure trait variation, fitness differences, and heritability in wild populations to confirm evolution.

mnemonic.explanation: Confirm variation, prove heritability, measure fitness differences, track changes, the empirical recipe for selection.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Selection requires controlled lab conditions.' Most classic selection studies are fieldwork in wild populations across decades.

actions.quizPrompt: What four pieces of evidence demonstrate selection in the wild?
actions.flashcardBack: Variation, heritability, fitness differences, generational change measured in nature.

nodes.title: Beach Mice: Parallel Evolution of Camouflage

sections.label: Slide 7: Beach mice (Peromyscus polionotus)
sections.body:
Beach mice (Peromyscus polionotus)

Pie charts: Frequency of light vs dark alleles
Gulf Coast white fur
Single amino acid mutation in Melanocortin receptor 1 gene (Mc1r) – stimulates production of melanin
Second mutation increased Agouti gene expression – Interferes with signaling of Mc1r
Atlantic populations lack white alleles – white color due to other genes
Steiner, C.C., Römpler, H., Boettger, L.M., Schöneberg, T. and Hoekstra, H.E., 2009. The genetic basis of phenotypic convergence in beach mice: similar pigment patterns but different genes. Molecular Biology and Evolution, 26(1), pp.35-45.

sections.label: Slide 8: Mainland population gave rise to both beach populations
sections.body:
Mainland population gave rise to both beach populations

White coat color evolved twice

Color arose via two different genetic changes in seemingly adaptive ways

Is white color really favored?
How can we test this?

sections.body:
Manipulate phenotypes
Common garden experiment
Measure predation on clay models

Less predation when fur color matches substrate
Vignieri, S.N., Larson, J.G. and Hoekstra, H.E., 2010. The selective advantage of crypsis in mice. Evolution, 64(7), pp.2153-2158.

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-1.dropbox.com/t/0/AAA_e1MKxAgOjooL3qP1_9kFBu_rMRA_O_CkQh7ZOHe-XQ/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1360515600/0/2/08.06_fmt.jpeg/ibnlQusn0_4AYDd--j2RPsb-Q-FprbK-_rU249UgHTY?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

sections.label: Slide 10: Two independent origins of white color in mice
sections.body:
Two independent origins of white color in mice
But what about repeated patterns in other organisms inhabiting similar environments?
White Sands National Monument, New Mexico

Melanocortin receptor 1 gene (Mc1r)
All have different mutations at this gene
White allele is dominant in S. undulatus
But white allele is recessive in A. inornata
White allele is more widespread in S. undulatus

SPEAKER NOTES: Independent and repeated patterns provide compelling evidence

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: http://farm3.static.flickr.com/2414/2258775727_416448b1fc.jpg)
Image 2: File "Picture6.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.eattrainlive.com/wp-content/uploads/2012/11/White-Sands-New-Mexico.jpg)
Image 3: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://scienceblogs.com/notrocketscience/wp-content/blogs.dir/474/files/2012/04/i-bbd454b7d527f8efca90d907436c8676-Lizards.jpg)

examples: Dark mainland Peromyscus polionotus populations gave rise to two independent light-colored beach populations.
Beach populations match the light sand habitat (cryptic coloration).
Hoekstra et al.: a single amino acid change in the Mc1r gene causes the light coat color.
The Mc1r mutation is different in each beach population — so camouflage evolved TWICE independently from the same starting genetic variation (parallel evolution at the gene level).
popup.mnemonic: MMMP: Mc1r mutation → Melanin loss → Match sand → Predation protection.

flashcard.front: How does the beach mouse study illustrate the interaction between genetics, phenotype, environment, and selection?
flashcard.back: MAINLAND Peromyscus polionotus have dark fur, matching forest soil. Two BEACH populations (Atlantic and Gulf coasts) have light fur, matching white sand. GENETICS: Hoekstra and colleagues identified a single amino acid change in the Mc1r gene causes the light color in both populations — but the exact mutation differs between the two beach populations. PHENOTYPE: Light fur is cryptic on sand. ENVIRONMENT: Visual predators (birds, mammals) hunt by sight. SELECTION: Predators preferentially eat mice that contrast with the substrate. Barrett & Hoekstra showed this experimentally using plasticine mouse models placed on light and dark substrates — the mismatched models were attacked at higher rates. This case study proves all four ingredients of natural selection in a single system AND shows parallel evolution.

quiz.question: Beach mice on the Atlantic and Gulf coasts have similar light coat colors but different Mc1r mutations. This is an example of:
quiz.correct: Parallel evolution — same phenotypic outcome from independent genetic changes
distractors: Convergent evolution between different species
Genetic drift eliminating variation
Hybridization between populations

visual.mnemonic: Two beaches, two mutations, one solution.
visual.trap: Parallel ≠ convergent. Parallel evolution = same gene in related lineages. Convergent = different genes or lineages arriving at similar phenotypes.

v2.definition: Beach mice evolved light coats independently in Florida and Alabama through Mc1r mutations, classic parallel evolution under predator selection.

mnemonic.explanation: Florida's gulf coast and Alabama mice evolved pale coats from different Mc1r mutations, identical adaptive phenotype.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Parallel evolution means identical mutations.' Parallel means same TRAIT from different mutations, convergence at the phenotype level only.

actions.quizPrompt: Why are beach mice considered an example of parallel evolution?
actions.flashcardFront: What gene drives beach mouse coat color?
actions.flashcardBack: Mc1r, with different mutations producing the same pale phenotype on each coast.

examples: In many reptiles (turtles, crocodilians), egg incubation temperature determines offspring sex.
Different species have different temperature-sex curves — some favor females at high temps, others at low temps.
Charnov-Bull model: TSD is adaptive when a temperature affects male and female fitness differently.
Climate change concern: rising temperatures can skew sex ratios, threatening TSD species.
warnings: TSD is a derived trait, not an ancestral one — it evolved multiple times independently.
popup.mnemonic: Charnov-Bull: TSD is adaptive when temperature ≠ sex × fitness interaction differs.

flashcard.front: Under what conditions is temperature-dependent sex determination (TSD) expected to be adaptive, according to the Charnov-Bull model?
flashcard.back: The CHARNOV-BULL model predicts TSD is adaptive when the environment (temperature) experienced during development differentially affects the fitness of males versus females. If a certain temperature makes males successful but females unsuccessful (or vice versa), producing the sex that benefits more at that temperature maximizes parental fitness. For example, if warmer nest temperatures produce larger offspring and female reproductive success depends on body size more than male success, then warm-temperature eggs should develop as females. Climate change can make TSD maladaptive if temperatures exceed the historical range.

quiz.question: Which of the following is the greatest conservation threat posed by temperature-dependent sex determination in sea turtles?
quiz.correct: Rising global temperatures may skew sex ratios so strongly that breeding populations cannot be sustained
distractors: Genetic drift will eliminate TSD
Predators will preferentially eat one sex
Sea turtles will lose the TSD gene entirely

visual.mnemonic: Three temperature-sex curve shapes across reptiles.
visual.trap: TSD is NOT a primitive trait — it evolved multiple times and can be lost again. Genetic sex determination is ancestral in most vertebrates.

v2.definition: Temperature-dependent sex determination ties offspring sex to nest temperature, an evolved plastic response to environmental cues.

mnemonic.explanation: Many turtles produce females at warm nest temperatures and males at cool temperatures, plasticity tuned by climate.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'TSD is a primitive flaw.' TSD is an EVOLVED adaptation: it works when temperature predicts which sex benefits more.

actions.quizPrompt: Why is temperature-dependent sex determination considered adaptive plasticity?
actions.flashcardBack: The threshold temperature producing a 1:1 sex ratio (species specific).

nodes.title: Fitness Landscapes
nodes.subtitle: Wright's metaphor for evolutionary dynamics (Lec 7 slides 23-26)

popup.heading: Lecture 7 — Fitness Landscape

sections.label: Slide 23: Fitness landscape
sections.body:
Fitness landscape
or
Adaptive landscape

Fitness
Trait 2
Trait 1
Used to visualize selection surfaces for combinations of traits

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://cairnarvon.rotahall.org/pics/complex.png)

sections.label: Slide 24: Metaphor for thinking about how populations change
sections.body:
Metaphor for thinking about how populations change

Different combinations of traits can impact fitness in different ways
Fitness peaks: regions of high fitness, or trait combinations with high fitness
Fitness valleys: regions of low fitness, or trait combinations with low fitness
Natural selection sorts through existing variation and follows a path uphill (but it’s tough to move back down)
Genetic drift can cause traits to move through valleys over to new peaks

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture1.jpg" (alt text: "img018.jpg")

sections.label: Slide 25: Combination of morphology and behavior
sections.body:
Combination of morphology and behavior
What type of selection is this an example of?

Northwestern garter snake
Morphology
Checkered vs striped pattern
Escape behavior
Sprint or reversal

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture6.jpg" (alt text: "img018.jpg")
Image 2: File "Picture4.jpg" (alt text: "img018.jpg")

sections.label: Slide 26: Take home points
sections.body:
Take home points
Single gene mutations can produce adaptive phenotypes (e.g., beach mice)
Experimental approaches can determine direct target of selection (e.g., beach mice)
Plasticity can increase fitness and is shaped by natural selection (e.g., TSD)
Multiple pressures can operate in opposing directions and affect the form of selection (e.g., gallfly)
Combinations of traits can have different fitness consequences (visualized with fitness landscapes)

VISUALS: None.


###############################################

examples: Sewall Wright (1932): fitness landscape — a metaphorical surface where x,y axes are genotypes/traits and z (height) is fitness.
Adaptive peaks: high-fitness genotype combinations.
Valleys: low-fitness genotype combinations between peaks.
Populations tend to climb local peaks via natural selection, but may get stuck on suboptimal peaks (can't cross valleys).
Drift or gene flow can help populations cross valleys and reach higher peaks.
popup.mnemonic: Peaks & Valleys: Selection → local peak; Drift/flow → cross valley to higher peak.

flashcard.front: What is a fitness landscape and why can selection alone get "stuck" on a local peak?
flashcard.back: A FITNESS LANDSCAPE (Wright 1932) is a metaphorical surface where axes represent allele combinations (or trait values) and the height at each point is the fitness of that combination. Populations evolve by "climbing" up the landscape — selection pushes them toward higher fitness. However, natural selection only moves a population LOCALLY UPHILL. If a population reaches a LOCAL PEAK, any small change reduces fitness — selection prevents the population from leaving. To reach a HIGHER peak across a valley, the population must temporarily pass through lower-fitness states, which selection opposes. GENETIC DRIFT or GENE FLOW can help populations cross valleys by randomly moving them off the current peak — a classic example of how drift and flow can actually accelerate adaptation under some conditions.

quiz.question: A population is stuck on a local fitness peak while a much higher peak exists across a fitness valley. Which process is most likely to help the population move to the higher peak?
quiz.correct: Genetic drift temporarily pushing the population off the local peak
distractors: Stronger stabilizing selection
Elimination of all mutations
Reduced population size to zero

visual.mnemonic: Selection is blind to height beyond its immediate neighborhood.
visual.trap: Real fitness landscapes are high-dimensional (thousands of genes). 2D visualizations are radical simplifications.

v2.definition: Fitness landscapes plot fitness as elevation across trait space, Sewall Wright's metaphor for visualizing evolutionary trajectories.

mnemonic.explanation: Selection pushes populations uphill toward local fitness peaks but cannot cross deep adaptive valleys.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Selection always reaches the highest peak.' Selection only climbs LOCAL peaks, populations get stuck on suboptimal ridges.

actions.quizPrompt: Why might natural selection fail to find the global fitness optimum?
actions.flashcardBack: Sewall Wright, in 1932, as a metaphor for evolutionary dynamics.

nodes.title: Adaptations: Trait vs Process
nodes.subtitle: Defining adaptation and novel traits (Lec 8 slides 1-6)

popup.heading: Lecture 8 — Complex Adaptations

sections.label: Slide 1: Complex adaptations
sections.body:
Complex adaptations
Dr. Travis Robbins
Evolution
BIOL 4340
University of Nebraska Omaha

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture3.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 2: Complex adaptations
sections.body:
Complex adaptations
Lecture goals

Explain how mutations in a regulatory network may affect development

Explain how changes in the timing and location of the expression of developmental genes can affect the appearance of complex traits

Understand some of the important steps in the evolution of the vertebrate eye

sections.label: Slide 3: All organisms are adapted to the environment they live in
sections.body:
All organisms are adapted to the environment they live in
There are no ‘higher’ or ‘lower’ species

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture6.jpg" (originally from URL: http://2.bp.blogspot.com/-MEutFPWwgCU/TclMECMm1AI/AAAAAAAAABw/YUi0bZuvx_4/s1600/cactus.jpg)
Image 2: File "Picture8.jpg" (originally from URL: http://imagesfromtheedge.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2010/04/dsc_8356.jpg)
Image 3: File "Picture10.jpg" (alt text: "Lampsilis ovata - pocketbook mussel")
Image 4: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: http://visalakshiramani.files.wordpress.com/2010/08/13.jpg?w=640)
Image 5: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://1.bp.blogspot.com/-t8_dAACEyks/Tav0FwO8AvI/AAAAAAAAHdY/Hgczl92-nh0/s1600/Uroplatus%2Bphantasticus%252C%2Bthe%2BSatanic%2BLeaf%2BTailed%2BGecko-774676.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 4: Adaptation: viewed as both a trait and a process
sections.body:
Adaptation: viewed as both a trait and a process
As a trait
Inherited aspects of an individual that allow it to outcompete other members of a population that lack the trait (or have a different variant of the trait)

Traits that have evolved through natural selection
As a process
Process that leads to acquiring a fitness-enhancing trait (i.e., an adaptive trait or an adaptation)

Contributes to the fitness of individuals

sections.label: Slide 5: Novel traits: Traits that arise de novo (not inherited from an ancestor) within a lineage and have no
sections.body:
Novel traits: Traits that arise de novo (not inherited from an ancestor) within a lineage and have no obvious counterparts in related lineages
How do novel traits arise in the first place?

“To suppose that the eye, with all its inimitable contrivances for adjusting the focus to different distances, for admitting different amounts of light, and for the correction of spherical and chromatic aberration, could have been formed by natural selection, seems, I freely confess, absurd in the highest possible degree.”
		(Darwin 1859)

But he provides a plausible explanation
		…more on that later
Need right kind of cells (photoreceptors)
Right kind of structures
Right kind of wiring to the brain
Not just a single trait, but a suite of traits

SPEAKER NOTES: Need right kind of cells (photoreceptors)
Right kind of structures
Right kind of wiring to the brain

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg"
Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://2.bp.blogspot.com/_1fVGf0SHO70/TU5EOUczJSI/AAAAAAAAAB4/YWcpoja3-6g/s1600/eye.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 6: Complex traits or adaptations
sections.body:
Complex traits or adaptations
Complex adaptation: Suites of co-expressed traits that together experience selection for a common function.

Phenotypes that are influenced by many environmental and genetic factors

Multiple components must be expressed together for the trait to function

Often multiple mutations are needed to achieve a fitness advantage

Typically a gradual step-by-step process

examples: Adaptation as TRAIT: a heritable feature that increases fitness in a particular environment.
Adaptation as PROCESS: the ongoing evolutionary change driven by natural selection.
Novel traits: arise de novo (not inherited) — e.g., limbs in tetrapods, wings in birds.
Complex traits: require coordination of multiple genes and developmental modules.
popup.mnemonic: Trait vs Process: the word "adaptation" carries both meanings — always specify which.

flashcard.front: What is the difference between "adaptation" as a trait and "adaptation" as a process?
flashcard.back: As a TRAIT (noun): an adaptation is a specific heritable feature shaped by past selection that increases fitness — e.g., the woodpecker's long tongue, the giraffe's neck, antifreeze proteins in Antarctic fish. As a PROCESS (verb): adaptation is the ongoing evolutionary mechanism by which populations become better matched to their environment through natural selection. These two meanings are related but distinct: the process produces the traits. In scientific papers, careful writers specify which they mean.

quiz.question: Which statement best describes a "novel trait" in evolution?
quiz.correct: A trait that arises de novo and is not directly inherited from an ancestor
distractors: Any new mutation that appears in a population
A trait that never existed before life began
A trait unique to a single individual

visual.mnemonic: Trait = what. Process = how.
visual.trap: Not every trait is an adaptation. Some are byproducts of other adaptations, some are vestigial, some are selectively neutral.

v2.definition: Adaptation is both a heritable trait increasing fitness and the historical process producing such traits.

mnemonic.explanation: A wing IS an adaptation (noun) and wings BECOME adaptations through selection (verb), one word, two meanings.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Every trait is an adaptation.' Some traits are byproducts, vestiges, or genetic drift outcomes, not adaptations.

actions.quizPrompt: Distinguish trait adaptation from process adaptation.
actions.flashcardBack: A fitness-enhancing trait, AND the process that produced it.

nodes.title: Evo-Devo & Regulatory Networks
nodes.subtitle: How gene networks build body plans (Lec 8 slides 7-22)

popup.heading: Lecture 8 — Evolutionary Developmental Biology

sections.label: Slide 7: Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo Devo):
sections.body:
Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo Devo):
Fusion of developmental biology with evolutionary biology
Pre-Darwinian ideas
German anatomist J.F. Meckel (1781-1833): developmental stages paralleled evolutionary history of a species.
Earnst Hackel (1834-1919): Theory of recapitulation-Ontogeny is a precise and compressed recapitulation of phylogeny

The developmental progress of an organism (ontogeny) replays (recapitulation) its evolutionary history (phylogeny)
Evolutionary processes produced new species by tacking on something new and novel to the terminal part of development of the ancestral species

SPEAKER NOTES: Pre-Darwinian ideas - Developmental stages of a particular species step through all the animal species that came before it

Those traits that appear early in development are resistant to evolutionary change

Hackel – evolution produced a new species by tacking something new on the terminal part of development of the ancestral species

The idea is not accepted today: Problem is that it implies progressive linear evolution, rather than a branching pattern

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture8.jpg" (alt text: "img020.jpg")

sections.label: Slide 8: Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo Devo)
sections.body:
Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo Devo)

A lot of work in the field focused on Heterochrony: changes in the rate and timing of development

Adults with juvenile traits
With modern molecular techniques, we now see how changes in genes or gene expression are responsible for the development of new traits
By integrating molecular biology, modern Evo Devo provides many new insights in the evolution of novel structures

SPEAKER NOTES: Interested in whether the time at which a trait was first expressed in a given species occurred at a different time in its ancestor

Trait in an adult of an ancestral species, is now seen in the juvenile stage in the derived species

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture8.jpg" (originally from URL: http://1.bp.blogspot.com/-jB08lXehIKk/TutZmWvRoaI/AAAAAAAACks/DpGGyblyddI/s1600/Axolotl8.jpg)
Image 2: File "Picture10.jpg" (alt text: "File:Aphid-giving-birth.jpg")

sections.label: Slide 9: Complex traits
sections.body:
Complex traits
Proteins can take on new functions
Sometimes new functions are selected against
But some proteins can serve more than one function at once
Promiscuous proteins: can carry out more than one function (e.g., enzymes that catalyze reactions of different substrates)
If environment changes, one function may become favored, and any mutations that increase the proteins performance will be selected for
e.g., enzymes that degrade organophosphates (pesticides) even though they naturally perform other functions (original function still maintained)

SPEAKER NOTES: Aharoni et al. 2005. Nature Genetics 37:73

For adaptations to arise, old proteins take on new functions

sections.label: Slide 10: Complex traits
sections.body:
Complex traits
Proteins can take on new functions
Sometimes new functions are selected against
But some proteins can serve more than one function at once
Can take on new functions via gene duplication
Paralogs: Homologous genes that arise by gene duplications
One copy performs original function, but other is free to evolve

Polyploids: 50-70% of flowering plants

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://encrypted-tbn2.gstatic.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcQSYv666lM-dcbG79o5_TgzGT0VWFkfC8S2Q34tj_oEl5ujm5ba)

sections.label: Slide 11: Complex traits
sections.body:
Complex traits
Proteins can take on new functions
Sometimes new functions are selected against
But some proteins can serve more than one function at once
Can take on new functions via gene duplication
Co-opt a particular gene/network for a new function via mutation
Gene recruitment: new function of gene due to mutation.
Expression in a new organ, and gene added to different network of genetic interactions.
Could reorganize entire regulatory network, resulting in a new and potentially adaptive trait

sections.label: Slide 12: Regulatory network: Systems of interacting genes, transcription factors, RNA, and other molecules
sections.body:
Regulatory network: Systems of interacting genes, transcription factors, RNA, and other molecules

Regulatory networks underlie complex traits
Function like biological circuits
Respond to signals with outputs
Outputs control gene activation
Often occurring during development
Hierarchy of genes that have cascading effects
Many genes are phylogenetically conserved
i.e., found across distantly related species

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture5.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 13: Small changes in network lead to new complex traits
sections.body:
Small changes in network lead to new complex traits
Hox genes: group of genes that control the body plan of an embryo

Hox genes and the evolution of animal body plans (1:54)
Distalless (Dll) gene repressed in back segments
Homothorax (hth) gene repressed in front segments

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-2.dropbox.com/t/0/AAB9m7JkJDQvqIhCxU1yqHQSgDhdizEyvaLJXNx0tar69A/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1360713600/0/2/10.03_fmt.jpeg/-gXNloM5ZgarzNYORhH_bijLvVHeBGn_vPxzrttpkW4?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

sections.label: Slide 14: Small changes in network lead to new complex traits
sections.body:
Small changes in network lead to new complex traits
Hox genes: group of genes that control the body plan of an embryo

Same network of Hox genes governs development across all bilaterian animals
Common ancestor already had these body-patterning genes

sections.label: Slide 22: Genes for beak size in Galapagos finches
sections.body:
Genes for beak size in Galapagos finches
Changes in expression of just two proteins can produce large diversity in beaks
Helps explain how the finches adapted so quickly to different niches over past few million years

Developing beaks of embryos

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg"
Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-1.dropbox.com/t/0/AACZBpY1-kozZ8Xv84nt7AvHkbflhNdO6tkvbf2jJh792A/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1360782000/0/2/10.15_fmt.jpeg/j0vjG8ttcwnnvpq6-3DRRkn1wmWX6rcjmv84t0vhPDo?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

examples: Evo-devo: the study of how developmental processes evolve and how gene regulation shapes morphology.
Hox genes: conserved master regulators of body plan across animals. Small changes in Hox expression → large morphological changes.
Limb development: same gene network (Hox, Tbx, Sonic hedgehog) is used by all tetrapods to build limbs.
Galapagos finches: beak size differences are controlled by Bmp4 and calmodulin expression differences, not new genes.
popup.mnemonic: Small regulatory changes → Big morphological differences. Same toolkit, different deployment.

flashcard.front: Why do evolutionary biologists emphasize regulatory evolution over protein-coding evolution for explaining body plan differences?
flashcard.back: Most animals share the SAME toolkit of developmental genes — Hox, Pax, Tbx, BMP, Wnt, Sonic hedgehog. These genes are nearly identical in fruit flies, mice, and humans. Major morphological differences (legs vs. no legs, big beak vs. small beak, limbs vs. fins) arise mostly from CHANGES IN WHEN, WHERE, AND HOW MUCH these genes are expressed — not from new protein-coding genes. A single mutation in a cis-regulatory element can shift the domain of expression, producing large phenotypic effects. This is why "small changes in networks lead to new complex traits."

quiz.question: The striking morphological differences in beak size among Galápagos finches are produced primarily by:
quiz.correct: Differences in the expression levels of regulatory genes like Bmp4 and calmodulin during beak development
distractors: Completely different sets of genes in each species
New proteins that evolved only in finches
Environmental sculpting of beak bone after hatching

visual.mnemonic: Same genes, different expression = different species.
visual.trap: Coding sequence changes matter too (e.g., Mc1r in beach mice) but regulatory changes dominate for large morphological differences.

v2.definition: Evo-devo studies how regulatory gene networks build body plans, explaining major evolutionary novelty through developmental change.

mnemonic.explanation: Animals share the same developmental toolkit genes; regulatory rewiring builds wildly different body plans.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'New body plans need new genes.' New body plans usually come from REWIRING existing toolkit genes through regulatory mutations.

actions.quizPrompt: How does cis-regulatory mutation produce major morphological change?
actions.flashcardBack: How developmental gene networks evolve to produce body-plan novelty.

nodes.title: Eye Evolution: From Photoreceptors to Camera Eyes
nodes.subtitle: Classic complex trait case study (Lec 8 slides 23-30)

popup.heading: Lecture 8 — Evolution of the Eye

sections.label: Slide 23: Evolution of complex traits – the eye
sections.body:
Evolution of complex traits – the eye
“…could have been formed by natural selection, seems, I freely confess, absurd in the highest possible degree.”
“Yet reason tells me, that if numerous gradations from a perfect and complex eye to one very imperfect and simple, each grade being useful to its possessor, can be shown to exist…”

“…if further, the eye does vary ever so slightly, and the variations be inherited, which is certainly the case…”

“…and if any variation or modification in the organ be ever useful to an animal under changing conditions of life, then the difficulty of believing that a perfect and complex eye could be formed by natural selection, though insuperable by our imagination, can hardly be considered real.”              (Darwin 1859, 186-187)
We now have a much better picture of eye evolution

sections.label: Slide 24: Eye evolution
sections.body:
Eye evolution
A complex trait with many integrated components

1) Photoreceptive cells
contain right proteins that convert a photon into a chemical signal

2) Correct structure
spherical structures that form images, lenses that focus light

3) Correct wiring to the brain
Eyes evolved through a series of gradual steps

sections.label: Slide 25: Photoreceptive cells
sections.body:
Photoreceptive cells

Light is captured by photoreceptor cells
Opsin: protein on the retina that triggers a reaction when struck by light
Crystallins: proteins that make lens
Bilaterians – 3 primary types of opsins
Ciliary opsin (C-opsins)
Rhabdomeric opsin (R-opsins)
RGR/Go (Rhodopsin)
Cnidarians
Also have opsin genes, slightly different proteins

SPEAKER NOTES: When light enters our eye it hits a protein call opsin. Sends a message to our brain

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture3.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 26: The original opsin gene was duplicated twice early in animals
sections.body:
The original opsin gene was duplicated twice early in animals

Most eukaryotes have G protein-coupled receptors, also known as Serpentine proteins
All animals
Fungi
Protozoans
Plants

Useful in low light conditions (rhodopsin)

SPEAKER NOTES: Opsins belong to the serpentine family of proteins

sections.label: Slide 27: Crystallins: Transparent proteins that make up the lens
sections.body:
Crystallins: Transparent proteins that make up the lens
Evolved from recruited genes
Co-opted from a heat-shock protein

Mutation caused it to be expressed in the vertebrate eye

Crystallins had the right optical properties for bending light

Enable a better focus of an image

sections.body:

Eye evolution in vertebrates

Image 3: File "Picture5.jpg" (originally from URL: http://luc.edu/biology/111/tuni.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 29: Almost all states of eye evolution are in mollusks
sections.body:
Almost all states of eye evolution are in mollusks

Figure 3.29 in B&D
Two independent origins of complex eyes with a lens in cephalopods and gastropods

Detects light vs dark
Detects direction of light
Conch snail

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (alt text: "img022.jpg")
Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://t1.gstatic.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcSk6EPPnoe49SYUY9saw41nNvd3AMyvvl5cllf1yZInSVNtiZTTeFq7O5Kz)
Image 3: File "Picture2.jpg" (alt text: "I was today years old when I learned that Conch snails have eyes : r/interestingasfuck")

sections.label: Slide 30: Complex traits have flaws
sections.body:
Complex traits have flaws
Complex traits are a product of natural selection

They are ‘built’ from existing variation (not from scratch)

Natural selection does not result in perfection

Blind spot in the vertebrate eye

SPEAKER NOTES: Optic nerve fibers pass through the retina and out of the eye

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture3.jpg"

examples: Photoreceptive cells were the earliest step — a single opsin molecule in a light-sensitive cell.
Opsin gene was duplicated twice early in animal evolution, creating different light-sensitivity classes.
Crystallins: transparent proteins that make up the lens — many are recruited from pre-existing enzymes (e.g., lactate dehydrogenase in some species).
Eye stages: simple photoreceptor patch → pit → pinhole → lensed camera eye.
Mollusks alone show ALL intermediate eye stages today — flat eyespots (limpets), pit eyes (abalone), pinhole eyes (Nautilus), lensed eyes (squid, octopus).
popup.mnemonic: From patch to camera: each stage provides its own fitness advantage. No "half eye" problem.

flashcard.front: How does eye evolution refute the creationist claim of "irreducible complexity" for complex organs?
flashcard.back: The argument from irreducible complexity claims complex organs like the eye cannot evolve because "half an eye" is useless. However, eye evolution shows INTERMEDIATE stages are each FUNCTIONAL and BENEFICIAL: (1) A photoreceptive patch detects light vs. dark (useful for circadian rhythm or shadow warning). (2) A cupped patch gives directional information. (3) A deepened cup becomes a pinhole, giving crude image formation. (4) A lens sharpens the image. All these stages exist TODAY in different mollusks (flatworms, limpets, abalone, Nautilus, squid) — providing living evidence of each intermediate. Crystallins (lens proteins) were recruited from pre-existing enzymes via gene duplication and co-option, not invented from scratch.

quiz.question: Why is the evolution of the eye often used to refute "irreducible complexity" arguments?
quiz.correct: Every intermediate stage of eye evolution (patch, pit, pinhole, lensed) is functional and provides fitness benefit
distractors: The eye evolved in a single mutational step
Only vertebrates have eyes
Eye evolution required miraculous intervention

visual.mnemonic: Patch → Pit → Pinhole → Lens → Camera.
visual.trap: Vertebrate and cephalopod camera eyes are CONVERGENT (not homologous as complete organs) — they share the opsin toolkit but arrived at the camera design independently.

v2.definition: Eyes evolved gradually from photosensitive patches through cup, pinhole, and lens stages, with each stage adaptive on its own.

mnemonic.hook: Patch to cup to pinhole to lens
mnemonic.explanation: Eye evolution proceeds through four functional stages, each one selectable and useful in its own right.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Half an eye is useless.' Every intermediate stage detected light and conferred a fitness advantage over having no eye.

actions.quizPrompt: Walk through the four functional stages of eye evolution.
actions.flashcardFront: Master gene shared by all eyes?
actions.flashcardBack: Pax6, conserved across animals from flies to humans.

nodes.title: Imperfect Adaptations & Antagonistic Pleiotropy
nodes.subtitle: Why organisms are not optimally designed (Lec 8 slides 31-35)

popup.heading: Lecture 8 — Flaws in Complex Adaptations

sections.label: Slide 31: Antagonistic pleiotropy
sections.body:
Antagonistic pleiotropy

Pleiotropy is when a single gene influences more than one trait
Evolutionary constraints
Hox genes that code for different numbers of cervical vertebrae have fitness costs
Linked to rapid metabolism
Linked to pediatric cancers

Slow metabolism may allow for different number of cervical vertebrae
Manatees (6 cv)
Sloths (5,6,8,9 cv)

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 32: Take home points
sections.body:
Take home points
Novel complex traits typically evolve gradually with selection acting on several mutations.

Modern Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo Devo) has given insights into how complex traits arise.

Proteins can take on new functions in many ways to give rise to new traits.

Hox genes are conserved across taxa, and slight variation in Hox genes or gene networks can dramatically change phenotypes.

The vertebrate eye evolved through several steps, but this complex trait is not perfect.

sections.label: Slide 33: Complex adaptations
sections.body:
Complex adaptations
The End
Lecture goals

Explain how mutations in a regulatory network  may affect development

Explain how changes in the timing and location of the expression of developmental genes can affect the appearance of complex traits

Understand some of the important steps in the evolution of the vertebrate eye
David Dancey-Wood, Wildlife Artist

sections.label: Slide 35: Brine shrimp
sections.body:
Brine shrimp

Image 9: File "Picture7.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.nhm.ac.uk/resources-rx/images/1014/onychophora-unidentified_97784_1.jpg)


###############################################

examples: Vertebrate retina: photoreceptors face AWAY from incoming light; blood vessels and optic nerve pass in front of the retina, creating a blind spot. Cephalopod retinas do NOT have this flaw.
Human spine: evolved from a quadruped's horizontal backbone — bipedalism causes chronic back pain.
Antagonistic pleiotropy: a gene that increases early reproduction but shortens lifespan (or causes disease later) will be favored because reproduction happens first.
warnings: Evolution cannot design from scratch — it can only modify what already exists. This "tinkering" produces jury-rigged solutions, not engineered ones.
popup.mnemonic: AP + Tinkering = Flaws. Evolution optimizes locally, not globally.

flashcard.front: What is antagonistic pleiotropy and how does it explain why aging and age-related diseases persist in human populations?
flashcard.back: ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY: one gene affects multiple traits (pleiotropy), with OPPOSING (antagonistic) fitness effects — beneficial for one trait but harmful for another. A classic example: a gene allele that INCREASES REPRODUCTIVE SUCCESS in young adulthood but CAUSES DISEASE or death in old age. Because reproduction happens first, the early benefit outweighs the late cost in terms of passing the allele on — so selection FAVORS the allele. This explains why humans suffer age-related diseases (Alzheimer's, cancer, heart disease): alleles contributing to these conditions in old age were (and are) maintained because they improved reproductive success earlier in life. This is why "why do organisms age" has a clear evolutionary answer: selection is weak on traits expressed after reproduction.

quiz.question: The vertebrate retina has a blind spot because photoreceptors face away from incoming light and the optic nerve passes in front. This is best explained as:
quiz.correct: Evolutionary tinkering — constrained by ancestral developmental pathways, not optimal design
distractors: Optimal design for human vision
A necessary consequence of using opsins
A sign that vision evolved recently

visual.mnemonic: Design ≠ Evolution. Tinkering leaves traces.
visual.trap: Flaws DO NOT refute evolution — they support it. They show the jury-rigged nature of descent with modification.

v2.definition: Antagonistic pleiotropy and developmental constraints produce imperfect adaptations because selection cannot redesign organisms from scratch.

mnemonic.explanation: Selection can only modify existing structures, never start over, so flaws like the human spine persist.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Natural selection produces optimal designs.' Selection produces COMPROMISES because of trade-offs, history, and pleiotropy.

actions.quizPrompt: Why is the human back not perfectly designed for upright walking?
actions.flashcardBack: One gene improving one trait while harming another (trade off).

nodes.title: Coevolution: Species as Selective Agents
nodes.subtitle: When evolution of one species drives evolution of another (Lec 9 slides 1-5)

popup.heading: Lecture 9 — Coevolution Introduction

sections.label: Slide 1: Coevolution
sections.body:
Coevolution

Dr. Travis Robbins
Evolution
BIOL 4340
University of Nebraska Omaha

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 2: Coevolution
sections.body:
Coevolution
Lecture goals
To describe the evolutionary dynamics of different types of species interactions

To understand how positive and negative frequency-dependent selection result in mutualism and antagonism

To compare and contrast Batesian and Mullerian mimicry and their evolutionary dynamics

To explain the evolution of the mitochondria

sections.label: Slide 3: Interactions among species
sections.body:
Interactions among species
Species interactions drive evolutionary processes

Competition
Plant-herbivore relationships
Predator-prey relationships
Host-parasite interactions
Some interactions benefit all species involved

SPEAKER NOTES: Fungi deliver minerals and nutrients, and plants provide organic carbon to the fungus

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture5.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 4: Coevolution
sections.body:
Coevolution
Reciprocal evolutionary change between interacting species, driven by natural selection

Natural selection on one species causes selection to operate in new ways on another species

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://t0.gstatic.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcTpebTptQOGLsSeQMa_BKJ619WXfVdETnxRAKUFL0GwT13crMFr)
Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://0.tqn.com/d/animals/1/0/J/f/shutterstock_140897.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 5: Species A
sections.body:
Species A

Reciprocal Selection: Selection that occurs in two species, due to their interactions with one another

Species B

If interaction increases fitness in both species: Mutualism

Fitness in one species increases, other is not affected: Commensalism

If interaction decreases fitness in one species: Antagonism

SPEAKER NOTES: Lichens: energy produced by photosynthesis in algae is provided to the fungi.  The fungus can pass nutrients and minerals to the algae

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://ferrebeekeeper.files.wordpress.com/2011/03/lichen27.jpg)
Image 2: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: http://im.glogster.com/media/5/27/24/27/27242770.jpg)
Image 3: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: http://thekeyofknowledge.net/downloads/Garden/KCCorganic_landscape_files/KCCorg1.jpg)

examples: Coevolution: reciprocal evolutionary change in two or more interacting species.
Types of interaction: antagonistic (predator-prey, parasite-host), mutualistic (pollinator-flower), neutral/commensal.
Strong coevolution requires intimate, long-term, specific interaction — generalists coevolve weakly.
popup.mnemonic: Coevolution = Reciprocal change. Both species adapt to each other, not just to environment.

flashcard.front: What is the difference between coevolution and simple adaptation to another species?
flashcard.back: COEVOLUTION requires RECIPROCAL evolutionary change: species A adapts to species B, AND species B adapts to species A in response. Each species acts as a selective force on the other. SIMPLE ADAPTATION occurs when one species evolves to use another as a resource without that other species evolving in response — e.g., a generalist herbivore that eats many plants doesn't significantly affect any single plant's evolution. True coevolution is most visible in tightly coupled pairs: predator-prey arms races (newt/snake TTX), mutualisms (fig/fig wasp), parasite-host (Red Queen dynamics). The key test: did the second species evolve specifically in response to the first?

quiz.question: Which of these best illustrates true coevolution?
quiz.correct: The garter snake and rough-skinned newt, where newts evolved TTX toxicity and snakes evolved TTX resistance in tight reciprocal fashion
distractors: A deer eating tree leaves
A bird building a nest in a tree
Humans domesticating dogs over 15,000 years

visual.mnemonic: Antagonistic, Mutualistic, Commensal.
visual.trap: Not all species interactions are coevolutionary. Coevolution requires RECIPROCAL evolution — both sides respond to each other.

v2.definition: Coevolution is reciprocal evolutionary change between two or more interacting species, each driving selection on the other.

mnemonic.explanation: Coevolution requires SELECTION going both ways: one species evolves, the other responds, and the cycle continues.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Two species evolving together is coevolution.' Coevolution requires RECIPROCAL selection, not just simultaneous evolution.

actions.quizPrompt: Distinguish pairwise from diffuse coevolution.
actions.flashcardBack: Reciprocal evolutionary change between two or more interacting species.

nodes.title: Antagonistic Coevolution & Arms Races
nodes.subtitle: Garter snake vs newt + geographic mosaic (Lec 9 slides 6-16)

popup.heading: Lecture 9 — Antagonistic Coevolution

sections.label: Slide 6: Antagonistic interactions
sections.body:
Antagonistic interactions

Antagonistic relationships generate negative frequency-dependent selection
Frequency-dependent selection is a form of natural selection where a phenotype's fitness depends on its frequency relative to others in a population.
In negative selection, rarer, unique traits are favored, promoting diversity.
In positive selection, common traits are favored, often reducing variation.
It is a key mechanism for maintaining polymorphism.

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture19.jpg" (alt text: "15.03_fmt1.jpeg")

sections.label: Slide 7: Antagonistic interactions
sections.body:
Antagonistic interactions

Antagonistic relationships generate negative frequency-dependent selection
e.g., host parasite interactions:

Parasites/pathogens are adapted to the most common host genotype within a population
Most common host genotype fitness
Common host fitness
Fitness of rare host genotype

Rare genotype now becomes more common
Cycle continues and can maintain genetic variation for host resistance and pathogen virulence

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture19.jpg" (alt text: "15.03_fmt1.jpeg")

sections.label: Slide 8: Antagonistic interactions
sections.body:
Antagonistic interactions

Reciprocal environmental pressures occur when species interact with each other
Lotka-Voltera Predator-Prey Model

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (alt text: "Chapter 7: Predation – Applied Ecology")

sections.label: Slide 9: Complex antagonistic interaction
sections.body:
Complex antagonistic interaction
Pairwise interactions between 2 species result in the strongest antagonistic coevolution, but . . .
Predators often have many prey species

Parasites often have many host species
Coevolutionary Alternation: Alternation between prey (or host) species depending on their relative abundance in the population

Paired interaction might be strong for a short time

But not necessarily reciprocal

Predator might shift to another prey species before any adaptation arises

Cortez, M.H. and Weitz, J.S., 2014. Coevolution can reverse predator–prey cycles.
Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 111(20), pp.7486-7491.

Defense and counter-defense

Coevolutionary arms race: species that interact antagonistically in a way that results in each species exerting reciprocal directional selection on the other.
Must be conflict
Selection must act reciprocally
Agents of selection are under selection themselves

Prey

Predator

TTX (newt)

sections.body:
The strength and direction of selection varies among populations

Populations might respond in different ways
Pleiotropic effects on coevolved traits
Trade-offs (investment in toxins, reduce investment elsewhere)

sections.label: Slide 16: Geographic mosaic theory of coevolution
sections.body:
Geographic mosaic theory of coevolution
Geographic  structure of populations is central to the dynamics of coevolution

Selection varies from population to population
Coevolved genes in populations mix together due to gene flow
Geographic variation in:
type of selection on the interacting species
the strength of reciprocal selection resulting from interactions
the distribution of traits that evolve in response to selection
Selection can be antagonistic or mutualistic

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-6.dropbox.com/t/0/AAD7BWg8FK5YUCi3qXPchLTPtXwHFENgRCLHKOF82i8N3A/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1361217600/0/2/15.07_fmt.jpeg/DhnZ4-aPb8YMayZp4A6j1G_4cbAJYWDTy-hpj3OgdfU?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

examples: Rough-skinned newt (Taricha granulosa) produces tetrodotoxin (TTX) — powerful neurotoxin.
Garter snake (Thamnophis sirtalis) evolved TTX-resistant sodium channels, can eat newts without dying.
Both toxicity and resistance have COSTS: TTX-resistant snakes move slower; highly toxic newts are energetically expensive.
Geographic mosaic theory (Thompson): coevolution is locally variable — some populations are coevolutionary "hotspots," others "coldspots."
popup.mnemonic: Arms race: each side escalates + pays costs. Locally variable = mosaic.

flashcard.front: Describe the garter snake / rough-skinned newt arms race and why it illustrates the costs of coevolutionary escalation.
flashcard.back: Rough-skinned newts (Taricha granulosa) produce TETRODOTOXIN (TTX), one of the most powerful neurotoxins known — it blocks voltage-gated sodium channels, causing paralysis and death in most predators. Garter snakes (Thamnophis sirtalis) in contact with toxic newts have evolved MUTATIONS IN THEIR Na+ CHANNEL GENES that prevent TTX binding — making them resistant. The snakes eat newts, and newts respond by becoming MORE toxic, driving further snake resistance — an escalating arms race. CRUCIALLY, BOTH SIDES PAY COSTS: (1) Resistant snakes have slower movement because their Na+ channels work less efficiently, (2) Highly toxic newts expend energy producing TTX. The geographic mosaic theory (Thompson) explains why coevolution varies by location — some populations are coevolutionary HOTSPOTS with extreme toxicity and resistance, while others are COLDSPOTS with little of either. The snake-newt system is a paradigmatic example of coevolution in action.

quiz.question: The garter snake/newt arms race demonstrates that coevolutionary escalation:
quiz.correct: Carries fitness costs on both sides, because the traits that win the arms race come at a price
distractors: Always produces perfectly adapted species
Only occurs in laboratory conditions
Has no measurable impact on population fitness

visual.mnemonic: Poison + Resistance + Costs + Mosaic.
visual.trap: Arms races are not infinitely escalating — costs constrain how far escalation can go.

v2.definition: Antagonistic coevolution drives escalating arms races, like garter snakes evolving resistance to newt tetrodotoxin in geographic mosaics.

mnemonic.hook: Newt poison, snake immunity, slower snake
mnemonic.explanation: Newts make tetrodotoxin, snakes evolve sodium channel resistance, but the resistant snakes pay the cost in speed.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Arms races escalate forever.' Costs eventually balance benefits, freezing the race or causing local extinction.

actions.quizPrompt: Describe the geographic mosaic of garter snake and newt coevolution.
actions.flashcardFront: What toxin do newts use against snakes?
actions.flashcardBack: Tetrodotoxin (TTX), a powerful sodium channel blocker.

nodes.title: Mutualistic Coevolution
nodes.subtitle: Flowers, pollinators, and mutual benefit (Lec 9 slides 17-20)

popup.heading: Lecture 9 — Mutualistic Coevolution

sections.label: Slide 17: Mutualistic interactions
sections.body:
Mutualistic interactions
Mutualistic interactions result when there is an increase in alleles that allow one species to better complement the alleles of its partner

Mutualistic relationships generate positive frequency-dependent selection
Increase in beneficial alleles
Increase in partner fitness
Increase in fitness of other partner
Positive frequency-dependent selection sweeps alleles to fixation

(Negative frequency-dependent selection maintains genetic variation)

SPEAKER NOTES: Mutation that makes plants supply mycorhizal fungi with more carbon, plant will also benefit because fungi will grow faster and provide the plant more nutrients
