sections.label: Slide 20: Mutualistic flies and flowers
sections.body:
Mutualistic flies and flowers

Disa nivea
Zaluzianskya flowers dominate at some sites: results in strong reciprocal selection.
At some sites, Zaluzianskya flowers compete with plants with short flowers – this dilutes reciprocal selection.
If fly can’t reach nectar in Zaluzianskya flowers, it can drink from another species.
Disa nivea deceptively mimics Zaluzianskya flowers!
Probably causing a geographic mosaic of coevolution

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-3.dropbox.com/t/0/AAD1UKpUpzqoZWwQ9RPmtgifYIAuJL7iIDBq8D4wq9znuw/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1361224800/0/2/15.13_fmt.jpeg/nBkhKZZze9rz6B0UQAHM_T-FVHqVs0zGjZC1PybdOsk?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)
Image 2: File "Picture3.jpg"
Image 3: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.pacificbulbsociety.org/pbswiki/files/Disa/Disa_nivea_AuroraPeak_CM3.jpg)

examples: Mutualisms: both species benefit from the interaction.
Specialized fly pollinators and matching flowers — e.g., long-tongued flies and long-spurred orchids coevolved.
Darwin's prediction: seeing a Madagascar orchid with a 30 cm nectary, Darwin predicted a moth with a matching 30 cm proboscis existed — confirmed 40 years later (Xanthopan morganii praedicta).
popup.mnemonic: Mutual benefit = coevolution with reward not punishment. Each side gets paid.

flashcard.front: Give an example of mutualistic coevolution where a specific prediction was made and later confirmed.
flashcard.back: DARWIN'S MADAGASCAR ORCHID PREDICTION: In 1862, Darwin examined a Madagascar orchid (Angraecum sesquipedale) with an unusually long nectary spur — 28-30 cm. He predicted that a pollinator with a matching 25-30 cm proboscis must exist, even though none was known at the time, because the orchid's nectar would otherwise be inaccessible. This was a bold prediction from coevolutionary theory. FORTY YEARS LATER (1903), after Darwin's death, the moth Xanthopan morganii praedicta (the "predicted" one) was discovered with exactly that proboscis length. This is a textbook case of a successful evolutionary prediction — and it demonstrated that coevolution can produce matching morphologies without either species having to "know" what the other is doing.

quiz.question: Darwin predicted the existence of a Madagascar moth with a 25+ cm proboscis based on what observation?
quiz.correct: A Madagascar orchid with a nectary 28-30 cm long that no known pollinator could reach
distractors: Fossil evidence of ancient moths
DNA analysis of modern moths
Direct observation of pollination

visual.mnemonic: Length matches length. Darwin predicted, nature confirmed.
visual.trap: Many mutualisms can break down if one partner evolves to cheat — obligate mutualisms can be vulnerable.

v2.definition: Mutualistic coevolution produces partnerships where both species benefit, like flowers and pollinators evolving matched traits.

mnemonic.explanation: Plants offer nectar bribes, pollinators carry pollen, neither could thrive alone, both win together.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Mutualism is always stable.' Cheaters that take rewards without pollinating destabilize mutualisms unless punished or excluded.

actions.quizPrompt: How does cheating threaten mutualistic coevolution?
actions.flashcardBack: Set of flower traits (color, shape, scent) matched to pollinator type.

nodes.title: Mullerian & Batesian Mimicry
nodes.subtitle: Signal evolution under predator selection (Lec 9 slides 21-29)

popup.heading: Lecture 9 — Mimicry

sections.label: Slide 21: Convergent coevolution
sections.body:
Convergent coevolution
Unrelated species converge on a similar phenotype, and the increased appearance of that phenotype benefits all species involved

Mullerian mimicry: Several harmful or distasteful species resemble each other in appearance, facilitating the learned avoidance of predators

Fritz Muller
1821-1897

Mullerian mimicry

Convergent coevolution

sections.label: Slide 24: Batesian mimicry
sections.body:
Batesian mimicry

Cyanide-producing millipedes
Aposematism:
Anti-predator strategy used by potential prey to signal danger

Warning coloration is most common

Apheloria (yellow) is toxic
Brachoria (blue) is harmless

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-3.dropbox.com/t/0/AAAJJMMNGOlZtNw15zbYk4UtBUyJjENoYDrNQG40j-tzFg/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1361224800/0/2/15.14_fmt.jpeg/kB6YST0UK3eh8VP8NwAFwyGpXAVp1OYrph9Ep9i0tKQ?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

sections.label: Slide 25: Batesian mimicry
sections.body:
Batesian mimicry
Effectiveness depends on population sizes of model vs mimic
If mimic is rare, then…
Predator will encounter harmful model more frequently
Learn to associate color pattern with the harmful model
Existing color pattern will be favored
Stabilizing selection leaves both model and mimic unchanged

Batesian mimics are cheaters
They don’t invest into producing toxins

Cheaters are very common

sections.label: Slide 26: Batesian mimicry
sections.body:
Batesian mimicry
Effectiveness depends on population sizes of model vs mimic
If mimic is more common, then…
Predators will encounter harmless species more often
They associate patterns with harmless mimic
Toxic model will now be attacked
Selection should favor toxic individuals that diverge from the mimic pattern
Harmless mimics that converge on the new pattern will be favored
Results in a coevolutionary chase: toxic species evolves away from its Batesian mimic, and the mimic evolves to catch up

sections.body:
The tūī
(Prosthemadera )novaeseelandiae
Coevolutionary interactions

Can increase biodiversity

Geographic mosaic

If one species goes extinct, so does the other

sections.body:
Coevolutionary interactions
Optimal bill depth in middle

sections.label: Slide 29: Coevolutionary interactions
sections.body:
Coevolutionary interactions
Mainland population of flower (Rhabdothamnus solandri) lost two of its three main pollinators.
High dependence resulted in low fruit production (reproduction)

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture5.jpg"

examples: MÜLLERIAN mimicry: multiple UNPALATABLE/dangerous species converge on similar warning signals. Benefits both — predators learn faster. e.g., Heliconius butterflies, coral snakes.
BATESIAN mimicry: a PALATABLE species mimics a dangerous/unpalatable model. Benefits only the mimic — cost to the model as predators sometimes attack (naive or if mimic becomes too common).
Mimicry requires (1) a model, (2) a mimic, (3) a deceivable predator.
Tūī bird (New Zealand): Some plants mimic flowers normally pollinated by tūīs to attract them.
warnings: Batesian mimicry is DECEPTIVE — mimics exploit the predator's learned avoidance. If mimics become too common relative to the model, the deception breaks down.
popup.mnemonic: Müllerian = Mutual protection (both toxic). Batesian = Bluffing (mimic is safe).

flashcard.front: Compare Müllerian and Batesian mimicry. Which is a mutualism and which is a form of parasitism?
flashcard.back: MÜLLERIAN MIMICRY: Multiple UNPALATABLE/dangerous species converge on similar warning colorations (often bright red/yellow/black bands). All the mimicking species BENEFIT because predators only need to learn ONE aposematic signal to avoid all of them — pooling the "teaching cost" of predator learning across many species. This is a MUTUALISM: every species gains. Example: Heliconius butterflies in the Amazon, where many toxic species share similar wing patterns. BATESIAN MIMICRY: A PALATABLE species mimics an unpalatable model. Only the MIMIC benefits (escaping predation by bluffing); the MODEL pays a cost because naive or confused predators may attack mistaken individuals. This is a form of PARASITISM — the mimic exploits the model's honest signal. Example: some flies and moths resemble stinging bees or wasps despite being harmless. KEY DIFFERENCE: Müllerian = both defended, mutual. Batesian = one defended, one not, parasitic.

quiz.question: Two unrelated toxic butterfly species gradually evolve the same warning color pattern. This is:
quiz.correct: Müllerian mimicry — mutually beneficial convergence on a shared warning signal
distractors: Batesian mimicry — one is palatable, one is not
Camouflage — both are trying to hide
Coincidental convergence with no fitness effect

visual.mnemonic: M = Mutual. B = Bluff.
visual.trap: Batesian mimics only work if rare relative to model. If mimics outnumber models, predators learn the signal is unreliable.

v2.definition: Mimicry evolves when species converge on warning signals: Batesian mimics fake danger, Mullerian mimics share real danger.

mnemonic.explanation: Batesian mimics are LIARS imitating toxic models; Mullerian mimics are HONEST partners sharing one warning.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Mimicry always benefits both species.' In Batesian mimicry, the model is HARMED because predators eventually attack the warning signal.

actions.quizPrompt: Distinguish Batesian from Mullerian mimicry.
actions.flashcardFront: Why must Batesian mimics stay rare?
actions.flashcardBack: If too common, predators learn the signal does not always mean danger.

nodes.title: Endosymbiosis & Microbes
nodes.subtitle: Mitochondria, chloroplasts, and leafhopper symbionts (Lec 9 slides 30-36)

popup.heading: Lecture 9 — Microbes and Endosymbiosis

sections.label: Slide 30: Important microbes
sections.body:
Important microbes
Endosymbionts: Mutualistic organisms that live within the body or cells of another organism
Typically bacteria that perform important functions
Digest plant matter
Synthesize vitamins
Defense against pathogens

Humans have 100 trillion bacteria and archaea,
representing thousands of species

sections.label: Slide 31: Aster leafhoppers and endosymbionts
sections.body:
Aster leafhoppers and endosymbionts
Feed on crops that lack necessary amino acids
Depend on sap eating bacteria living in bacteriomes inside leafhopper
Nasuia
Sulcia
Sulcia is also found in other sap-feeding insects
Long coevolutionary history with Nasuia and Sulcia (260-280 mya)

Figure 15.24 Aster leafhoppers feed on crops, spreading a disease known as aster yellows. They can survive on nutrient-poor plant sap by harboring two species of bacteria in special organs called bacteriomes. The bacteria synthesize amino acids that they provide to the insects
Macrosteles quadrilineatus

sections.label: Slide 32: Aster leafhoppers and endosymbionts
sections.body:
Aster leafhoppers and endosymbionts
Feed on crops that lack necessary amino acids
Depend on sap eating bacteria living in bacteriomes inside leafhopper
Nasuia
Sulcia
Sulcia is also found in other sap-feeding insects
Long coevolutionary history with Nasuia and Sulcia
280 million years
Can reconstruct phylogenies of bacteria and their hosts

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture5.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 33: Endosymbiosis
sections.body:
Endosymbiosis
Origin of organelles
Mitochondria – much like a cell within a cell
Once a free-living, oxygen consuming bacteria that entered a single-celled host

First proposed in late 1800’s, but idea was revived in the 1960’s by Lynn Margulis.
Lynn Margulis
(1938-2011)
1970’s: Mitochondria have more similarities with bacteria than with genes in animals

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-2.dropbox.com/t/0/AACT6MnHcn1ibTLOCkv7EfxS2N4Mccp5MwN3RtP59q1YFQ/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1361232000/0/2/15.23B_fmt1.jpeg/hj96XHarinXJq9wf36VXWHw8hayra1907yDHGIZDX8E?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)
Image 2: File "Picture6.jpg" (originally from URL: http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Lynn_Margulis.jpg/220px-Lynn_Margulis.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 34: Endosymbiosis
sections.body:
Endosymbiosis
Closest relative to mitochondria: clade of bacteria called Pelagibacteracea
Includes the SAR11 clade
Marine bacteria use oxygen to feed on dissolved organic carbon
inside some of the first eukaryotes ~2 billion years ago
With the energy provided by the endosymbiont, early Eukaryotes lost their own energy-generating proteins
Very tight relationship after evolving together for billions of years
Other plastids became organelles
Present day single-celled Eukaryotes are full of bacteria

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture5.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 35: Plant plastids are derived from free-living bacteria
sections.body:
Plant plastids are derived from free-living bacteria
Euglena have both animal and plant properties.

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture1.jpg"
Image 2: File "Picture4.jpg" (alt text: "15.24_fmt.jpeg")

sections.label: Slide 36: Take home points
sections.body:
Take home points
Interactions between species leads to reciprocal evolutionary change driven by natural selection

Reciprocal selection can lead to antagonistic or mutualistic relationships

Antagonistic relationships can lead to an evolutionary arms race

Population differences in selection can lead to geographic variation in the degree of coevolution

Organelles arose via endosymbiotic relationships with unicellular host

VISUALS: None.


###############################################

examples: Endosymbiosis theory (Margulis): mitochondria and chloroplasts are descendants of free-living bacteria engulfed by ancestral eukaryotes.
Evidence: mitochondria and chloroplasts have their own circular DNA, double membranes, and divide by binary fission like bacteria.
Aster leafhoppers carry bacterial endosymbionts that provide essential amino acids.
Plant plastids are derived from free-living cyanobacteria.
Many insects depend on bacterial symbionts for survival — without them, they cannot develop.
popup.mnemonic: Endosymbiosis: not just a historical event — happening continuously today. Life is collaborative.

flashcard.front: What is the endosymbiotic theory, and what evidence supports it for mitochondria and chloroplasts?
flashcard.back: ENDOSYMBIOTIC THEORY (Lynn Margulis, 1967): mitochondria and chloroplasts originated as FREE-LIVING BACTERIA that were engulfed by (or entered) ancestral eukaryotic cells and became permanent intracellular symbionts. Mitochondria descend from an alpha-proteobacterium; chloroplasts descend from a cyanobacterium. EVIDENCE: (1) Mitochondria and chloroplasts have their OWN circular DNA (like bacteria), separate from the nuclear genome. (2) They have DOUBLE MEMBRANES — the inner membrane derived from the bacterial cell membrane, the outer from the host phagosome. (3) They DIVIDE BY BINARY FISSION, like bacteria. (4) Their ribosomes are 70S (bacterial type), not 80S (eukaryotic). (5) Some antibiotics that target bacterial ribosomes also affect mitochondria (but not cytoplasmic ribosomes). (6) Phylogenetic analysis of mitochondrial/chloroplast DNA places them within specific bacterial clades. The theory is now universally accepted and is one of the most important ideas in evolution.

quiz.question: Which feature of mitochondria provides the strongest evidence for their bacterial origin?
quiz.correct: They have their own circular DNA, double membrane, and divide by binary fission
distractors: They contain ATP
They are found only in animal cells
They produce oxygen as a byproduct

visual.mnemonic: Two bacterial ancestors, two major leaps.
visual.trap: Only some eukaryotes (plants, algae) have chloroplasts — all eukaryotes have mitochondria (or mitochondrial remnants).

v2.definition: Endosymbiosis is intimate coevolution where one organism lives inside another, producing mitochondria, chloroplasts, and insect symbionts.

mnemonic.explanation: An ancient cell ate a bacterium and never digested it; the bacterium became your mitochondria.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Mitochondria are part of the original cell.' Mitochondria descend from ENGULFED alpha-proteobacteria and still keep their own DNA.

actions.quizPrompt: What evidence shows mitochondria began as free-living bacteria?
actions.flashcardBack: Mitochondria from alpha-proteobacteria, chloroplasts from cyanobacteria.

nodes.title: Why Sex? Costs & Benefits
nodes.subtitle: The twofold cost of sex and the Red Queen (Lec 10-11 slides 1-13)

popup.heading: Lecture 10-11 — Why Sexual Reproduction?

sections.label: Slide 1: Sex and sexual selection
sections.body:
Sex and sexual selection
Dr. Travis Robbins
Evolution
BIOL 4340
University of Nebraska Omaha

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 2: Sex and sexual selection
sections.body:
Sex and sexual selection
Lecture goals

To understand the costs and benefits of sexual reproduction

To explain how an evolutionary arms race favors the evolution of sex

To describe one model for the evolution of anisogamy

To understand how differential reproductive investment by males and females leads to sexual selection

sections.label: Slide 3: Which mode of reproduction is better: sexual or asexual?
sections.body:
Which mode of reproduction is better: sexual or asexual?
Sexual reproduction: process that creates new offspring by combining the genetic material from the fusion of gametes (sperm & egg) from two individuals.
Asexual reproduction: process that creates new offspring from a single parent, and offspring inherit the genes of that parent only. Generally, . . .
Does not involve meiosis
Does not involve ploidy reduction
Does not involve fertilization

Hermaphrodites: individuals that produce both male and female gametes

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.dereilanatureinn.ca/lagoon/livingwaters/Barnicle.jpg)
Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-4.dropbox.com/t/0/AADHPVctg2MhwpgCFv8-jtUS3tCJwLupPRSyotgH5BqMpA/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1361739600/0/2/11.02_fmt1.jpeg/k3b3260MdlgUib8W5yVs3L9EFMMiIiL091KjH_dUaa0?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

sections.label: Slide 4: Costs and benefits
sections.body:
Costs and benefits

Must mate with males

Half of offspring (males) cannot produce offspring

Sex cuts reproducing population in half

Asexual females produce twice that of sexual females

Only daughters are produced

Asexual genotypes should increase in frequency
Twofold cost of sex: Asexual lineages multiply faster than sexual lineages because all progeny are capable of reproducing. In sexual lineages, half the offspring (males) cannot reproduce, thus halving the rate of replication.

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (alt text: "11.03_fmt.jpeg")
Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (alt text: "11.03_fmt.jpeg")

sections.label: Slide 5: Even more costs to sexual reproduction
sections.body:
Even more costs to sexual reproduction
Break up favorable gene combinations

Because of meiosis, sex halves relatedness between parents and offspring

Search for potential mates requires time and energy

Exposure to predators when searching for mates

Courtship requires energy

Parasite infection and sexually-transmitted diseases

sections.label: Slide 6: Why isn’t sexual reproduction replaced by asexuality more often?
sections.body:
Why isn’t sexual reproduction replaced by asexuality more often?

>42,000 recognized vertebrate species

Number of asexual vertebrates (~0.17%)
~22 species of fish
~23 species of amphibians
~29 species of reptiles

Some benefits of sexual reproduction
Combining beneficial mutations and generating novel genotypes, resulting in faster evolution
Clearance of deleterious mutations
Reduction of sibling competition
Fighting parasitic infection & the Red Queen Effect

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://ichef.bbci.co.uk/naturelibrary/images/ic/credit/640x395/p/pa/parthenogenesis/parthenogenesis_1.jpg)
Image 2: File "Picture18.jpg" (originally from URL: https://encrypted-tbn3.gstatic.com/images?q=tbn:ANd9GcRcnuomNHdhQp3jk21H6y64RSmELCSs48FkcF4_TO8MPO6GafQ5gQ)
Image 3: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Rana_esculenta_on_Nymphaea_edit.JPG/250px-Rana_esculenta_on_Nymphaea_edit.JPG)

sections.label: Slide 7: Combining beneficial mutations and generating novel genotypes
sections.body:
Combining beneficial mutations and generating novel genotypes
Brings together separate beneficial mutations in a single individual

Occurs faster than arising spontaneously in the same genome

Allows for quicker adaptation to changing environments
Water fleas switch between sexual and asexual

New gene combinations increase the chance that at least some offspring will be a good match to the new environment.

More sexual reproduction in unpredictable environments

SPEAKER NOTES: If parasites infect an asexual lineage, the parasites are liktely to be very successful, since in each generations, their host’s genome remains largely unchanged. But the generation of genetic variation due to sexual reproduction creates a moving target for parasites.  Sexual reproduction  will enable the host to keep pace with the parasites.

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture3.jpg" (alt text: "img023.jpg")

sections.label: Slide 8: Combining beneficial mutations and generating novel genotypes
sections.body:
Combining beneficial mutations and generating novel genotypes
Alleles A’, B’, and C’ are beneficial mutations at different loci that arise in different individuals

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture5.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 9: 2) Clearance of deleterious mutations
sections.body:
2) Clearance of deleterious mutations
Meiosis can generate allelic combinations that exclude deleterious mutations
Beneficial mutations can be separated from harmful ones (not all offspring will inherit deleterious mutations)
Asexual reproduction cannot separate bad mutations from good ones

Bad mutations can accumulate in asexual species, but are broken apart in sexual species
Parent
Offspring

Muller’s ratchet:
When genomes of asexual populations accumulate deleterious mutations in an irreversible manner

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture3.jpg" (alt text: "img025.jpg")
Image 2: File "Picture5.jpg" (alt text: "img025.jpg")
Image 3: File "Picture9.jpg" (alt text: "img025.jpg")

sections.label: Slide 10: 3) Reduction of sibling competition
sections.body:
3) Reduction of sibling competition
Individuals with similar genotypes will require the same resources.

Siblings’ parents will have increased competition under asexual reproduction

Genetic variability is increased under sexual reproduction
Sex creates opportunity for offspring to specialize in different niches in an environment, thereby reducing sibling competition

New Zealand snail with both sexual and asexual forms
Sexual form is more common in habitats with more ecological niches (lakes) than those with few niches (streams)

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a2/Potamopyrgus_antipodarum_shell.png)

sections.label: Slide 11: 4) Fighting parasitic infection & the Red Queen Effect
sections.body:
4) Fighting parasitic infection & the Red Queen Effect
An evolutionary arms race between hosts and parasites
Parasites specialize on the most common genotypes in host populations

Negative frequency-dependent selection results in cycles of common and rare genotypes

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture10.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 12: 4) Fighting parasitic infection & the Red Queen Effect
sections.body:
4) Fighting parasitic infection & the Red Queen Effect
Co-evolutionary arms race

Sexual reproduction enables more rapid counter-adaptation to parasites

Red Queen Effect: For co-evolving populations, each population must constantly adapt to the other to maintain relative fitness
Lewis Carroll – Through the Looking Glass
Hosts have to evolve rapidly to stay in place (i.e., survive)

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.kpkaiser.com/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/redqueen.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 13: 4) Fighting parasitic infection & the Red Queen Effect
sections.body:
4) Fighting parasitic infection & the Red Queen Effect
Parasites

Hosts
Sexual reproduction enables rapid adaptation to defenses and counter-defenses
Parasites will adapt quickly to asexual hosts, but lack of new genetic variation will constrain reciprocal adaptation in asexual hosts

New Zealand freshwater snail

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: https://photos-1.dropbox.com/t/0/AABXxSNL1pRM2ImskXPLeQ3sWzTS52yoMSID4RB5NC5Ddg/10/69891698/jpeg/32x32/2/1361746800/0/2/11.06_fmt.jpeg/zAGim5IZY0Th6TdNDvgPAjLeIl2EqAh6Sna30BgVRC8?size=1280x960&size_mode=2)

examples: Twofold cost of sex: an asexual female passes 100% of her genes to each offspring; a sexual female passes only 50%. Asexual lineages should grow twice as fast.
Other costs: finding a mate, courtship energy, risk of STDs, loss of coadapted gene combinations.

Benefits: (1) combining beneficial mutations, (2) clearing deleterious mutations (Muller's ratchet), (3) reducing sibling competition through variation, (4) Red Queen effect — staying ahead of coevolving parasites.
popup.mnemonic: Red Queen: "It takes all the running you can do, to keep in the same place." Sex = running against parasites.

flashcard.front: What is the Red Queen hypothesis for the maintenance of sexual reproduction, and what evidence supports it?
flashcard.back: The RED QUEEN HYPOTHESIS (named after Lewis Carroll's Red Queen): sexual reproduction persists despite its twofold cost because it allows hosts to stay AHEAD of rapidly coevolving parasites. Parasites evolve fast (short generations, large populations) and constantly generate new genotypes that evade host defenses. Asexual hosts cannot shuffle their genes, so parasites adapt to them. Sexual hosts produce genetically variable offspring, some of which are resistant to current parasite genotypes — a moving target. EVIDENCE: (1) Lively studied New Zealand freshwater snails (Potamopyrgus antipodarum) that have both sexual and asexual lineages. In populations with high parasite pressure, sexual lineages dominate; in low-parasite populations, asexuals dominate. (2) Experimental coevolution of bacteria and phages shows sexual hosts maintain higher fitness under parasite pressure. The Red Queen provides the strongest answer to why sex is worth its twofold cost.

quiz.question: The "twofold cost of sex" refers to:
quiz.correct: An asexual female passes 100% of her genes to each offspring, while a sexual female passes only 50%
distractors: Sexual reproduction requires twice as much energy per offspring
Males are twice as likely to get sick as females
Sexual offspring have half the survival rate of asexual offspring

visual.mnemonic: Costs 2×, Benefits variable.
visual.trap: No single benefit fully explains sex — most evolutionary biologists believe multiple benefits (not one alone) outweigh the costs.

nodes.lectureTitle: Sexual Selection: Origins and Why Sex

v2.definition: Sex is costly because males contribute only genes, yet the Red Queen and recombination benefits keep sex universal.

mnemonic.explanation: The Red Queen runs forever just to stay still; sexual species recombine to outpace evolving parasites.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Sex evolved for genetic diversity.' Sex is COSTLY; the question is what benefit overcomes the twofold cost of producing males.

actions.quizPrompt: Explain the twofold cost of sex and how the Red Queen resolves it.
actions.flashcardBack: Asexual lineages accumulate deleterious mutations because they cannot recombine.

nodes.title: Anisogamy: The Origin of Sexual Selection
nodes.subtitle: Why males and females exist + investment asymmetry (Lec 10-11 slides 14-22)

popup.heading: Lecture 10-11 — Anisogamy and Sexual Selection

sections.label: Slide 14: Anisogamy
sections.body:
Anisogamy
Anisogamy: Sexual reproduction involving the fusion to two dissimilar gametes
Males – many, but small sperm

Females – few, but large eggs

An extreme example – Kiwi bird

Trillion sperm can be produced with similar amount of energy to produce a single egg

SPEAKER NOTES: Cow’s ovum is 20,000 times the size of a bull’s sperm

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture5.jpg" (alt text: "11.08_fmt.jpeg")

sections.label: Slide 15: Model for the evolution of Anisogamy
sections.body:
Model for the evolution of Anisogamy

Arose from isogamy: production of one type of gamete, but still fuse together

Probably an ancestral marine organism that sheds gametes into water

Wide range of gametes (proto-sperm & proto-egg) produced

Suppose the following
Tradeoff between size & number of gametes produced
Larger size, less mobile
Larger gametes have higher survival

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture5.jpg" (alt text: "img024.jpg")
Image 2: File "Picture9.jpg" (alt text: "img024.jpg")

sections.label: Slide 16: Model for the evolution of Anisogamy
sections.body:
Model for the evolution of Anisogamy
Most zygotes come from small gametes (proto-sperm) fusing with other small gametes (proto-sperm) due to the large numbers produced
But if small proto-sperm fuse with large gametes (proto-eggs) they have higher survival – i.e., favored by selection

Large gametes fusing with large gametes would be best, but relatively rare
less mobile
Increases inbreeding

Disruptive selection favored the two extremes of gamete size

SPEAKER NOTES: Because large gametes are rare, selection will favor proto-sperm fusing with proto-eggs more often than eggs fusing with eggs.

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture1.jpg" (alt text: "img024.jpg")

sections.label: Slide 17: Anisogamy results in differential reproductive investment
sections.body:
Anisogamy results in differential reproductive investment
Males and females invest differently into gametes

Different strategies for reproductive success for males and females
Greater investment by females
Gamete production - Large, yolk-rich gamete
Post-fertilization investment - Parental care
Maternity is certain
Less investment by males
Relatively small inexpensive in sperm
Spend time and energy pursuing more chances to mate
Paternity is uncertain

sections.label: Slide 18: Anisogamy results in differential reproductive investment
sections.body:
Anisogamy results in differential reproductive investment
Female reproductive success is rarely, if ever, limited by sperm availability
A single man can fertilize all the eggs of every woman on Earth
But male reproductive success is limited by females (eggs)
Different strategies lead to sex differences in reproductive rate
Males are ready to breed quickly
Females require time to care for offspring

Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://magickcanoe.com/insects/egg-laying-1.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 19: Leads to biases in the
sections.body:
Leads to biases in the
Operational Sex Ratio (OSR)
Operational sex ratio:
Ratio of sexually active males to sexually-receptive females
At a given moment, there may be more males ready to mate than there are females

Females
Operational sex ratio
Increase male-male competition
Increase female choosiness
Increases selection on male traits

sections.label: Slide 20: Leads to sexual selection
sections.body:
Leads to sexual selection
“the advantage which certain individuals have over others of the same sex and species, in exclusive relation to reproduction”
							- Darwin
Differential reproductive success resulting from competition for mates
Intrasexual selection (male-male competition)
Intersexual selection (Female choice for male traits)
Sexual selection is typically stronger on males
Maximize fitness by mating multiple times
Male-biased operational sex ratio (OSR)

sections.label: Slide 21: Male-male competition or female choice
sections.body:
Male-male competition or female choice
Sexual dimorphism:
Difference in form between males and females of a species
Body color
Body size
Body structures
Behavior

Armaments – Weaponry to outcompete other individuals (used in male-male competition)

Ornaments – attractive traits that increase reproductive success (used in female choice)

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture10.jpg" (alt text: "Red Anolis sagrei.JPG")
Image 2: File "Picture8.jpg" (originally from URL: http://belforest-centre.com/images/animals/red_deer/red_deer_006.jpg)
Image 3: File "Picture6.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.abc.net.au/catalyst/stories/img/FiddlerCrabs_large.jpg)
Image 4: File "Picture10.jpg" (originally from URL: http://www.goldengateaudubon.org/wp-content/themes/inove/timthumb.php?src=http://www.goldengateaudubon.org/wp-content/uploads/grebe-lewis3.jpg&w=250&h=250)
Image 5: File "Picture12.jpg" (originally from URL: http://static.guim.co.uk/sys-images/Guardian/About/General/2012/1/19/1326995924683/Bowerbird-007.jpg)
Image 6: File "Picture4.jpg" (originally from URL: http://ferrebeekeeper.files.wordpress.com/2010/10/peacock.jpg)

sections.label: Slide 22: Variance in reproductive success
sections.body:
Variance in reproductive success
Elephant seals
Males aggressively defend harems of females
Competition leads to large variation in reproductive success

Big males have higher reproductive success

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (alt text: "11.12B_fmt.jpeg")
Image 2: File "Picture6.jpg" (alt text: "11.13_fmt.jpeg")
Image 3: File "Picture3.jpg" (alt text: "11.12A_fmt1.jpeg")

examples: Anisogamy: gametes of unequal size. Small cheap gametes (sperm) = male. Large expensive gametes (egg) = female.
Isogamy → anisogamy evolved because disruptive selection on gamete size: a few large, well-provisioned gametes + many small, cheap gametes outperform intermediates.
Anisogamy leads to differential reproductive investment: females invest more per offspring → fewer offspring per female.
Result: males benefit from MORE MATINGS, females benefit from BETTER MATES. Leads to sexual selection.
popup.mnemonic: Small sperm, Big eggs → different strategies → Sexual selection.

flashcard.front: Explain how anisogamy leads to differential reproductive investment and, ultimately, sexual selection.
flashcard.back: ANISOGAMY = unequal gamete sizes. It evolved from isogamy (equal gametes) because disruptive selection favored two strategies: (A) a few large, nutrient-rich gametes (EGGS) that give each offspring a survival advantage, and (B) many small, cheap gametes (SPERM) that can contact more eggs. Intermediates lose to both strategies. Because EGGS are expensive, each female can produce only a LIMITED number of eggs per lifetime. Because SPERM are cheap, males can produce nearly unlimited sperm. CONSEQUENCE: Females are limited by RESOURCES (to make eggs and invest in offspring). Males are limited by MATINGS (access to females). This creates an ASYMMETRY: males benefit from many matings with any available female; females benefit from selective mating with the BEST male. This is the foundation of sexual selection — male-male competition AND female choice both arise directly from anisogamy. This logic (Bateman 1948, Trivers 1972) is one of the most important ideas in evolutionary biology.

quiz.question: Why do anisogamy and differential investment lead to sexual selection?
quiz.correct: Males compete for limited female matings while females choose carefully among many available males
distractors: Males and females are fundamentally equal in reproductive strategy
Sexual selection is independent of gamete size
Only females experience sexual selection

visual.mnemonic: Small + Many + Cheap vs Big + Few + Expensive.
visual.trap: Sex ROLES are not fixed — in some species (pipefish, seahorses) males invest more and females compete. The rule is: the sex investing MORE becomes the choosy one.

nodes.lectureTitle: Sexual Selection: Origins and Why Sex

v2.definition: Anisogamy is the asymmetry between large eggs and small sperm, the gamete-size difference that defines males and females.

mnemonic.explanation: Females are simply the sex with bigger gametes; everything else (size, behavior) flows from that asymmetry.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Males and females are defined by chromosomes.' Males and females are defined by GAMETE SIZE (small vs large), not chromosomes.

actions.quizPrompt: How does anisogamy explain the origin of sexual selection?
actions.flashcardBack: Female produces large gametes (eggs); male produces small gametes (sperm).

Variance in reproductive success

Number offspring over male lifetime

Number offspring over female lifetime
Opportunity for selection:  Variance in fitness within a population
No variance = no selection
Lots of variance = large opportunity for selection

sections.body:
# Female choice
Benefits of being choosy

Direct benefits: Benefits that affect a female directly
Food
Nest sites
Protection
Help with raising young
Indirect benefits: Benefits that affect the genetic quality of the female’s offspring
Showy ornaments (sexy son hypothesis)
Increased survival and reproductive success

sections.label: Slide 29: Direct benefit - Nuptial gifts
sections.body:
Direct benefit - Nuptial gifts

Nuptial gifts
Food that males provide the females
can boost female reproductive output

sections.label: Slide 37: # Ornaments can signal quality
sections.body:
# Ornaments can signal quality

Ornaments must be honest if it is continuously favored by selection
Honest signals
Ornaments can be costly,
But can indicate that the individual is strong enough to deal with it
Good genes passed on to offspring
(Cut)

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture3.jpg" (alt text: "11.25_fmt.jpeg")

examples: Side-blotched lizards: three morphs (rock-paper-scissors) maintained by negative frequency-dependent selection.
Nuptial gifts: male provides food to female (e.g., scorpionflies, crickets, spiders). Female evaluates gift quality as a signal of male quality.
Redback spiders: males voluntarily sacrifice themselves to be eaten, prolonging copulation and siring more offspring.
Leks: males gather at display sites for female inspection (sage grouse, manakins).
Sensory bias: female preferences may arise from pre-existing sensory biases (e.g., swordtail fish — females prefer swords even in species without them).
Ornaments can signal GENETIC QUALITY (handicap principle) or arbitrary runaway selection.
popup.mnemonic: Costly signal = honest. Ornaments can't be faked if they are genuinely handicapping.

flashcard.front: What is the "handicap principle" for sexual ornaments, and why must the ornaments be costly to function as honest signals?
flashcard.back: The HANDICAP PRINCIPLE (Zahavi 1975): exaggerated male ornaments (peacock tails, elaborate colors, long calls) evolve because they serve as HONEST SIGNALS OF QUALITY precisely BECAUSE they are costly. Only a high-quality male can afford to grow and maintain an extravagant ornament — low-quality males would die or fail to reproduce under the handicap. Therefore, a male with a massive ornament is demonstrating "I can thrive even with this burden." If ornaments were cheap, ANY male could grow one, and the signal would become uninformative (dishonest). The cost ENFORCES honesty — it's a signal that cannot be faked. Low-quality males could grow fake ornaments only at unsustainable fitness cost, so they don't. This is why peacock tails, male deer antlers, and stag beetle mandibles are so extravagant and metabolically expensive — the expense is the point.

quiz.question: According to the handicap principle, why do peacock tails evolve to be so large and metabolically expensive?
quiz.correct: The cost ensures the signal is honest — only high-quality males can afford it, so the tail truthfully signals genetic quality
distractors: Peahens cannot distinguish different tail sizes, so size is random
Large tails are actually free to produce
Large tails improve peacock flight ability

visual.mnemonic: Fight, Display, or Cheat.
visual.trap: Runaway selection (Fisherian) and handicap signals (Zahavian) are not mutually exclusive — both can operate simultaneously.

v2.definition: Males compete for mates while females choose, producing ornaments, leks, sensory bias, and runaway sexual selection on display traits.

mnemonic.explanation: Males compete by fighting (intrasexual) or displaying (intersexual) — two paths to the same goal: more matings.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Female choice is always for honest signals.' Females may pick traits like long tails simply because of pre-existing sensory biases.

actions.quizPrompt: Distinguish intrasexual from intersexual selection with examples.
actions.flashcardBack: Female preference and male trait coevolve, escalating beyond utility.

nodes.title: Sperm Competition & Sexual Conflict
nodes.subtitle: When females mate with multiple males (Lec 10-11 slides 38-45)

popup.heading: Lecture 10-11 — Sperm Competition and Sexual Conflict

sections.label: Slide 38: Sexual selection does not end at mating
sections.body:
Sexual selection does not end at mating
Post-copulatory selection
Sperm competition: Sperm compete for fertilization of a female’s eggs after mating
Many different strategies to increase fertilization success
Most commonly seen in polyandrous mating systems: females mate with multiple males

sections.label: Slide 39: Strategy 1: Spines can remove sperm from other males
sections.body:
Strategy 1: Spines can remove sperm from other males

Seed beetle penis

sections.label: Slide 40: Strategy 2: Larger testes produce more sperm
sections.body:

Strategy – produce more sperm when there is high competition

Small testes in monogamous mating systems:
One male pairs with one female

Large testis size in polyandrous mating systems

sections.label: Slide 41: Strategy 3: Sperm cooperate to increase swimming efficiency
sections.body:

Promiscuous mating system – many males mate with many females

Aggregate more often with related sperm
“brother sperm” from same Dad aggregate
“cousin sperm” aggregate but to lesser extent

sections.label: Slide 42: Strategy 4: Males prevent subsequent matings
sections.body:
Strategy 4: Males prevent subsequent matings
Mate guarding to chase off rival males

Chemicals in semen that kill sperm of other males and make female unreceptive to subsequent mating (Drosophila)

Produce giant sperm that fill up entire reproductive tract of the female (Drosophila)

Copulatory plugs: inserting mucus into female reproductive tract to prevent more matings (insects, rodents, snakes)

sections.label: Slide 43: Sexual conflict
sections.body:
Sexual conflict
Sexual conflict: The evolution of phenotypic characteristics that confer a fitness benefit to one sex, but a fitness cost to the other sex
Adaptations that arise due to sperm competition can harm the female
As a result, females have counter defenses to males

Traits coevolve antagonistically
Coevolutionary arms race

sections.label: Slide 44: Chemicals in Drosophila semen
sections.body:
Chemicals in Drosophila semen
Ward off other male sperm, but are toxic to females
Females produce proteins that destroy some of the toxic proteins

Females then forced to mate with males reared in a competitive environment
Males and females are in an arms race
Monogamy should remove antagonism
Experimental test: forced monogamy

40 generations later
 Semen was less toxic
 Females lost their defenses

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture3.jpg" (alt text: "11.33_fmt.jpeg")

sections.label: Slide 45: Sexual selection – Take home points
sections.body:
Sexual selection – Take home points
Anisogamy leads to differential investment by males and females, which leads to biases in the OSR
Biased OSRs lead to sexual selection
Sexual selection can be inter- or intra-sexual
Male-male competition and female choice can lead to large variance in male reproductive success
Female choice has direct and indirect benefits
Sexual selection does not end at mating (sperm competition)
Sexual selection can lead to sexual conflict

VISUALS: None.


###############################################

examples: Sperm competition: when multiple males' sperm compete within the female reproductive tract to fertilize eggs.

Male strategies: (1) Spines remove rival sperm (Odonata), (2) Larger testes produce more sperm (primates with promiscuous females), (3) Sperm cooperate by forming swimming trains (some rodents), (4) Mate guarding / mating plugs / copulatory prolongation.
Drosophila seminal fluid contains chemicals that suppress female remating and reduce sperm of previous males — BUT also shorten female lifespan (sexually antagonistic).
Sexual conflict: what is optimal for males may harm females.
warnings: Sexual conflict can drive rapid reciprocal evolution — an arms race between the sexes within a species.
popup.mnemonic: Remove, Out-produce, Cooperate, Prevent remating.

flashcard.front: What is sexual conflict and how does it manifest in Drosophila seminal fluid proteins?
flashcard.back: SEXUAL CONFLICT arises when what is optimal for male reproductive success is HARMFUL to female fitness (or vice versa). Because males and females share most of their genomes, evolution of male-beneficial traits can reduce female fitness and drive counter-adaptations in females — a sexually antagonistic coevolutionary arms race WITHIN a species. DROSOPHILA EXAMPLE: Male seminal fluid contains proteins (ACPs) that (1) suppress female remating with other males, (2) increase female egg-laying rate, and (3) kill sperm of previously mated males. These adaptations BENEFIT the male (by monopolizing paternity and accelerating reproduction). HOWEVER, the same chemicals ALSO SHORTEN FEMALE LIFESPAN and REDUCE HER LIFETIME REPRODUCTIVE SUCCESS. Females experience selection to resist the harmful effects, potentially by evolving detoxifying enzymes or refusing to mate. This drives reciprocal evolution between male offense and female defense — a form of intragenomic arms race. Sexual conflict is one of the most important insights of modern sexual selection theory.

quiz.question: A species where females mate with many males during one estrus period is most likely to have evolved:
quiz.correct: Relatively large testes to produce more sperm for competition
distractors: Elaborate male display ornaments for female choice
Complete monogamy
Reduced sexual dimorphism

visual.mnemonic: RCOP: Remove, Cooperate, Out-produce, Prevent.
visual.trap: Testis size (relative to body mass) is a strong predictor of mating system — promiscuous species have large testes, monogamous species have small ones.

v2.definition: Sperm competition occurs when females mate with multiple males, selecting for sperm traits and antagonistic male strategies.

mnemonic.explanation: Males evolve plugs, displacement structures, and mate guarding when females mate with multiple partners.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Sperm competition only happens after mating.' It begins WHEN females mate with multiple males, before AND after copulation.

actions.quizPrompt: How does polyandry drive the evolution of sperm plugs?
actions.flashcardBack: Female reproductive tract biases which sperm fertilizes the egg.

Life History Evolution
Reproductive strategies after conception are extremely diverse

Laws of physics make this an impossibility

Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (alt text: "The Thrip Mite (Adactylidium), a Tale of Incest and Matricide! Adactylidium is a genus of mites known for its unusual life cycle. The pregnant female mite feeds upon a single egg of a thrips, growing five to eight female offspring and one male in her body. The offspring devour their mother from the inside out, and the single male mite mates with all the daughters when they are still in the mother. The females, now impregnated, cut holes in their mother’s body so that they can emerge to find new thrips eggs. The male emerges as well, but does not look for food or new mates, and dies after a few hours. The females die at the age of 4 days, when their own offspring eat them alive from the inside. (text via: Wikipedia) (photo via: Suededeutsche)")

sections.label: Slide 7: Trade-Offs Involving Energy and Time
sections.body:
Trade-Offs Involving Energy and Time
Strategy 1
Maximize size and/or condition at reproduction by allocating energy to growth for a long time.
Benefits
Reach a larger a size enabling production of more or larger offspring.
Costs or trade-offs
An individual that takes a long time to grow is exposed for a longer time to predators, disease, or accidents, and incurs a greater risk of never having reproduced at all.

SPEAKER NOTES: K-selected strategy

sections.label: Slide 8: Trade-Offs Involving Energy and Time
sections.body:
Trade-Offs Involving Energy and Time
Strategy 2
Minimize time to reproductive age by shifting energy to reproduction at a younger age
Benefits
Begin reproduction sooner and perhaps produce more offspring over lifespan

Less likely to die without having reproduced at all
Costs or trade-offs
Mature at smaller size and so produce smaller or fewer offspring with each bout of reproduction

Early maturation may lead to decreased lifespan, which can also limit total reproduction success

SPEAKER NOTES: R-selected strategy

sections.label: Slide 9: Trade-Offs Involving Energy and Time
sections.body:
Trade-Offs Involving Energy and Time
Energy and time devoted to one activity cannot be devoted to another activity
Natural selection should favor an optimal balance between the benefits and costs so as to maximize lifetime reproductive success

Different balances will be optimal in different environments

Environmental variation (i.e., local conditions) should influence life history variation among living organisms

sections.label: Slide 10: Life history strategies
sections.body:
Life history strategies
Strategies that organisms use to invest energy into different functions
Growth
Maintenance and repair
Immune system
Reproduction
Traits that affect fitness across a lifetime, rather than a single reproductive period
Age at first reproduction
Duration of reproduction
Number and size of offspring
Life span

examples: Life history: the schedule of birth, maturation, reproduction, and death.
r-strategy: many small offspring, little parental care, fast maturation (insects, weeds).
K-strategy: few large offspring, heavy parental care, slow maturation (elephants, humans, oak trees).
Trade-offs: energy spent on reproduction CANNOT be spent on growth or survival. Time spent finding a mate cannot be spent foraging.
popup.mnemonic: r vs K: rabbits (r) vs K-apes. Live fast, die young OR slow and steady.

flashcard.front: What are the classic r vs K life history strategies, and what trade-off underlies the distinction?
flashcard.back: The r/K strategy framework describes two ends of a continuum of life-history strategies. r-SELECTED species (named for r in population growth models) live in unstable or unpredictable environments. They evolve FAST reproduction: many small offspring, rapid maturation, little parental care, short lifespan. Examples: insects, dandelions, mice. Each offspring has low survival probability, but sheer numbers compensate. K-SELECTED species (K = carrying capacity) live in stable, competitive environments. They evolve SLOW reproduction: few large offspring, long maturation, heavy parental investment, long lifespan. Examples: elephants, humans, oak trees. Each offspring has high survival probability. The UNDERLYING TRADE-OFF is that energy/time devoted to reproduction cannot be used for growth or maintenance, and vice versa. The r/K framework is now considered a simplification — modern life history theory uses continuous traits and optimization models — but it remains a useful conceptual starting point.

quiz.question: An r-selected species is most likely to:
quiz.correct: Produce many small offspring with little parental care and mature quickly
distractors: Produce few large offspring with heavy parental care
Have very long lifespans
Live in highly stable environments

visual.mnemonic: r = reckless; K = careful.
visual.trap: r/K is a continuum, not a binary. Most species are somewhere between the extremes.

v2.definition: Life history is the schedule of birth, growth, reproduction, and death shaped by trade-offs between competing fitness components.

mnemonic.hook: Many cheap kids or few quality
mnemonic.explanation: r-selected species flood with offspring while K-selected species invest deeply in fewer survivors.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'r and K strategies are absolute categories.' They are ENDPOINTS of a continuum; most species blend both strategies.

actions.quizPrompt: Explain the trade-off between offspring number and offspring quality.
actions.flashcardBack: r equals many small fast offspring; K equals few large slow offspring.

Extrinsic mortality – mortality due to external events
Predation

Extrinsic mortality rate – rate at which external events lead to the death of individuals in a population
Probability of survival to any given age declines with time

sections.label: Slide 15: Pleiotropic effects & life history
sections.body:
Pleiotropic effects & life history
Pleiotropy: Mutation in a single gene affects expression of many phenotypes
Antagonistic pleiotropy: Mutation that has beneficial effects for one trait, but causes detrimental effects on other traits

Antagonistic effects can occur at different life stages

Selection will favor mutations that have a beneficial effect early in life, even if they have a detrimental effect later

High extrinsic mortality rates favor short lifespan
No benefit to late age reproduction
High extrinsic mortality
=
Populations would acquire mutations that raise fitness early in life, even if they had antagonistic pleiotropic effects late in life

Might explain why opossums reproduce early and die at a young age
Older individuals experience deleterious effect

Populations with high extrinsic mortality
Selection would favor:
Rapid growth (age rapidly)
Early sexual maturation
Large number of offspring  per litter
Shortened lifespan

These would maximize fitness if they have a short time to live
Opposite predictions for populations with low extrinsic mortality

Mainland opossum population with many predators
vs.
Sepelo Island opossum population with no predators

sections.label: Slide 24: Why do organisms age?
sections.body:
Why do organisms age?
Aging or senescence: The deterioration in the biological functions of an organism
Aging reduces an individual’s fitness, and should be opposed by natural selection

Key Point
Natural selection operates more strongly on mutations that benefit fitness early in life than those that benefit fitness late in life
Mortality reduces probability of survival throughout life
Selection is weak late in life

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture2.jpg" (originally from URL: http://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0065266003010022-gr1.gif)

sections.label: Slide 25: Why do organisms age?
sections.body:
Why do organisms age?
Mutation accumulation hypothesis
For late-life traits, selection is not strong enough to purge deleterious mutations
Result: Mutations that have a deleterious effect later in life build up in the genome

Deleterious mutations early in life will be purged
Individuals that live long enough, will experience the negative effects (senescence)

sections.label: Slide 26: Why do organisms age?
sections.body:
Why do organisms age?
Antagonisitic pleiotropy hypothesis
Allele has beneficial effect early in life, but deleterious effect late in life
Result: Pleiotropic alleles will be favored by selection because the benefit occurs early in life when selection acts strongest
Individuals that live long enough, will experience the negative effects (senescence)
In both hypotheses:
The rate of senescence is influenced by the rate of extrinsic mortality

sections.label: Slide 27: Why do humans experience so many age-related diseases?
sections.body:
Why do humans experience so many age-related diseases?

100,000 people per day die of age-related diseases

Most pronounced in industrialized nations (~90%)
CDC Data!

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg" (alt text: "Increase of age-related diseases in Humans during aging. | Download Scientific Diagram")
Image 2: File "Picture2.jpg" (alt text: "Products - Data Briefs - Number 26 - December 2009")

examples: High extrinsic mortality (predators, disease) favors fast reproduction and short lifespan — there's no point "saving" resources for old age if you probably won't reach it.
Low extrinsic mortality favors slow development and longer lifespan — energy investment in maintenance pays off.

Reznick's guppy experiment: high-predation Trinidad populations evolve faster maturation and higher reproductive rates than low-predation populations. Experimentally confirmed in 11 years after transplant.
MEDAWAR'S THEORY: mutations with late-life harmful effects escape selection because most individuals die before expressing them.
WILLIAMS' antagonistic pleiotropy theory: genes beneficial early in life can be maintained even if harmful later.
warnings: Aging is NOT inevitable in all species — some organisms (hydra, some clams, lobsters) show negligible senescence.
popup.mnemonic: MEDI: Mutation accumulation + Extrinsic mortality → Declining selection → Inevitable aging.

flashcard.front: Why does high extrinsic mortality favor the evolution of shorter lifespans and earlier reproduction?
flashcard.back: EXTRINSIC MORTALITY = death from environmental sources (predation, disease, accidents, starvation) that are NOT age-related. When extrinsic mortality is HIGH, few individuals survive to old age regardless of their biology — so selection for traits that extend life has little effect (because the life extension never happens before extrinsic death). Instead, selection strongly favors EARLY, RAPID REPRODUCTION to pass on genes before random death strikes. GUPPY EXPERIMENTS (Reznick et al.): In Trinidad streams, guppies below waterfalls face predation from pike cichlids (high extrinsic mortality) and evolve FAST maturation and HIGH reproductive rates. Guppies above waterfalls face killifish (low extrinsic mortality) and evolve SLOW maturation and LOWER reproductive rates. When Reznick experimentally transplanted low-predation guppies to high-predation sites, within 11 years (roughly 30 guppy generations) they evolved the fast life history of native high-predation fish. This is a textbook demonstration that extrinsic mortality shapes life history evolution.

quiz.question: Reznick's guppy transplant experiment showed that after 11 years in high-predation streams, guppies evolved:
quiz.correct: Faster maturation and earlier, higher reproductive rates
distractors: Longer lifespans and delayed reproduction
Increased body size and reduced fertility
No detectable evolutionary change

visual.mnemonic: Dangerous world → live fast. Safe world → live slow.
visual.trap: Aging is not "programmed" — it emerges from weakened selection at older ages, not from an evolved death program.

v2.definition: Senescence evolves because selection weakens with age, allowing late-acting deleterious mutations to accumulate beyond reproduction.

mnemonic.explanation: Selection cannot remove harmful mutations that act after reproduction is finished, so aging accumulates.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Aging is wear and tear.' Aging EVOLVES because selection weakens with age, not because cells simply break down over time.

actions.quizPrompt: Why do mice age faster than naked mole rats?
actions.flashcardFront: Williams' antagonistic pleiotropy theory of aging?
actions.flashcardBack: Genes good for early reproduction harm late life and persist anyway.

sections.label: Slide 41: Life-history evolution take-home points
sections.body:
Life-history evolution take-home points
Energy is finite and must be allocated to different functions
These functions differ throughout life span
Different environments select for different allocation strategies that will enhance lifetime reproductive success
High extrinsic mortality rates favor ‘faster pace of life’ (i.e., grow fast early, reproduce young, short lifespan)
Selection acts stronger on mutations expressed at an early age
Selection favors flexible allocation strategies in unpredictable environments

VISUALS: None.


###############################################

examples: Parental investment: any contribution of resources or time to one offspring that reduces the parent's ability to invest in others.
Sex role reversal: pipefish and seahorses — males carry offspring in brood pouches; females compete for male mates.
Female-biased operational sex ratios create male choice and female competition.
Fisher's principle: at equilibrium, parents should invest equally in males and females. If one sex is rare, selection favors producing more of it.
Trivers-Willard: parents in good condition should invest in sons (in species where male RS depends on condition); poor-condition parents should invest in daughters (safer reproductive return).
popup.mnemonic: Fisher says 50/50. Trivers-Willard says adjust by condition. Seahorses reverse it all.

flashcard.front: State Fisher's principle of sex allocation and explain why the equilibrium sex ratio is 1:1.
flashcard.back: FISHER'S PRINCIPLE (R.A. Fisher 1930): at evolutionary equilibrium, parents should invest equally in male and female offspring, which produces (roughly) a 1:1 SEX RATIO at sexual maturity. REASONING: Suppose males become rare. Each male then has higher mating success than each female (because there are fewer males to share the females). Parents who happen to produce more sons gain a fitness advantage because their sons have higher per-capita reproductive success. Selection favors producing more sons, UNTIL males stop being rare. The reverse applies if females become rare. The only STABLE (evolutionarily stable strategy) equilibrium is when producing either sex yields equal expected fitness returns — which happens when the SEX RATIO is 1:1 (weighted by INVESTMENT cost, not counts, if sexes are unequally costly). This explains why most species with genetic sex determination produce ~50/50 sex ratios despite the apparent waste of producing "surplus" males.

quiz.question: In a pipefish species where males carry offspring in a brood pouch and females compete for males, who is expected to be the choosier sex?
quiz.correct: Males, because they invest more per offspring and have limited brood capacity
distractors: Females, because they produce eggs
Neither — pipefish mate randomly
Both are equally choosy

visual.mnemonic: Fisher = baseline. Trivers-Willard = condition. Pipefish = reverse.
visual.trap: Fisher's principle is about INVESTMENT not COUNT. If males are cheaper to produce, more males are expected.

v2.definition: Parental investment and sex allocation are shaped by Trivers-Willard theory and Fisher's principle of equal sex-ratio investment.

mnemonic.explanation: Fisher's principle says parents should INVEST equally in sons and daughters, not necessarily produce equal counts.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Sex ratios are always 50:50.' They equalize INVESTMENT, not numbers; if sons cost twice as much, expect 2:1 daughter bias.

actions.quizPrompt: How does the Trivers-Willard hypothesis predict offspring sex ratios?
actions.flashcardBack: Parents should invest equally in male and female offspring (1:1 by cost).

Evolution of Social Behavior

sections.label: Slide 2: Evolution of Social Behavior (in brief)
sections.body:
Evolution of Social Behavior (in brief)
Goals
To understand the difference between individual selection and group selection

To explain why selfish behaviors will ‘takeover’ cooperative behaviors

To describe how kin selection can give rise to altruistic behavior

sections.label: Slide 3: Evolutionarily stable strategy (ESS)
sections.body:
Evolutionarily stable strategy (ESS)
Behavioral strategy, if adopted by most members of a population, that cannot be invaded by any alternative behavioral strategy (in a given environment)
Once fixed in a population, natural selection alone is sufficient to prevent alternative (mutant) strategies from replacing it
Although this does not preclude the possibility that a better strategy, or set of strategies, will emerge in response to selective pressures resulting from environmental change.

sections.label: Slide 4: Many male strategies maintained by negative frequency-dependent selection
sections.body:
Many male strategies maintained by negative frequency-dependent selection
Three male reproductive strategies in a single population

											              Rock – Paper – Scissors game
Male side-blotched lizards

sections.label: Slide 6: Game theory
sections.body:
Game theory
Mathematical approach to studying behavior
Optimal decision in strategic situation when payoff depends on choices of others

VISUALS: Image 1: File "Picture4.jpg"

sections.label: Slide 7: Natural selection and social behavior
sections.body:
Natural selection and social behavior
Selection on groups or individuals?
Individual selection (selfishness)
Differential fitness of individuals
Favors alleles that contribute to individual fitness
Genotypes outcompete other genotypes
Group selection (good of the species logic)
Differential fitness of groups of individuals
Favors alleles that contribute to group performance
Groups outcompete other groups
Still a strongly debated topic in evolutionary biology

examples: ESS: a strategy that, if adopted by most of the population, cannot be invaded by a rare alternative.
Hawk-Dove game (Maynard Smith 1973): hawks fight for resources, doves share. At equilibrium, a mixed population of hawks and doves is stable.
Rock-paper-scissors dynamics: side-blotched lizards with orange/yellow/blue throat morphs maintained by negative frequency-dependent selection.
Game theory assumes frequency-dependent payoff — your fitness depends on what others are doing.
popup.mnemonic: ESS = Evolutionarily Stable Strategy. Stable = can't be invaded by a rare mutant.

flashcard.front: What is an Evolutionarily Stable Strategy (ESS) and why can a population of hawks-only or doves-only NOT be an ESS in the Hawk-Dove game?
flashcard.back: An EVOLUTIONARILY STABLE STRATEGY (ESS; Maynard Smith & Price 1973) is a strategy that, once common in a population, CANNOT be invaded by any rare alternative mutant strategy — that is, a rare mutant playing something different has LOWER fitness than the common strategy. HAWK-DOVE GAME: Hawks always fight over resources (high win-or-lose payoff). Doves always share/retreat (low but safe payoff). (1) PURE HAWK POPULATION: Every contest results in a fight, so every hawk suffers expected injury. A rare DOVE mutant avoids all fights and gets lower but consistent payoff — sometimes higher than the hawks. So Dove INVADES — hawk-only is NOT an ESS. (2) PURE DOVE POPULATION: Every contest is shared peacefully. A rare HAWK mutant takes all resources without competition and has much higher fitness than doves. Hawk INVADES — dove-only is NOT an ESS. (3) MIXED EQUILIBRIUM: At a specific ratio of hawks to doves, both strategies have equal expected fitness, and neither can invade. This mixed ratio IS the ESS — and shows how multiple strategies can coexist within a single species.

quiz.question: Why are side-blotched lizards (with orange, yellow, and blue throat morphs) maintained in a rock-paper-scissors pattern?
quiz.correct: Negative frequency-dependent selection — each morph is favored when the morph it beats is common
distractors: Random mutation constantly generates new morphs
Genetic drift randomly shifts morph frequencies
The environment changes each year

visual.mnemonic: Rock beats scissors beats paper beats rock — cycles without winner.
visual.trap: Frequency-dependent selection can maintain multiple alleles indefinitely — heterozygote advantage is only ONE way genetic variation is maintained.

nodes.lectureTitle: Game Theory, Altruism, and Kin Selection

v2.definition: An evolutionarily stable strategy (ESS) is a behavior that, once common, cannot be invaded by any rare alternative strategy.

mnemonic.explanation: An ESS is a strategy locked in by frequency dependence: rare alternatives always do worse and disappear.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'ESS means optimal strategy.' ESS means UNINVADABLE in current population, often suboptimal compared to coordinated alternatives.

actions.quizPrompt: Why does hawk-dove produce a stable mix instead of all hawks or all doves?
actions.flashcardBack: A strategy that, when common, no rare mutant strategy can invade.

nodes.title: Levels of Selection: Group vs Individual
nodes.subtitle: Why selection mostly acts on individuals (Lec 13 slides 8-13)

popup.heading: Lecture 13 — Levels of Selection

sections.label: Slide 8: Selection on groups or individuals?
sections.body:
Selection on groups or individuals?
Group selection logic:
Adaptations minimize the chances that the species or populations as a whole would go extinct

Individuals cooperate to enhance group success
…But models and empirical evidence suggest:

Individuals with mutations that make them behave selfishly will take over a group of cooperators

sections.body:
A thought exercise
2 bird populations
A
Birds lay 6 eggs per

B

sections.label: Slide 11: Selection operates on the individual
sections.body:
Selection operates on the individual
But why are cooperative behaviors and social groups so common?
Benefits
Increased vigilance
Dilution effect (safety in numbers, selfish herd)
Enhanced predator defense capability
Cooperative foraging/hunting
Improved defense of critical resources

Costs
Increased conspicuousness to predators
Increased food competition
Increased mate competition
Decreased certainty of paternity
Increased disease/parasite transmission

sections.label: Slide 12: Group hunting in wild dogs
sections.body:
Group hunting in wild dogs

Each dog gets more energy per day if it belongs to a larger pack

examples: Classical group selection (Wynne-Edwards) proposed that traits "good for the species" evolve because groups with those traits outcompete others.
Williams' critique (1966): naive group selection usually fails because individual-level selection is stronger — selfish individuals in a group invade and destroy group traits.
Group hunting in African wild dogs works not because it helps the species but because each individual hunter gets more food than hunting alone.
Cliff swallows: colonies provide anti-predator vigilance, but each individual benefits from being in the group.
popup.mnemonic: Selection acts on the INDIVIDUAL. "Good for the species" is a common but wrong framing.

flashcard.front: Why is "group selection" controversial and why does most modern evolutionary biology emphasize individual-level selection?
flashcard.back: GROUP SELECTION hypothesizes that traits evolve because they benefit the GROUP (or species), even if they reduce individual fitness. CLASSICAL EXAMPLE: "Lemmings leap off cliffs to prevent overpopulation." PROBLEM (Williams 1966): individual selection is much STRONGER than group selection in most cases, because the fitness differences between individuals WITHIN a group are larger and change faster than fitness differences between groups. A "selfish" individual who does NOT cooperate gains an immediate fitness advantage within the group — their genes spread. Within a few generations, the cooperative trait is replaced by the selfish one. For group selection to work, groups must have very restricted gene flow, very high extinction/founding rates, and the group-beneficial trait must be strongly heritable at the group level. Modern biology generally explains "group-like" behavior through (1) KIN SELECTION (helping relatives who share your genes), (2) RECIPROCAL ALTRUISM (I help you expecting future help), or (3) MUTUALISTIC BENEFIT (cooperation that also helps the cooperator directly). MODERN MULTI-LEVEL SELECTION theory accepts some group selection under narrow conditions but emphasizes that most apparent "group selection" is really selection at lower levels.

quiz.question: A population of wolves hunts in packs. What level of selection best explains pack hunting?
quiz.correct: Individual selection — each wolf gets more food per hunt when hunting as a pack than alone
distractors: Group selection only — for the good of the pack
Kin selection only — because wolves are related
Pure altruism with no individual benefit

visual.mnemonic: Gene → Individual → Kin → Rarely group.
visual.trap: Do NOT say a trait evolved "for the good of the species" unless you can rule out individual-level explanations.

v2.definition: Selection mostly acts on individuals, not groups, because cheaters within groups exploit altruists faster than group-level selection.

mnemonic.explanation: Groups of altruists do better than groups of cheaters, but within any group, cheaters always win.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Animals act for the good of the species.' Selection acts on INDIVIDUALS; species-level benefit is a misconception called naive group selection.

actions.flashcardFront: Why selection mostly acts on individuals?

examples: Coefficient of relatedness (r): the probability that two individuals share an allele by descent. Full siblings: r = 0.5. Half siblings: r = 0.25. First cousins: r = 0.125.
Hamilton's rule: altruism evolves when rB > C, where r = relatedness, B = benefit to recipient, C = cost to actor.
Inclusive fitness: an individual's total fitness = direct fitness (own reproduction) + indirect fitness (from helping relatives reproduce).
Haldane: "I would lay down my life for two brothers or eight cousins" — illustrates rB > C logic.
Haplodiploidy in Hymenoptera: sisters are more related to each other (r = 0.75) than to their own offspring (r = 0.5), which may have facilitated eusociality in ants, bees, wasps.
popup.mnemonic: Hamilton's rule: rB > C. Relatedness × Benefit > Cost.

flashcard.front: State Hamilton's rule and use it to explain why an alarm call that alerts relatives to a predator can evolve despite putting the caller at risk.
flashcard.back: HAMILTON'S RULE (1964): an altruistic behavior can evolve whenever rB > C, where r = the COEFFICIENT OF RELATEDNESS between actor and recipient (probability of sharing an allele by descent), B = the FITNESS BENEFIT received by the beneficiary, and C = the FITNESS COST to the altruist. APPLICATION TO ALARM CALLS: Suppose a ground squirrel spots a hawk. Calling out attracts the hawk's attention (COST = increased risk of predation on the caller). But the alarm alerts nearby squirrels who escape to burrows (BENEFIT to them). If the nearby squirrels are RELATIVES (mother, siblings, cousins), they share many alleles with the caller — so genes for alarm-calling behavior benefit copies of themselves in the bodies of saved relatives. When rB > C (where r is typically averaged across all beneficiaries), alarm-calling spreads even though individual callers suffer. This is INCLUSIVE FITNESS: the actor's fitness = own direct reproduction + indirect reproduction through helped relatives. HALDANE QUOTE: "I would lay down my life for two brothers [r = 0.5, 2 × 0.5 = 1.0] or eight cousins [r = 0.125, 8 × 0.125 = 1.0]" — the math of kin selection in one joke.

quiz.question: According to Hamilton's rule, an altruistic behavior is favored when:
quiz.correct: rB > C, where r is the coefficient of relatedness, B is the benefit to the recipient, and C is the cost to the actor
distractors: B > rC — benefit exceeds cost scaled by relatedness
r + B > C — relatedness plus benefit exceeds cost
r > B/C — relatedness exceeds benefit-to-cost ratio

visual.mnemonic: Two brothers or eight cousins.
visual.trap: Altruism doesn't require conscious calculation — the rB > C condition operates through selection over evolutionary time.

nodes.lectureTitle: Game Theory, Altruism, and Kin Selection

v2.definition: Hamilton's rule rB > C explains altruism: helping relatives spreads helper alleles when benefit times relatedness exceeds cost.

mnemonic.explanation: Help kin when relatedness times benefit exceeds your own cost, the math behind every altruistic act.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Altruism is selfless.' Altruism evolves only when it serves the gene's COPIES in relatives (rB greater than C).

actions.quizPrompt: Apply Hamilton's rule to a worker bee giving up reproduction.
actions.flashcardBack: rB greater than C: relatedness times benefit must exceed cost for altruism to evolve.

examples: Special Creation: ~6,000 years (incorrect — contradicted by all physical evidence).
Darwin's estimate: several billion years based on sediment accumulation rates. Remarkably close for the 1800s.
Lord Kelvin: ~20 million years based on rock cooling rates. WRONG because Kelvin didn't know Earth's interior generates heat via radioactive decay.
Modern answer: 4.568 billion years via radiometric dating of the oldest zircon crystals (4.4 By old, Western Australia).
Counting to 1 million seconds = 11 days. Counting to 1 billion seconds = 31 years.
warnings: Kelvin's math was not wrong — his ASSUMPTION (that Earth was a homogeneous cooling rock) was wrong. Always check assumptions.
A half-life is a STATISTICAL property, not a deterministic one. You cannot predict when a single atom will decay, only the population rate.
popup.mnemonic: 4.568 billion years — Earth is old beyond human intuition. Deep time.

flashcard.front: How does radiometric dating work, and why is it trusted?
flashcard.back: Radiometric dating relies on radioactive isotopes decaying at a CONSTANT rate into daughter isotopes. The half-life (time for 50% of parent to decay) is fixed for each isotope — unaffected by temperature, pressure, or chemistry. To date a rock: (1) measure the ratio of parent to daughter isotope, (2) know the half-life, (3) calculate elapsed time. Multiple isotope systems (U-Pb, K-Ar, Rb-Sr, C-14) with different half-lives can be applied to the same sample as independent cross-checks. Zircon crystals trap uranium but exclude lead, making them excellent time capsules — the oldest known zircon (4.4 Bya, Western Australia) was dated using both U-235 and U-238 systems and gave the same age. Results also match tree rings and ice cores for recent dates.

quiz.question: Why was Lord Kelvin's estimate of Earth's age (20 million years) so far off?

He assumed Earth was older than it is

visual.trap: Carbon-14 dating only works for organic material up to ~50,000 years. For geology, use U-Pb, K-Ar, or Rb-Sr.

mnemonic.hook: Four point five six eight billion

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Carbon-14 dates dinosaurs.' Carbon-14 only works under 60,000 years; older fossils need uranium or potassium isotopes.
