sections.label: LECTURE AUDIO — Early Earth formation (brief mention, recorded 2026-04-01)
sections.body: gs as old as the solar system itself. How do we use this to get the age of something? The unstable radioactive element that you use is called the parent, and the stable heavy ice tube you end up with is called the daughter. Dating only works if the only source the daughter isotope is the decay of the parent You also need a rock that behaves a bit like a seal time capsule, preventing daughter isotopes from leaving and keeping the new parent isotopes from wandering in. That way, if you end up with half daughter and half topes, you know that one half life has gone by, since a rock kept everything locked into the same place. Certain minerals form a toilet cage around any larger ice tube. This locks everything in place and makes for an excellent time cap. Circum crystals, for example, contract atoms of radioactive uranium inside them, as well as its daughter isotope, lead. So measure the proportion of uranium's lead in the zircon crystal, and you can figure out when it must abort. Take this tiny zircon, for example, which would fit comfortably on the head of a pen. Believe it or not, this tiny zircon, which comes from western Australia, is the oldest piece of we've ever found. It's an absolutely mind 4,400,000,000 years old, and comes from when the was only a hundred and fifty million years after its formation. The zircon was dated using two different isotopes of uranium uranium two thirty five and uranium two thirty eight. This provided a built in cross check as you head two different clocks keeping time within the crystal. Whichever clock look at, they get the same result. Uranium has been trapped in that zircon since it formed four point four million years ago. Most dating

examples: Early Earth: no free oxygen, atmosphere of methane/hydrogen/ammonia/water/CO₂. Energy from lightning, UV, volcanoes, cosmic rays.
Murchison meteorite (1989, Australia): contained carbon, purines, pyrimidines — extraterrestrial inputs may have enriched the prebiotic soup.
Miller-Urey (1953): spark-discharge experiment produced 20+ amino acids including glycine, alanine, valine.
Sydney Fox (1977): heated amino acids → weak peptide bonds when added to water. Claudia Huber (1997): CO made stable peptide bonds.
RNA world hypothesis: RNA can BOTH store info AND catalyze reactions — likely predates DNA/protein life.
warnings: Abiogenesis is still an active research area — we know the building blocks can form, but the complete pathway from molecules → self-replicating cell is not fully solved.
Darwin 1871 letter: "a warm little pond with all sorts of ammonia and phosphoric salts, light, heat, electricity" — Darwin imagined it 80+ years before Miller-Urey tested it.
popup.mnemonic: Prebiotic soup: methane + ammonia + lightning = amino acids (Miller-Urey).

flashcard.front: What did the Miller-Urey experiment demonstrate, and why does it matter for origin-of-life theory?
flashcard.back: In 1953, Stanley Miller and Harold Urey built a closed apparatus simulating early Earth conditions: a flask of water (ocean), gases representing the early atmosphere (methane, ammonia, hydrogen, water vapor), and electric sparks (lightning). After running the experiment for a week, they analyzed the water and found 20+ amino acids — including glycine, alanine, and valine — the building blocks of proteins. This proved that ORGANIC molecules essential for life can form spontaneously from INORGANIC precursors under plausible early-Earth conditions. It didn't prove life originated this way, but it showed abiogenesis is chemically plausible. Later experiments by Sydney Fox and Claudia Huber showed those amino acids could also be strung into peptide chains.

quiz.question: What did the Miller-Urey experiment produce?
quiz.correct: Over 20 amino acids from inorganic precursors
distractors: A complete living cell
DNA from RNA
Proteins with catalytic activity

visual.mnemonic: Gas + spark → amino acids → peptides → RNA → life.
visual.trap: The early atmosphere composition is debated. Modern estimates include more CO₂ and N₂ than the original Miller-Urey mix — but amino acid production still occurs.

v2.definition: Life began through abiogenesis, with Miller-Urey showing amino acids form spontaneously and the RNA world preceding DNA-based life.

mnemonic.explanation: RNA can both store info AND catalyze reactions, so it likely arose first as the original self-replicator.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Life began as DNA.' RNA evolved first because it both stores genetic information AND can catalyze chemical reactions.

actions.quizPrompt: Why does the RNA-world hypothesis precede DNA-based life?
actions.flashcardBack: Amino acids form spontaneously from inorganic gases plus electricity.

examples: Biomarkers (3.8 Bya): DNA fragments, isotopic ratios (organic carbon has different ratios than volcanic carbon), molecules like okenane (lipid pigment).
Stromatolites (3.45 Bya): layered mounds formed when biofilm bacteria trap sediment. Still alive today in Shark Bay, Australia.
LUCA (Last Universal Common Ancestor): ~3.5-3.8 Bya. All extant life descends from this.
Archaea (3.5 By): single-celled, biochemically distinct from bacteria and eukaryotes.
Cyanobacteria (2.6 By): first photosynthesis with O₂ as byproduct. First atmospheric oxygen.
Eukaryotes (1.8 By): 100× bigger than bacteria. Endosymbiosis: archaeal cell engulfed a bacterium → mitochondrion. Second event → chloroplast.
Multicellularity evolved INDEPENDENTLY multiple times: animals, plants, fungi, red algae, slime molds.
First animals: sponges, 650-635 Mya.
warnings: The fossil record for microbial life is fragmentary. Biomarker evidence (isotope ratios in rocks) is often how we detect life before 2 Bya.
Endosymbiosis was proposed by Lynn Margulis in the 1960s and was initially rejected. Now it is textbook-established (mitochondria have their own DNA, reproduce by fission, have double membranes).
popup.mnemonic: LUCA → Archaea → Bacteria → Eukaryotes (via endosymbiosis). Multicellularity: many independent origins.

flashcard.front: How did eukaryotic cells arise, and what evidence supports endosymbiotic theory?
flashcard.back: Lynn Margulis's endosymbiotic theory proposes that eukaryotic cells formed when a larger archaeal cell engulfed (but did not digest) a free-living bacterium, which persisted inside as a mitochondrion. A later endosymbiosis brought in a cyanobacterium that became the chloroplast. Evidence: (1) Mitochondria and chloroplasts have their OWN circular DNA (like bacteria) separate from the nucleus. (2) They reproduce by binary fission, not with the cell cycle. (3) They have double membranes — the outer membrane from the host, the inner from the original bacterium. (4) Their ribosomes are bacterial (70S), not eukaryotic (80S). (5) Antibiotics targeting bacterial ribosomes also affect mitochondria. This was once radical; now it is textbook.

quiz.question: Which piece of evidence most strongly supports endosymbiotic theory?
quiz.correct: Mitochondria and chloroplasts have their own DNA and double membranes
distractors: All eukaryotes have mitochondria
Bacteria are smaller than eukaryotic cells
Mitochondria generate ATP

visual.mnemonic: 3.8 → 3.5 → 2.6 → 1.8 → 0.65 (Bya): life, oxygen, complexity, animals.
visual.trap: Oxygen almost killed early life — the Great Oxidation Event (~2.4 Bya) was a mass extinction for anaerobes. Obligate anaerobes retreated to oxygen-free niches.

v2.definition: Early life included stromatolite-forming bacteria 3.5 billion years ago, with eukaryotes by 2.1 Gya and animals by 600 Mya.

mnemonic.explanation: Life climbed four major levels of organization across three billion years before animals finally appeared.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Multicellularity evolved once.' Multicellularity evolved INDEPENDENTLY at least 25 times across plants, animals, fungi, and algae.

examples: Ediacaran (575-535 Mya): earliest definitive multicellular animals. Many body plans disappeared before the Cambrian.
Cambrian explosion (542-488 Mya): trilobites, early arthropods, first chordates (Haikouichthys, amphioxus-like). Huge burst of body plan diversity.
Burgess Shale (505 Mya, Canadian Rockies): 65,000+ specimens, 93 species. Anoxic mudslides preserved soft tissues. A "Lagerstätte" — exceptional fossil site.
Ordovician (488-444 Mya): early bony fish, first land plants (~475 Mya, moss/liverwort-like).
Silurian (444-416 Mya): first land animals (millipede ~428 Mya). Invertebrates came out of water before vertebrates.
Devonian (416-359 Mya): AGE OF FISHES. Earliest tetrapods (~370 Mya). Dunkleosteus — giant predatory fish up to 6 m long.
Carboniferous (359-299 Mya): AGE OF AMPHIBIANS. Massive coal deposits from the lush land plants. Synapsid/sauropsid split (~300-280 Mya).
Permian (299-251 Mya): first reptiles. Synapsids (→ mammals) vs sauropsids (→ reptiles, dinosaurs, birds). Skull window counts: anapsid, synapsid, diapsid.
warnings: "Age of X" means the period when X DOMINATES, not when X first appeared. E.g., Age of Fishes = Devonian, but fish originated in the Ordovician.
Synapsid ≠ reptile, even though "mammal-like reptiles" is sometimes used casually. Synapsids are a separate lineage from the reptile lineage (sauropsids).
popup.mnemonic: COME OVER SOME DAY MAYBE PICK UP HER VAUPHILIES — Cambrian, Ordovician, Silurian, Devonian, Mississippian, Pennsylvanian, Permian, Triassic, Jurassic, Cretaceous (not quite right but close to the professor's mnemonic).

flashcard.front: What was the Cambrian explosion, and why is the Burgess Shale so important?
flashcard.back: The Cambrian explosion (~542-488 Mya) was a relatively brief geological interval when most major animal PHYLA first appear in the fossil record. It represents a rapid burst of body-plan diversification: trilobites, early arthropods, brachiopods, mollusks, chordates, echinoderms all show up in the Cambrian. The Burgess Shale (Canadian Rockies, 505 Mya) is one of the most important fossil sites on Earth because (1) it captures a snapshot of an entire Cambrian ecosystem, (2) the anoxic conditions from underwater mudslides preserved SOFT tissues — normally fossils only preserve hard parts, (3) it contains 65,000+ specimens representing 93 species, including bizarre forms like Hallucigenia, Opabinia, and Anomalocaris that have no close modern relatives. Because of its exceptional preservation, Burgess Shale is called a "Lagerstätte" (German: storage place).

quiz.question: Which major event characterizes the Devonian period?
quiz.correct: Earliest tetrapods emerging onto land (Age of Fishes)
distractors: First appearance of trilobites
Origin of flowering plants
Mass extinction at the K-T boundary

visual.mnemonic: Cambrian Ordovician Silurian Devonian Carboniferous Permian.
visual.trap: The Permian ended with the LARGEST mass extinction in Earth's history (~251 Mya, ~96% of marine species lost) — even bigger than the K-T dinosaur extinction.

v2.definition: The Cambrian Explosion (541 Mya) produced almost all major animal body plans, beginning the Paleozoic vertebrate radiation.

mnemonic.explanation: Every major animal phylum showed up in a 20-million-year window 541 million years ago, then no new phyla since.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'The Cambrian Explosion was instantaneous.' It spanned roughly 20 million years, fast in geological time but slow in human terms.

actions.flashcardBack: 541 million years ago, producing nearly all modern animal phyla.

sections.label: Slide 36: Lecture take home points
sections.body:
Lecture take home points
Earth is about 4.5 billion years old.

Simple amino acids can be formed from inorganic material.

Earliest signs of life (biomarkers) occur about 3.8 billion years ago.

Multicellularity evolved many independent times, perhaps via “cooperative behaviors” among cells.

Fossil record provides clues to the history of life, but it isn’t perfect.

K-T boundary marks an extinction event due to asteroid crash.

examples: Triassic (250-202 Mya): first dinosaurs appear ~230 Mya. Dinosaurs dominate land up to ~65 Mya.
Jurassic (202-145 Mya): dinosaur diversification. First mammals (~200-180 Mya, small, nocturnal). Birds arise within Dinosauria (~150 Mya, Archaeopteryx).
Cretaceous (145-65 Mya): rise of flowering plants (~132 Mya). Coevolution of flowers and pollinators → massive insect diversification.

K-T boundary (65 Mya): asteroid impact at Chicxulub, Yucatán. ~10-mile diameter asteroid, ~100 trillion tons TNT equivalent. Iridium-rich layer in rocks worldwide. Global climate change → extinction of non-avian dinosaurs and ~2/3 of all species.
Cenozoic (65 Mya–now): mammals radiate into vacated niches. Primates (~50 Mya, squirrel-sized arboreal). Apes (~20 Mya) as climate cooled and grasslands spread.
Hominins ~7 Mya (bipedal primates). Homo sapiens ~195,000 years ago (oldest known fossils).
warnings: Birds ARE dinosaurs. They are the only surviving dinosaur lineage. The phrase "dinosaurs went extinct" is only true for non-avian dinosaurs.
The K-T boundary is now often called the K-Pg boundary (Cretaceous-Paleogene). "K" comes from German "Kreide" (chalk) because "C" was already taken by Cambrian.
Mammals existed ALONGSIDE dinosaurs for ~150 million years — they just stayed small and nocturnal while dinosaurs dominated day and large-body niches.
popup.mnemonic: K-T boundary: asteroid → iridium → Chicxulub crater → 2/3 extinction → mammalian radiation → us.

flashcard.front: What is the evidence that the K-T extinction was caused by an asteroid impact?
flashcard.back: Multiple lines of evidence converge: (1) A thin global layer of IRIDIUM — an element rare on Earth but common in asteroids — is found in rocks worldwide at exactly 65 Mya, proposed by Walter and Luis Alvarez in 1980. (2) The Chicxulub crater (Yucatán, Mexico), ~180 km across, was identified in the 1990s and dates to precisely 65 Mya. (3) Shocked quartz and microtektites (glass spherules) from the impact are found in the boundary layer. (4) The impact size (~10 mile diameter asteroid) releases energy equivalent to ~100 trillion tons of TNT — enough to throw debris globally, trigger massive fires, and block sunlight for years. (5) The global extinction pattern matches an impact-driven climate collapse: 2/3 of all species lost, preferentially large-bodied animals and anything dependent on photosynthesis. The non-avian dinosaurs went extinct; birds (a dinosaur lineage) survived.

quiz.question: Why is iridium the "smoking gun" for the K-T extinction being caused by an asteroid?
quiz.correct: Iridium is rare in Earth's crust but abundant in asteroids, and a global iridium layer dates to exactly 65 Mya
distractors: Iridium is toxic to dinosaurs specifically
Iridium only forms from impacts
Iridium was used by dinosaurs for camouflage

visual.mnemonic: Asteroid → iridium → climate collapse → mammalian radiation.
visual.trap: The K-T event is only one of 5 MAJOR mass extinctions. The biggest was the end-Permian (~251 Mya), wiping ~96% of marine species. We may be entering a 6th (anthropogenic).

v2.definition: The Mesozoic was the age of dinosaurs ending with the K-Pg asteroid impact 66 Mya, opening the Cenozoic mammal radiation.

mnemonic.explanation: A six-mile asteroid struck Yucatan 66 Mya, ending non-avian dinosaurs and opening niches for mammals to radiate.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'All dinosaurs died at K-Pg.' Bird dinosaurs survived and still live today; only NON-avian dinosaurs went extinct.

actions.quizPrompt: What evidence supports the asteroid extinction hypothesis?
actions.flashcardBack: 66 million years ago, ending non-avian dinosaurs.

sections.body:
Tree thinking
Components of a phylogenetic tree (or phylogeny)
Tips: terminal ends of an evolutionary tree (species, populations, molecules)

Branches: Lineages evolving through time between divergence events (speciation)
Nodes: The point where a lineage splits (a speciation event)
Root: Ancestor from which all other nodes descend
Clade: An organism and all of its descendents

examples: Tip: terminal ends of a tree — species, populations, or molecules being compared.
Branch: lineage evolving through time between divergence events (speciation).
Node: point where a lineage splits. Internal nodes = inferred common ancestors.
Root: ancestor from which all other nodes descend. Usually chosen with an outgroup.
Clade: an ancestor plus ALL of its descendants. A monophyletic group.
Sister taxa: two lineages that share one immediate common ancestor (one node apart).
Snail island example: populations can evolve in place (in situ) OR move to new islands. Current island 1 snails are NOT the direct ancestors of island 3 & 4 snails — evolution did not stop.
warnings: Trees can be drawn in MANY visual styles without changing the phylogeny. Rotations at any node are free — the topology is what matters.
NEVER read trees left-to-right as "progress." Humans are NOT at the "tip" of evolution — every living species is equally "evolved."
Tree orientation (horizontal, vertical, radial, circular) is a display choice, not a biological statement.
popup.mnemonic: Tip, Branch, Node, Root, Clade — the five words that describe any tree.

flashcard.front: Why is reading a phylogenetic tree left-to-right as "progress toward humans" (or any tip) a fundamental misconception?
flashcard.back: Phylogenetic trees show BRANCHING patterns of descent, not linear progress. Every species at the tips is equally "evolved" — they have all been evolving for the same amount of time since their last common ancestor. A tree can be rotated at ANY node without changing its biological meaning, which proves that the left-to-right order of tips has no biological significance. The "ladder of progress" or "scala naturae" view — where amoebae are "primitive" and humans are "advanced" — was explicitly rejected by Darwin, who famously wrote "never use the words higher and lower" in the margin of an early manuscript. Modern tree thinking requires reading from ROOT to TIPS, following actual ancestry, not left-to-right along the tips.

quiz.question: In a phylogenetic tree, what does a "node" represent?
quiz.correct: A point where a lineage splits (a common ancestor and a speciation event)
distractors: A living species
A fossil specimen
The root of the entire tree

visual.mnemonic: Root → Branches → Nodes → Tips.
visual.trap: A "clade" must include ALL descendants. If you exclude some descendants (e.g., "reptiles minus birds"), it is PARAPHYLETIC — not a valid clade.

v2.definition: Phylogenetic trees diagram evolutionary relationships using tips, nodes, branches, and roots to show common ancestry through time.

mnemonic.explanation: A tree shows relationships by branching pattern, not by tip order; rotate any branch and meaning stays.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Species closer on the page are more related.' Relatedness depends on shared NODES, not visual proximity.

actions.quizPrompt: How do you read a phylogenetic tree to find sister taxa?
actions.flashcardBack: A common ancestor plus all of its descendants (monophyletic group).

popup.heading: Lecture 15 — Cladistics: Building Trees from Characters

sections.label: Slide 13: Phylogeny and taxonomy
sections.body:
Phylogeny and taxonomy
Early naturalists (Linnaeus) named and classified organisms

Used shared characteristics
Essentially what taxonomists do today
Systematics: Scientific field devoted to classification of organisms
Synapomorphy:  A shared derived character. Inherited by all descendents of an ancestor
Taxonomy should reflect phylogeny
Morphological traits, but now DNA is used

Fish

Derived characters
Cladistics: phylogenetic methods used to construct phylogenies by using shared derived characteristics (synapomorphies)

sections.label: Slide 22: Which phylogeny is correct?
sections.body:
Which phylogeny is correct?

Principle of Parsimony: a criterion for selecting among alternative patterns based on minimizing the total amount of change or complexity
Most parsimonious tree is that with the fewest evolutionary steps
Important! - A phylogenetic tree is a hypothesis that can be tested with further evidence
Such as comparing molecular versus morphological trees

examples: Systematics: the scientific field of classification.
Synapomorphy: a shared derived character — inherited from a common ancestor by all descendants. The KEY tool for building clades.
Ancestral characters (symplesiomorphies) are NOT informative for defining relationships within a group because EVERY member has them.
Monophyletic group: an ancestor + ALL of its descendants. A valid clade.
Paraphyletic group: an ancestor + SOME descendants. NOT a valid clade.
Classic example: "reptiles" (excluding birds) is PARAPHYLETIC. To make it monophyletic, you must INCLUDE birds as reptiles — which is what modern taxonomy does (Aves is a lineage within Sauropsida).
Character matrix: rows = taxa, columns = characters (wings, antennae, elytra, mandibles). Identical rows group together, with an outgroup to root the tree.
With 5 taxa, there are 105 possible tree topologies. With 9 taxa, 2 million. With more, it becomes astronomical — requiring computers.
warnings: "Fish" as commonly used is paraphyletic — it excludes tetrapods (which descend from lobe-finned fish). A monophyletic "fish" group would include us.
Morphological characters can mislead if based on ancestral features. Always use DERIVED characters for clade-building.
Synapomorphy / homology / homoplasy — keep these terms straight. Homology = same due to common ancestry. Homoplasy = same NOT due to common ancestry.
popup.mnemonic: Synapomorphy = Shared + Derived. Ancestral doesn't count, derived does.

flashcard.front: Explain why "reptiles" (excluding birds) is a paraphyletic group, and how this violates cladistic principles.
flashcard.back: A MONOPHYLETIC group (clade) must include a common ancestor AND ALL of its descendants. "Reptiles" as traditionally defined (lizards, snakes, crocodiles, turtles, tuataras — but not birds) is PARAPHYLETIC because birds ARE direct descendants of the same reptilian ancestor that gave rise to crocodiles and dinosaurs. Phylogenetically, crocodiles are MORE closely related to birds than to lizards. To make "reptiles" monophyletic, birds MUST be included — hence modern taxonomy calls birds "avian reptiles" and places Aves within the clade Sauropsida/Archosauria. This is why cladists emphasize: definitions must reflect ACTUAL ancestry, not superficial similarity or traditional categories. "Fish" has the same problem — it excludes tetrapods, but tetrapods are descended from lobe-finned fish.

quiz.question: Why is "reptiles" (excluding birds) considered a paraphyletic group?
quiz.correct: It excludes birds, which are direct descendants of the reptilian common ancestor
distractors: Reptiles and birds evolved independently
Reptiles are a monophyletic group by definition
Birds diverged from mammals, not reptiles

visual.mnemonic: Mono = all. Para = some. Poly = random.
visual.trap: Linnaean groupings (Class Reptilia, etc.) were often paraphyletic. Modern cladistics has forced many revisions.

v2.definition: Cladistics builds trees from synapomorphies, shared derived characters that mark monophyletic groups inherited from common ancestors.

mnemonic.explanation: Only DERIVED shared traits prove common ancestry; ancestral shared traits like 'has DNA' tell you nothing.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Reptiles are a valid clade.' Reptiles are PARAPHYLETIC because they exclude birds, which descend from the same ancestor.

actions.quizPrompt: Why are synapomorphies more useful than plesiomorphies for building trees?
actions.flashcardBack: Mono includes ALL descendants; para excludes some descendants of a common ancestor.

sections.label: Slide 24: Homoplasy: Character state similarity, but NOT due to shared descent (i.e., not a homology)
sections.body:
Homoplasy: Character state similarity, but NOT due to shared descent (i.e., not a homology)
Two types of homoplasies:
Evolutionary reversals: the reversal of a derived character state back to its ancestral state
Convergent evolution: The independent origin of similar traits in separate evolutionary lineages, typically due to similar environmental pressures
Homoplasy can create the mistaken impression that two species are closely related when they are not

examples: Principle of parsimony (Occam's razor): the tree requiring the FEWEST evolutionary steps is the best hypothesis (starting point).
Phylogenetic trees are HYPOTHESES. New evidence can revise them — e.g., molecular data has reshuffled many morphological trees.
Homoplasy: similarity NOT due to common ancestry. Two types: evolutionary reversal (return to ancestral state) and convergent evolution (independent origin of similar trait).

Camera-eye convergence: vertebrate eyes and octopus eyes both have lens, retina, iris — but evolved INDEPENDENTLY (the tree shows separate origins requires fewer steps than "ancestral loss by all other groups").
Mammal aquatic reversal: whales, dolphins, and dugongs are mammals that returned to water. Their closest living relatives are hippopotamuses (molecular and fossil evidence).
Shark vs killer whale body shape: convergent, not related. Similar selective pressure (fast swimming) → similar form.
Limblessness in squamates: EVOLVED INDEPENDENTLY in snakes, glass lizards, legless skinks, amphisbaenians. Some snakes still retain vestigial hind-limb spurs (boas, pythons).
warnings: Parsimony is a starting assumption, NOT a guarantee. Real evolution can take non-parsimonious routes (e.g., when selection pressure is strong or ancestral states are truly rare).
Homoplasy is RAMPANT. Do not mistake convergent similarity for homology. Always test with independent character sets (morphology + molecules).
Molecular data has overturned many morphology-based groupings. When molecular and morphological trees conflict, molecular trees are usually (but not always) more reliable.
popup.mnemonic: Parsimony = fewest steps. Homoplasy = similarity that LIES (reversal or convergence).

flashcard.front: How does the camera eye of vertebrates and octopuses illustrate both parsimony and convergent evolution?
flashcard.back: Both vertebrates and octopuses have complex camera eyes with a lens, retina, iris, and focusing mechanism. If camera eyes were HOMOLOGOUS (inherited from a common ancestor), then the common ancestor of vertebrates and cephalopods (~600 Mya) must have had a camera eye — and EVERY other lineage descending from that ancestor (arthropods, echinoderms, flatworms, annelids, etc.) must have LOST it. That would require many evolutionary steps. Under the CONVERGENT hypothesis, the camera eye evolved TWICE — once in the vertebrate lineage, once in the cephalopod lineage — requiring only 2 steps. Parsimony favors the convergent interpretation. Additionally, cephalopod eyes have their photoreceptors facing the light (better design), while vertebrate eyes have them facing backward (creating the blind spot) — strong evidence of independent origin through different evolutionary paths.

quiz.question: The principle of parsimony in phylogenetics states that:
quiz.correct: The best hypothesis requires the fewest evolutionary steps
distractors: The simplest organism is the ancestor
Evolution always takes the shortest path
Older lineages have fewer mutations

visual.mnemonic: Homology = real kinship. Homoplasy = false resemblance.
visual.trap: Two species sharing a trait does NOT prove common ancestry. Always test with multiple independent characters.

v2.definition: Parsimony picks the tree requiring fewest character changes, but homoplasy from convergence and reversal can mislead the analysis.

mnemonic.explanation: Convergence and reversals create matching traits that came from independent origins, not from common ancestry.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Similar traits always indicate close relatives.' Convergence (dolphins and fish) creates similarity from completely independent evolutionary origins.

actions.quizPrompt: How does convergence violate cladistic assumptions?
actions.flashcardBack: Pick the tree requiring the fewest evolutionary changes.

examples: Coelacanths: lobe-finned fish thought extinct since Cretaceous — found ALIVE in 1938 off South Africa. Living fossil, close living relative of tetrapods.
Lobe-finned fish have fin bones HOMOLOGOUS to tetrapod limb bones (humerus, radius/ulna). They are cousins, not ancestors, but they preserve the transitional anatomy.
Phylogenies generate HYPOTHESES. Given that tetrapods arose from lobe-finned fish in the Devonian (~370 Mya), transition fossils should exist in mid-Devonian coastal wetland rocks.
Neil Shubin + Ted Daeschler searched exactly those rocks in Northern Canada (Ellesmere Island).
Tiktaalik (2004): transitional fossil with weight-bearing elbows, bending wrist, neck (lost bony operculum), lateral line (still aquatic), eyes on top of head (looking up).
Tiktaalik could do "push-ups" — support body weight on its pectoral fins. Probably not walking on land, but could prop itself at the water surface to look around.
Transitional features: from fins → arms, from no neck → neck, from gills-only → lungs + gills, from aquatic ear → terrestrial ear.
warnings: Tiktaalik is NOT "our ancestor" in the strict sense — it's a cousin preserving transitional anatomy. The direct ancestor probably existed in the same environment but has not been found as a fossil.
"Missing link" is a misleading phrase — evolution doesn't have a single chain of links. Tiktaalik is ONE of many transitional forms (Acanthostega, Ichthyostega, Eusthenopteron all show stages).
popup.mnemonic: Phylogeny → prediction → dig in the right rocks → Tiktaalik (2004). Science works.

flashcard.front: How did Neil Shubin use phylogenetic reasoning to predict where transitional fish-to-tetrapod fossils would be found?
flashcard.back: Shubin and Daeschler reasoned: (1) Tetrapods (4-legged vertebrates) are closely related to lobe-finned fish based on phylogenetic analysis and existing fossil evidence. (2) The transition fish → tetrapod must have occurred during the mid-to-late DEVONIAN period (~380-360 Mya) based on the age of known early tetrapod fossils (Acanthostega, Ichthyostega). (3) The environment where this transition occurred should be COASTAL WETLANDS — shallow, warm, freshwater habitats where a transitional animal could benefit from push-up style locomotion. (4) Therefore, they needed to find mid-Devonian COASTAL WETLAND rocks that were accessible. They found exactly that in Northern Canada (Ellesmere Island, Nunavut). After multiple field seasons, in 2004 they discovered TIKTAALIK — a fish with weight-bearing elbows, bending wrist, a NECK (loss of bony operculum), and other "fishapod" features. This was a triumph of phylogenetically-guided prediction: science using theory to direct empirical discovery, rather than stumbling upon fossils by accident.

quiz.question: Which Tiktaalik feature most strongly suggests it could support its body weight?
quiz.correct: Weight-bearing elbows and a bending wrist
distractors: A dorsal fin
A long tail
Scales covering its body

visual.mnemonic: Fins → Fishapod (Tiktaalik) → Limbs.
visual.trap: Tiktaalik was NOT a land-walker. It still had gills, a lateral line, and was aquatic. "Walking on land" came later with Acanthostega and Ichthyostega.

v2.definition: Tiktaalik is a fish-tetrapod transitional fossil predicted by phylogeny and discovered in 375-million-year-old Devonian rocks.

mnemonic.hook: Fish wrist, fish neck, fish lung
mnemonic.explanation: Tiktaalik had a real wrist, neck, and lungs while still living in water, exactly the predicted fish-tetrapod intermediate.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Transitional fossils are missing.' Tiktaalik was PREDICTED by phylogeny, then found in the predicted age and rock layer.

actions.quizPrompt: How did phylogenetic prediction lead to the discovery of Tiktaalik?
actions.flashcardBack: 375 Mya fish-tetrapod transitional form (Devonian period).

 Insulation

sections.label: Slide 43: Another example of an exaptation
sections.body:
Another example of an exaptation
Swim bladders are homologous with lungs. Early lung (sac connected to the gut) was co-opted into a buoyancy organ.

In early fish, lungs likely functioned as a way to gulp air from the surface to supplement oxygen intake, especially in low-oxygen waters. Tetrapods (amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals) adapted their lungs for respiration, while many fish modified their lungs into swim bladders for buoyancy.

SPEAKER NOTES: Argument over the origin of the lung.

sections.label: Slide 44: Lecture take home points
sections.body:
Lecture take home points
Phylogenetic relationships are reconstructed using shared derived characteristics (synapomorphies)

A monophyletic group includes all the descendents from a common ancestor.

Homoplasy is a shared character state, but NOT due to common ancestry (convergence & reversal).

Phylogenies are hypotheses that can be further tested.

Phylogenies can help us track evolutionary changes in traits.

examples: Archaeopteryx (discovered 1860, ~145 Mya): classic transitional form between theropod dinosaurs and modern birds. Has feathers AND teeth, claws on wings, long bony tail.
Feathered non-flying dinosaurs: Velociraptor, Oviraptor, Tyrannosauroid relatives — small arms, could NOT have used feathers for flight. Feathers preceded flight.
Pre-flight feather functions: species recognition, mate attraction (color), insulation, egg incubation (Oviraptor was found brooding eggs, 1993).

Quill nodes: regularly-spaced bumps on the ulna where feather quills attach. Found on modern bird bones (turkey vulture example) AND on Velociraptor ulna fossils — direct evidence of feather attachment in non-flying dinosaurs.
Exaptation: a trait that originally evolved for one function, later co-opted for a NEW function. Feathers (insulation) → flight. Not the same as adaptation (trait evolved FOR its current function).

Another exaptation: swim bladders are HOMOLOGOUS to lungs. The earliest fish had primitive lungs to gulp air at the surface in low-oxygen water. Tetrapods kept lungs; many fish modified lungs into gas-filled buoyancy organs (swim bladders).
Flight stroke may have evolved as running-assist (flapping while chasing prey or escaping predators) — wing-assisted incline running (WAIR) is hypothesized as the bridge.
warnings: Exaptation does NOT require gene duplication (unlike co-option at the molecular level). The same structure is REPURPOSED without gaining a copy.
Birds ARE dinosaurs (theropod dinosaurs, specifically). They are the only surviving dinosaur lineage.
Do not confuse exaptation with LAMARCKISM. The feather-to-flight transition was driven by SELECTION at each stage, not by an organism "striving" toward flight.
popup.mnemonic: Exaptation = "ex-adaptation" — same trait, NEW function. Feathers → insulation → FLIGHT.

flashcard.front: What is exaptation, and how do feathers illustrate this concept?
flashcard.back: EXAPTATION is a trait that originally evolved via selection for ONE function, but was later CO-OPTED for a different function — with or without further modification. Coined by Gould and Vrba in 1982. Feathers are the classic example: early theropod dinosaurs (long before birds and flight) had feathers that likely served INSULATION (keeping warm in cold climates, since many were small) and/or DISPLAY (mate attraction, species recognition, egg incubation). Evidence: (1) Feathered non-flying dinosaurs like Velociraptor and Sinosauropteryx, which had arms too short to fly. (2) Oviraptor fossils found brooding their eggs (1993) — feathers useful for insulation. (3) Velociraptor ulna shows QUILL NODES — regularly-spaced bumps where feathers attached, matching modern birds. Flight evolved LATER, using the already-present feathers. Another example: swim bladders are HOMOLOGOUS to lungs — early fish used primitive lungs to gulp air at the surface; many fish lineages later repurposed those lungs into buoyancy organs. Exaptation is crucial because it explains how complex adaptations can "sneak up" on a new function.

quiz.question: Why is the evolution of feathers considered an example of EXAPTATION rather than adaptation for flight?
quiz.correct: Feathers evolved first for other functions (insulation, display), then were co-opted for flight
distractors: Feathers are made of the same protein as scales
All dinosaurs had feathers
Flight evolved before feathers

visual.mnemonic: Insulation → Display → Running-assist → Flight. Exaptation every step.
visual.trap: Do NOT say "feathers evolved FOR flight." That is a teleological error. Feathers evolved for OTHER reasons and were LATER useful for flight.

v2.definition: Feathers evolved before flight as insulation or display, then were co-opted for flight, a classic example of exaptation.

mnemonic.explanation: Feathers warmed dinosaurs before they ever flew; selection then co-opted them for gliding and flight.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Feathers evolved for flight.' Feathers evolved FIRST for insulation or display, then were co-opted (exapted) for flight much later.

actions.quizPrompt: Why is exaptation crucial for evolving complex novel traits?
actions.flashcardBack: Feathers evolved for insulation, then were co-opted for flight.

nodes.title: Species Concepts & Reproductive Isolation
nodes.subtitle: Defining species and the barriers that keep them apart (Ch13)

popup.heading: Species Concepts & Reproductive Isolation (Chapter 13)

sections.label: Species Concepts
sections.body: Biological Species Concept (Mayr): species are groups that can interbreed and produce fertile offspring. Morphological: defined by physical features. Phylogenetic: smallest monophyletic group on a tree. Each concept has strengths and limitations — BSC fails for asexuals, fossils.

sections.label: Reproductive Isolation Mechanisms
sections.body: Prezygotic barriers: habitat, temporal, behavioral, mechanical, gametic isolation — prevent mating or fertilization. Postzygotic barriers: hybrid inviability, hybrid sterility (mule = horse × donkey), hybrid breakdown. Prezygotic barriers are energetically cheaper to evolve.

sections.label: Speciation Modes
sections.body: Allopatric: geographic barrier splits population (most common). Sympatric: divergence within same range (e.g., host-race formation, polyploidy in plants). Parapatric: adjacent populations with partial contact. Reinforcement: selection strengthens prezygotic barriers when hybrids are unfit.

quotes: "Species are groups of actually or potentially interbreeding natural populations which are reproductively isolated from other such groups." — Ernst Mayr, 1942
examples: Galápagos finches — allopatric speciation across islands followed by secondary contact.
Apple maggot fly (Rhagoletis) — sympatric host-race formation within 150 years of apple introduction to North America.
warnings: Reproductive isolation doesn't require morphological difference (cryptic species). Conversely, similar-looking species can be fully isolated.
Polyploidy creates instant speciation in plants but is rare in animals.
popup.mnemonic: GAPS: Geographic isolation → Allopatric divergence → Prezygotic barriers form → Sympatric meeting fails.

flashcard.front: Why is allopatric speciation considered the most common mode, and what breaks the process if it doesn't go far enough?
flashcard.back: Allopatric speciation is most common because geographic barriers prevent gene flow, allowing independent divergence by drift and selection. If secondary contact occurs BEFORE reproductive isolation is complete, gene flow swamps divergence and the populations remerge. Complete reproductive isolation must evolve before contact for two species to persist.

quiz.question: Two plant populations grow on opposite sides of a river. After the river shifts course, they come into contact. Hybrids are viable but sterile. Which type of reproductive barrier is sterility?
quiz.correct: Postzygotic barrier — hybrid sterility prevents gene flow after fertilization
distractors: Prezygotic barrier — gametes fail to meet
Allopatric barrier — geographic separation prevents mating
Behavioral barrier — courtship signals differ

tooltips.plain: Any mechanism preventing gene flow between populations — the defining criterion for separate species.
Having more than two sets of chromosomes — common in plants, causes instant reproductive isolation.

visual.mnemonic: Allo = Other place. Sym = Same place. Allo needs a WALL; Sym needs a WALL in their HEADS.
visual.trap: Speciation doesn't require millions of years. Sympatric speciation via polyploidy or host-race formation can happen in decades.

v2.definition: Species concepts define what counts as a species, with the biological species concept relying on reproductive isolation between populations.

mnemonic.hook: Two pools, no flow, two species
mnemonic.explanation: Biological species are defined by reproductive isolation: no gene flow means independent evolutionary trajectories.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'BSC works for all life.' BSC fails for asexual species, fossils, and ring species: each species concept has limits.

actions.quizPrompt: When does the biological species concept break down?
actions.flashcardBack: Allopatric needs geographic barrier; sympatric occurs without one.

nodes.title: Biogeography, Speciation & Extinction
nodes.subtitle: Geographic patterns of life, adaptive radiation, and mass extinctions (Ch14)

popup.heading: Biogeography, Speciation & Extinction (Chapter 14)

sections.label: Biogeography
sections.body: The study of geographic distribution of species. Island biogeography (MacArthur & Wilson): species richness on islands depends on island size and distance from mainland — equilibrium between immigration and extinction rates. Continental drift (plate tectonics) explains disjunct distributions — e.g., ratites (ostriches, emus, rheas) on separate continents from Gondwana breakup.

sections.label: Adaptive Radiation
sections.body: Rapid diversification of one lineage into many ecological niches, often after a mass extinction or colonization of new habitat. Examples: Cichlid fish in African Rift Lakes (~500 species in ~15,000 years), Darwin's finches, placental mammals post-Cretaceous extinction.

sections.label: Mass Extinctions & Punctuated Equilibrium
sections.body: Five major extinction events (Big Five). K-Pg extinction (66 Ma) killed ~76% of species. Extinctions reset ecological opportunity, enabling radiation of survivors. Punctuated equilibrium (Gould & Eldredge): long stasis punctuated by rapid change during speciation events — matches most fossil evidence. Current biodiversity crisis may constitute a 6th mass extinction.

quotes: "Macroevolution occurs by the same mechanisms as microevolution, just over longer time scales." — Zimmer & Emlen
examples: Cambrian Explosion (~541 Ma): most animal body plans appeared within ~20 million years.
Cichlids in Lake Victoria: ~500 species evolved in ~15,000 years after lake refilled.
warnings: Macroevolution is NOT a different mechanism from microevolution — same natural selection/drift acting over geological time.
Punctuated equilibrium doesn't deny natural selection — it describes tempo, not mechanism.
popup.mnemonic: MARE: Mass extinction → Adaptive Radiation → Ecological opportunity → new body plans.

flashcard.front: What is punctuated equilibrium, and what does it predict about the fossil record that gradualism does not?
flashcard.back: Punctuated equilibrium (Gould & Eldredge, 1972): evolution is characterized by long periods of morphological stasis punctuated by rapid bursts of change, usually associated with speciation events. Predicts the fossil record will show abrupt transitions rather than smooth gradients — which matches most fossil evidence. Gradualism predicts smooth, continuous change, which is rarely observed.

quiz.question: After a mass extinction event, surviving lineages often diversify explosively into newly vacant niches. This pattern is called:
quiz.correct: Adaptive radiation — rapid diversification to fill ecological opportunity
distractors: Punctuated equilibrium — long stasis followed by rapid change
Allopatric speciation — geographic isolation drives divergence
Convergent evolution — independent evolution of similar traits

visual.description: Major macroevolutionary events through deep time

tooltips.plain: Long periods with little morphological change in a lineage — common in the fossil record.
Rapid diversification of one ancestor into many species filling different ecological roles.

visual.mnemonic: CAMP: Cambrian animals explode. Mass extinctions Purge. K-Pg Mammals rise. Present — we're causing the next one.
visual.trap: The Cambrian Explosion took 20 MILLION years — 'rapid' in geological time. Don't confuse geological and human timescales.

v2.definition: Biogeography traces how geography and adaptive radiation shape species distributions, while mass extinctions reset diversity globally.

mnemonic.hook: Big Five: Ord, Dev, Per, Tri, K-Pg
mnemonic.explanation: Five mass extinctions hit Ordovician, Devonian, Permian, Triassic, and Cretaceous-Paleogene boundaries through Earth's history.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'The K-Pg was the worst extinction.' The PERMIAN-Triassic was Earth's worst, killing roughly 96 percent of marine species.

actions.quizPrompt: How does vicariance generate biogeographic patterns?
actions.flashcardBack: Permian-Triassic (252 Mya), killed about 96 percent of marine species.

nodes.title: Conservation & Humans as Selective Force
nodes.subtitle: How human activity drives evolution and threatens biodiversity (Ch8)

popup.heading: Conservation & Humans as Selective Force (Chapter 8)

sections.label: Humans as Evolutionary Agents
sections.body: Humans impose powerful selection pressures: artificial selection in agriculture/domestication, unintentional selection via hunting (trophy hunting selects for smaller tusks/horns), pollution (heavy metal tolerance in plants), urbanization (changes in bird song frequency, moth coloration). Antibiotic and pesticide resistance are direct results of human-imposed selection.

sections.label: Conservation Genetics
sections.body: Small populations lose genetic diversity through drift, reducing adaptive potential. Inbreeding depression increases homozygosity, exposing deleterious recessive alleles. Minimum viable population (MVP) size needed to maintain evolutionary potential. Genetic rescue: introducing individuals from other populations to restore diversity (e.g., Florida panther rescue with Texas pumas, 1995).

sections.label: Extinction & the 6th Mass Extinction
sections.body: Current extinction rates are ~100-1000x background rates. Habitat destruction, overexploitation, invasive species, climate change, and pollution are the main drivers (HIPCO). Island species are disproportionately vulnerable due to small populations and naivety to novel predators. Conservation strategies must account for evolutionary processes — preserving genetic diversity, maintaining gene flow corridors, managing evolutionary potential.

quotes: "The question is not whether evolution is relevant to conservation, but whether we can afford to ignore it." — adapted from conservation genetics literature
examples: Trophy hunting of bighorn sheep in Alberta selected for smaller horns over 30 years — rams' horn size declined ~20% because large-horned males were killed before reproducing.
Florida panther (Puma concolor coryi): rescued from inbreeding depression by introducing 8 Texas pumas in 1995 — survival and genetic diversity increased dramatically.
warnings: Genetic rescue (introducing outside genes) can sometimes cause outbreeding depression if populations are locally adapted to very different environments.
Minimum viable population estimates vary widely — 50/500 rule (50 for inbreeding, 500 for evolutionary potential) is a rough guideline, not a law.
popup.mnemonic: HIPCO: Habitat loss, Invasive species, Pollution, Climate change, Overexploitation — the five horsemen of extinction.

flashcard.front: How does trophy hunting act as a selective force, and what evolutionary consequence has been documented in bighorn sheep?
flashcard.back: Trophy hunting selectively removes individuals with the largest horns/tusks before or during peak reproduction. This imposes directional selection AGAINST large ornaments. In Alberta bighorn sheep, 30 years of selective hunting of large-horned rams caused average horn size to decline ~20%. This is evolution in action — human harvesting changes allele frequencies in wild populations by non-randomly removing certain phenotypes.

quiz.question: A small, isolated population of endangered birds shows high rates of genetic diseases and low reproductive success. The most likely explanation is:
quiz.correct: Inbreeding depression — small population size increases homozygosity, exposing deleterious recessive alleles
distractors: Outbreeding depression — too much gene flow from distant populations
Stabilizing selection — selection removing extreme phenotypes
Frequency-dependent selection — rare genotypes have lower fitness

visual.description: Threats to biodiversity and conservation genetics solutions

tooltips.plain: Reduced fitness from mating between close relatives — increases homozygosity and exposes harmful recessive alleles.
Introducing individuals from another population to increase genetic diversity and reduce inbreeding depression.

visual.mnemonic: HIPCO threatens, GENETICS saves. Small populations need gene flow like drought-stressed plants need rain.
visual.trap: Conservation isn't just about saving individuals — it's about preserving evolutionary potential (genetic diversity) so populations can adapt to future challenges.

v2.definition: Humans drive contemporary evolution through hunting, habitat fragmentation, and climate change, often selecting against the very traits we value.

mnemonic.explanation: Trophy hunters select against big-horned rams; within decades populations evolve smaller horns, the opposite of natural selection.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Humans don't drive evolution.' Humans are now Earth's strongest selective agent: hunting, fishing, and antibiotics all select within decades.

actions.quizPrompt: Give an example of human-driven contemporary evolution.
actions.flashcardBack: Trophy hunting selects against big horns in bighorn sheep within decades.

nodes.title: Human Evolution
nodes.subtitle: Hominin phylogeny and what makes us a unique kind of ape (Ch17)

popup.heading: Human Evolution (Chapter 17)

sections.label: Definition
sections.body: Hominins = humans and our bipedal ancestors, separate from chimps ~6-7 Ma. Modern Homo sapiens evolved ~300,000 years ago in Africa. We are one of ~8 million animal species, classified within Hominidae (great apes).

sections.label: Key Derived Traits
sections.body: Bipedalism (freeing hands, earlier than large brains), large neocortex (~3x larger than body size predicts), language and symbolic thought, prolonged childhood (extended learning period), reduced sexual dimorphism vs. earlier hominins.

sections.label: Hominin Timeline
sections.body: Ardipithecus ~4.4 Ma (facultative biped). Australopithecus ~4-2 Ma (obligate biped, small brain ~450cc). Homo habilis ~2.4 Ma (stone tools, brain ~600cc). Homo erectus ~1.9 Ma (fire, migrated out of Africa). H. sapiens ~300 ka (Africa); ~60-70 ka out of Africa.

quotes: "We are a very peculiar kind of ape." — Zimmer & Emlen, Ch17
examples: Lucy (Australopithecus afarensis): 3.2 Ma, walked upright but had chimp-sized brain — proves bipedalism preceded brain expansion.
Neanderthal-Homo sapiens hybridization: 1-4% of non-African human genomes are Neanderthal.
warnings: Human evolution is NOT a linear chain (caveman → modern human). It's a branching bush with many coexisting species.
Bipedalism evolved ~2-3 million years BEFORE our large brains — do not assume they co-evolved simultaneously.
popup.mnemonic: ABLE: Ardipithecus → Bipedalism first. Lucy → Evidence. Brain enlargement comes Later. Ever-expanding: we interbred with Neanderthals.

flashcard.front: What does the fossil record reveal about the sequence of bipedalism and brain expansion in human evolution, and why does this sequence matter?
flashcard.back: Bipedalism evolved first (~4.4 Ma in Ardipithecus, obligate in Australopithecus ~3.5 Ma) with chimp-sized brains (~400-500cc). Brain expansion to human size began ~2 Ma with Homo habilis and continued in H. erectus and H. sapiens. This matters because it disproves the 'big brain caused bipedalism' hypothesis — upright walking freed the hands for tool use, which may have then driven selection for larger brains.

quiz.question: A fossil hominin shows a pelvis adapted for bipedal walking but a brain volume of only 430cc (similar to a chimpanzee). When did this individual most likely live?
quiz.correct: ~3-4 million years ago, consistent with Australopithecus
distractors: ~2 million years ago — this matches Homo habilis brain size
~100,000 years ago — early modern humans had brains >1200cc
~1.5 million years ago — Homo erectus had ~900cc brains

tooltips.plain: Humans and our bipedal relatives after the split from chimpanzees — NOT all great apes.
Walking upright on two legs — the first major derived trait distinguishing hominins.

visual.mnemonic: ABLE: Apes → Bipedal → Larger brains → Everywhere (out of Africa). The body walked before the mind raced.
visual.trap: Human evolution is a BUSH not a ladder. Many hominin species coexisted — H. sapiens and Neanderthals overlapped for thousands of years.

v2.definition: Humans evolved in Africa about 300,000 years ago, with bipedalism, brain expansion, and culture as our defining derived traits.

mnemonic.explanation: Bipedalism evolved at least four million years ago; large brains came two million years later in Homo.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Humans evolved from chimps.' Humans and chimps SHARE a common ancestor; neither descended from the other.

actions.quizPrompt: In what order did bipedalism, large brains, and culture evolve?
actions.flashcardBack: About 300,000 years ago in Africa.

nodes.title: Evolutionary Medicine
nodes.subtitle: Why evolution explains disease vulnerability and drug resistance (Ch18)

popup.heading: Evolutionary Medicine (Chapter 18)

sections.label: Definition
sections.body: The application of evolutionary principles to understand why we are vulnerable to disease. Asks: why didn't selection eliminate this disease susceptibility? Often reveals trade-offs, mismatch, and coevolution.

sections.label: Core Frameworks
sections.body: 1) Trade-offs: sickle cell anemia protects against malaria — one allele copy is beneficial, two are harmful. 2) Mismatch: evolved for ancestral environments; modern diet causes type 2 diabetes, obesity. 3) Arms race: pathogens and hosts coevolve rapidly (Red Queen). 4) Defenses (fever, nausea): may be adaptive, not just symptoms.

sections.label: Antibiotic Resistance
sections.body: Resistance evolves by natural selection: random mutations arise, antibiotics kill susceptible bacteria, resistant survivors reproduce. MRSA, XDR-TB are direct results. Evolutionary solution: cycling antibiotics, combination therapy, phage therapy.

quotes: "Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution." — Dobzhansky, 1973 (adapted for medicine by Nesse & Williams as: 'Nothing in medicine makes sense except in the light of evolution')
examples: Sickle cell trait: heterozygotes have 10x lower malaria mortality in sub-Saharan Africa — balanced polymorphism maintained by selection.
Morning sickness peaks during organogenesis (weeks 6-18) when embryo is most vulnerable to teratogens — Profet's hypothesis: nausea protects embryo.
warnings: Not all disease symptoms are adaptive — fever above 41°C is dangerous, not beneficial. Distinguish 'designed' defense from harmful pathology.
Evolutionary medicine explains WHY we're vulnerable, not how to cure — but it generates better hypotheses.
popup.mnemonic: TAMED: Trade-offs, Arms race, Mismatch, Evolved defenses, Drug resistance. Evolution TAMED our expectations of perfect health.

flashcard.front: What is the 'mismatch hypothesis' in evolutionary medicine, and give two examples of modern diseases it explains?
flashcard.back: Mismatch hypothesis: our bodies evolved adaptations for ancestral (Pleistocene) environments but now face novel modern environments — the mismatch causes disease. Examples: 1) Type 2 diabetes/obesity: we evolved to store fat during feast-famine cycles; constant food availability causes chronic overconsumption. 2) Myopia: eyes evolved outdoors in bright light; indoor childhoods during eye development disrupt focal length calibration.

quiz.question: In malaria-endemic regions, the sickle cell allele is maintained at ~10-20% frequency despite causing severe anemia in homozygotes. This is best explained as:
quiz.correct: Balancing selection — heterozygote advantage maintains both alleles in the population
distractors: Directional selection — the sickle allele is increasing toward fixation
Genetic drift — random frequency fluctuations in small populations
Mutation-selection balance — new mutations constantly replenish the allele

visual.description: Evolutionary vs. proximate explanations for disease

tooltips.plain: When selection maintains two or more alleles because heterozygotes are fitter than either homozygote.
Coevolutionary arms race between hosts and parasites — both must constantly evolve just to stay in place.

visual.mnemonic: TAMED: Trade-offs, Arms race, Mismatch, Evolved defenses, Drug resistance. Medicine without evolution is like engineering without physics.
visual.trap: Students assume symptoms = malfunction. Many symptoms (fever, diarrhea, nausea) may be evolved defenses — suppressing them can worsen outcomes.

v2.definition: Evolutionary medicine explains why we get sick: pathogen evolution, mismatch with ancestral environments, and trade-offs in our biology.

mnemonic.explanation: Disease often reflects evolutionary mismatch, pathogen evolution, or unavoidable trade-offs baked into our biology.

v2.examTrap: Wrong: 'Sickle cell is just a defective allele.' Sickle cell HETEROZYGOTES resist malaria, a textbook heterozygote-advantage trade-off.

actions.quizPrompt: How does antibiotic resistance illustrate evolution in real time?
actions.flashcardFront: Why does sickle cell allele persist?
actions.flashcardBack: Heterozygote advantage: carriers resist malaria infection.
